scenario_meta.notes: Struggles to articulate reasons, no strong achievements, basic language.

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
Dear Admission Committee,

I want to study at inVision U because I do not have a lot of opportunities in my city. Growing up in Zhezkazgan, it is not easy to find good resources. My family is not very rich, and there is nothing special here for young people. School is okay, but it is not interesting or motivating for me. I like learning, but sometimes it is hard because there are too many students and the teachers cannot really help each person. I try my best, but my grades are not perfect.

Sometimes, I feel like I am just repeating things from books instead of really understanding them. The school library has old books, and we do not have clubs or extra classes, so after school I usually go home. Some classmates just want to leave the city as soon as possible, and others do not care much about learning. But I always wanted to see something more, maybe in a bigger city or in another country. I heard about opportunities online, but sometimes I do not know if they are real, or if I can even try. My internet is not very fast, and my English is not so good, but I try to read articles and watch videos to learn more.

I want something different for my life. I heard about inVision U from a teacher who told me this is for people who want to try more in life. I think if I go to this university, I can learn many new things and find friends who have ideas. I do not know exactly what I want to do, but maybe projects and teamwork will be good for me. I am not the best leader, but I think if I learn more I can improve. Sometimes I do not understand big words or new ideas quickly, but I try to keep learning. My English is not perfect, but I keep practicing. For example, I watch movies in English, and sometimes I pause to look up the words I do not know. It is slow, but I feel happy when I understand more.

At home, my father lost his job two years ago and it was a very hard time for our family. I helped my mother with the housework and taking care of my younger brother. That made me think about being responsible. I liked when we solved things together. We did not have much money, so I learned to fix small things in the house and cook simple meals. At first I made mistakes, like burning the soup or forgetting to buy bread, but after a while I got better and my mother said she was proud. But most of all, I really want to have a good education to help my family in the future.

I did not do big projects before, but I would like to try. I once helped organize a birthday for my brother, and everyone was happy, so maybe I can do bigger things later. I also helped my classmates before exams by making small notes and sharing them, because some people do not have internet at home. I want to learn how to work in a group and to make real projects that help people. I feel there is not enough chance here, so I hope you will let me try at your university.

Thank you for reading my letter. I am ready to try hard even if it is difficult at the beginning. I hope to learn, grow, and maybe help others like me one day.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: The most difficult time in my life was when my father lost his job during the pandemic. For many months we did not have enough money. I stayed at home and helped my mother with cleaning and cooking. I also helped my younger brother with homework because my mother had to find extra work. There was a lot of stress because we did not know what would happen. I coped by trying to stay calm and focusing on helping my family. After this, I became more responsible and I think I started taking family more seriously than before.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: I wake up early every day, around 6:45, and help prepare breakfast for my family. Then I check if my younger brother needs help with his school things, like packing his bag or finding his uniform. After my own school ends, I try to finish my homework before helping at home with chores, such as washing dishes or sweeping. Sometimes, if I am not too tired, I read for 30 minutes before sleeping, usually something interesting, not just textbooks. This makes me more organized, and I don’t forget things as often as before. I chose to make this routine because my mother needed help, and I saw that when we all do our part, things are better for everyone and there is less stress.

motivation_questions.question: Was there a time when you worked toward a goal for a long time but never reached it? What did you take away from that experience?
motivation_questions.answer: I wanted to get a prize in a school competition about math, but I did not win. I studied for a long time, spending many evenings working through practice problems and reading extra chapters, but still could not get to the last round. At first I felt upset and even a bit embarrassed when I saw others moving on, but I learned that sometimes even if you try, things don’t work, and you can still be proud of yourself for just trying. Now I do not give up so quickly, and I know it is important to try even if the result is not perfect. I also realized that the effort I put in helped me understand math better, and later I was able to solve problems that I could not before.

motivation_questions.question: Describe a project, idea, or solution you came up with entirely on your own initiative - not as an assignment. Where did the idea come from, and what was the outcome?
motivation_questions.answer: One time I decided to organize all the books in our home because everything was messy and people kept losing their things. Nobody told me to do this, but I noticed it was hard to find schoolbooks, especially when we were in a hurry before school. I made a small bookshelf from old boxes I found in our storage room and sorted the books by who they belong to and also by subject, like math and literature. After that, it was easier for everyone to find what they needed. My mom said it saved her time in the mornings. It made me feel happy to help in a small way, especially because my brother could finally find his books quickly and was less stressed before his classes.

motivation_questions.question: How do you usually make important decisions - do you rely on intuition, data, or advice from others? Walk us through a specific real example from your life.
motivation_questions.answer: I talk with my mother most of the time when I have an important decision. When I thought about applying to universities outside my city, at first I was scared. I had never been far from home before, and I worried about not seeing my family or friends so often. But my mother said to try, so I listened to her and also read online about universities. I looked at student reviews and watched some YouTube videos to see what life is like there. In the end, I applied because both my mother and I agreed that it could be a good chance. I think I rely on advice first, but I also check information to feel more sure.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever faced a situation where you had to choose between what was efficient and what was right? What did you do, and why?
motivation_questions.answer: In group assignments at school, sometimes people want to just finish fast without doing everything well. One time, during a math project, I told my group that we should check our answers more carefully, even if it would take longer. Some people were not happy, because they wanted to go home early or just get it done. But I pushed for us to review each step and double-check the calculations. It felt uncomfortable because I worried they might think I was being difficult. Still, we did it, and in the end, we got a better grade than we expected. I chose what was right because I thought it was important to be honest, not just with the teacher but with ourselves about our work. This experience showed me that doing the right thing can be harder, but it’s worth it.

motivation_questions.question: Is there a problem in your city, country, or the world that genuinely troubles you? Why that one specifically - and what do you think is its root cause?
motivation_questions.answer: In my city, there are not many good jobs. Many people have to leave for bigger cities, and families get separated. This makes it hard, and I think the main reason is that factories and companies do not open here. Maybe there are not enough investments. I wish there were more chances for young people in Zhezkazgan. For example, my cousin had to move to Almaty because he couldn’t find work after university. A lot of my friends also talk about leaving after graduation. The city feels emptier every year. I think it’s because businesses don’t want to invest in small cities, maybe because of distance or lack of resources. It’s a cycle that keeps repeating, and it worries me about the future.

motivation_questions.question: How has your worldview shifted over the last 2-3 years? What drove that change - a book, a person, an event, an experience?
motivation_questions.answer: After my father lost his job, I understood that things can change anytime and that nothing is really permanent. Before that, I always thought life is always the same, like each day would be predictable and nothing big would happen. Seeing my father struggle with finding new work made me realize how quickly situations can shift. Then, when my mother started going to work, I saw how hard it can be for her to come home tired every evening, but still prepare food and take care of us. This made me respect working people more, not just people with important jobs, but also people doing everyday work. It also made me start thinking about different jobs that I never thought about before, like being a cashier or cleaning, and how all work deserves respect.

motivation_questions.question: What do you think Kazakhstan will look like in 20 years - and what role do you believe your generation will play in shaping it?
motivation_questions.answer: I hope Kazakhstan will have more factories and places to work, especially in small cities. Maybe more young people will stay in their hometowns instead of leaving. If there are more jobs and opportunities, people might not feel like they have to move to Almaty or abroad. I think my generation can study and bring new ideas to our cities. For example, some of my classmates are interested in technology or small business, and maybe we can start something new together in the future. I am not sure exactly what I will do, but I want to be part of improving my city if I can. Even small changes, like volunteering or starting a club, could help my community grow.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: There was a time when other students were making fun of a boy for being not very smart. I stood up and told them to stop. I did not like it, and I knew it was wrong. It was not popular, and some of them laughed at me, but I knew I should not just watch and do nothing. Even though I felt nervous and my voice was a bit shaky, I still said something, because I remembered how it felt when someone made fun of me before. I thought maybe if I didn’t say anything, it would just keep happening, and that seemed unfair. Afterward, a few people ignored me for a while, but I still think it was better than staying silent.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: I helped my neighbor, who is an old lady, with carrying her groceries and cleaning her small garden, because I saw she could not do it herself. She was very happy every time I came. Sometimes she would give me tea or tell me stories about her family. I liked feeling useful, and it made me realize small things can make people’s lives a bit better. Before, I didn’t think such simple help could matter, but when I saw her smile or when she told me her back hurt less because I helped with the heavier things, I understood it was important. It also made me feel more connected to my neighborhood and that I can do something, even if it’s not big.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer: My first value is family, because I always try to help my family when they need. For example, when my younger brother was sick, I stayed home from an outing with friends to take care of him and help my mom. Second is honesty. At school, I admitted once when I made a mistake on my homework, even though I was worried the teacher would be disappointed. I think telling the truth, even when it’s uncomfortable, is important. Third is kindness. I help my neighbor sometimes with cleaning her garden, not because I must but because it is the right thing. She is elderly and lives alone, and I feel good knowing I can make her day a bit easier.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: I helped organize my brother’s birthday at home with a small party for his friends. I made games and found prizes, like some chocolate bars and little toys. It was nice to see everyone having fun, but we did not have enough food, so some kids were a little hungry, and a few even asked if there were more snacks. I didn’t expect everyone to eat so much, and I also forgot to check if anyone had allergies or special food needs. Next time, I would plan better and make sure there is enough for everyone, maybe by asking parents ahead of time what their kids like or bringing some extra snacks just in case. I realized organizing things means thinking about details I didn’t notice before.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: In summer, I helped my uncle fix his car, and he paid me some money. I just watched and listened to him, and handed tools sometimes. I also helped clean his garage. It was not much, but I was proud to earn my first money and used it to buy a school bag for my brother. Before this, I never really worked or earned anything myself, so it felt different to actually get paid for helping. My uncle showed me how to change oil and explained what each tool does. Sometimes I made mistakes, like giving the wrong wrench, but he was patient. Cleaning the garage was hard work because it was hot and dusty, but I did it anyway. Earning that money made me feel more responsible.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: If I had $1,000, I would buy school supplies for kids in my neighborhood who cannot afford them, because I saw many come to school without notebooks or pens. I remember one boy who used to borrow a pen every day, and I could see he felt embarrassed. Sometimes teachers even had to share their own supplies. I think if I could give out basic things like notebooks, pens, pencils, and maybe even some backpacks, it would help them to study better, and maybe feel more equal with others. I would try to ask teachers which families need help most, so I don’t miss anyone. I know it would not fix everything, but it could make small differences that matter.

text_inputs.interview_text:
I want to study at inVision U because I do not have many chances in my city. School here is not very good, and I want to try something new. Sometimes our teachers are absent, and we don’t have many clubs or extra activities. I heard that your university has new ways of learning that are interesting. I saw a video about students working together on projects, and I imagined myself there, trying to learn with others. I am not the best student, but I am trying to learn more and improve myself. For example, I started reading some books in English, even though it takes me a long time. I am not used to working in teams but want to learn because in the future I think it will be important. When we had a group project in class, I was nervous, but I tried to speak up and share my ideas, even if my language was not perfect.

I think I can learn more about how to make projects and maybe help my city later. My city needs more people who try new things and bring back good ideas. I do not have a lot of experience with different activities or clubs, but if given a chance I will not give up even if things are hard for me. Sometimes I feel behind others, but I keep going and do not stop.

My English is not very good, but I try to read and watch videos to get better. I hope to meet people from different cities and learn new things. If I am accepted, I will work hard and try to not waste the opportunity. I am not sure yet about my future job, but I hope I can make my family proud and maybe help other young people in my city to follow a similar path. Thank you for considering me.

scenario_meta.notes: Very strong grades and academic discipline, but vague aspirations, little reflective motivation.

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
To the Admissions Committee,

I am applying to inVision U due to its strong academic reputation and interdisciplinary approach, which I believe will complement my existing foundation in mathematics and sciences. I have maintained high grades at Nazarbayev Intellectual School in Astana and always prioritized my studies, focusing especially on mathematics and physics. I am motivated by a desire to learn at the highest level and develop analytical thinking.

My main academic interests are mathematics and physics because I have always been curious about how the world works on both a theoretical and practical level. For example, when I first encountered calculus, I was fascinated by how it could describe motion and change, and I spent extra time outside class working through problems that were not assigned, simply out of curiosity. This habit has helped me build a strong foundation in abstract thinking and problem-solving. I also enjoy physics because it connects math to real-world phenomena—I remember being especially interested in a project on mechanics, where we analyzed the motion of a pendulum and compared our theoretical predictions with experimental results. That was one of my first experiences seeing how theory and practice interact, even if it was on a small classroom scale.

While my academic performance has been consistent, I feel I have not been able to explore many extracurricular or community-based projects because my main focus has always been on Olympiad training and exam preparation. I have participated in math Olympiads, such as the Republican Olympiad, and have several awards at the regional and city levels. Preparing for these competitions demanded a lot of time and discipline; I often spent weekends working through problem sets or discussing solutions with a small group of classmates who also competed. These experiences have developed my logical thinking, but I recognize that my teamwork and practical implementation skills need more development. Most of my work so far has been individual, and I realize that in university and beyond, group work is important. Sometimes, in group assignments at school, I found it challenging to communicate my ideas or to blend different viewpoints into a shared solution. I want to improve in this area.

I look forward to the opportunity to study in an environment that encourages different perspectives and hands-on projects. Although I do not have a specific project or social initiative to mention, I have always done my best in every academic setting. My teachers say that I am hard-working and responsible. For example, my math teacher once told me that I was reliable because I never gave up on difficult problems, even if it took several attempts. I would like to see how my skills adapt to group work, problem-solving, and potentially leadership roles, as these are areas I have less exposure to. I am also interested in learning how to apply theoretical knowledge to real-world problems, which is something I have not had many chances to do.

Studying at inVision U will help me grow outside my comfort zone and allow me to interact with peers who also care deeply about advancing their knowledge. I am interested in discovering how different subjects connect—sometimes in unexpected ways—and learning how to build something meaningful through interdisciplinary collaboration. For example, I am curious about how mathematics can be used in computer science or economics, and how ideas from physics might influence engineering or environmental studies. It is also important for me to contribute to Kazakhstan’s future scientific and educational progress, even if I am not yet sure in which way exactly. I hope that by learning from a diverse group of students and professors, I will find new directions for my interests and ways to make a positive impact. I am grateful for the chance to apply and for your consideration.

Sincerely,  
Ermek

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: The most difficult period was when I failed to advance to the final round of the National Mathematics Olympiad in my 10th grade. I had trained for several months, often sacrificing free time and sleep. When I realized I had not made the national team, I felt disappointed and even doubted my abilities. I coped by reviewing my mistakes and talking to my teacher, who encouraged me to focus on the progress I had made. This experience made me more resilient and showed me that setbacks do not define my capabilities.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: I have a very structured schedule. I dedicate the early morning to revising theoretical material, then practice problem sets in the afternoons, and in the evenings I re-read mistakes and summarize new concepts. I also use spaced repetition for memorization. These routines are designed to maximize my academic performance, as they help me retain information and practice efficiently. For example, when preparing for math Olympiads, I noticed that reviewing my errors at night helped me avoid repeating them. I chose this system because I realized that regular, distributed practice gave me the best results during Olympiad preparation. It’s not always easy to keep up with this schedule, but over time it has helped me stay organized and less anxious before exams.

motivation_questions.question: Was there a time when you worked toward a goal for a long time but never reached it? What did you take away from that experience?
motivation_questions.answer: As mentioned before, I spent almost a year preparing for the national math team selection. Despite my effort, I did not make the team. I learned that effort does not always guarantee success, but it almost always leads to improvement. The process itself gave me more confidence in problem-solving and helped me identify my weak areas. For example, I realized I struggled with combinatorics, so I focused more on that topic even after the selection ended. Although not making the team was disappointing, I see now that I grew a lot during that time. I also learned to manage frustration and keep working even when results are uncertain. Now, when I approach ambitious goals, I do it with this awareness and more realistic expectations.

motivation_questions.question: Describe a project, idea, or solution you came up with entirely on your own initiative - not as an assignment. Where did the idea come from, and what was the outcome?
motivation_questions.answer: Last year, I started tutoring my younger cousin to help her with geometry. I came up with my own way of explaining proofs using colored diagrams, not just text, because I noticed she was a visual learner. For example, I would draw triangles and use different colors to highlight each angle or side, which made it easier for her to follow my explanations. It was not a formal project, but it helped her grades improve by the end of the semester. Sometimes I even tried making up little stories to go along with the diagrams, like naming the shapes or giving them personalities, to keep things interesting for her. Seeing how different approaches work for different people made me appreciate adaptation in teaching, even outside a classroom. It also made me realize that even small changes can make a big difference for someone’s understanding.

motivation_questions.question: How do you usually make important decisions - do you rely on intuition, data, or advice from others? Walk us through a specific real example from your life.
motivation_questions.answer: I tend to rely on a mix of data and advice. For example, when choosing whether to focus on math or physics Olympiads this year, I looked at my past performance, my teachers' feedback, and asked classmates who had participated previously. I made a table of my results and finally chose to focus on mathematics, as data showed it was my stronger subject. It wasn’t an easy decision because I enjoy both subjects, and some friends thought I should try physics for a new challenge. But I noticed that I consistently scored higher in math, and my math teacher also encouraged me to pursue it. In the end, I felt more confident preparing for math Olympiads, though sometimes I still wonder if I should have taken the risk with physics instead.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever faced a situation where you had to choose between what was efficient and what was right? What did you do, and why?
motivation_questions.answer: Once, during a group lab at school, some classmates wanted to skip certain steps to save time. Instead, I insisted that we follow the protocol, even though it took longer. I felt that accuracy and integrity were more important, especially in a scientific context. It was tempting to go along with the group and finish early, but I kept thinking about how skipping steps might lead to mistakes or unreliable results. The others weren’t very happy at first, because we had to stay after class to finish. But in the end, the result was that we got better results in our experiment, and the teacher praised our methodology. That experience made me realize that doing what is right isn’t always easy, but it can lead to better outcomes, even if it takes more effort or time.

motivation_questions.question: Is there a problem in your city, country, or the world that genuinely troubles you? Why that one specifically - and what do you think is its root cause?
motivation_questions.answer: One problem in Kazakhstan that concerns me is the brain drain of talented students to other countries. I think this happens because opportunities and research facilities here are limited. For example, my older cousin left for Germany because he wanted to work in a more advanced robotics lab, and he said it wasn’t possible to find that here. Many young people feel they cannot realize their potential without going abroad, and it’s almost like a tradition now to dream about leaving. This makes me think about how local academic and scientific environments could be strengthened, so students would have more reasons to stay. It also worries me because losing young talent can slow down progress in Kazakhstan and make it harder for the country to develop new ideas.

motivation_questions.question: How has your worldview shifted over the last 2-3 years? What drove that change - a book, a person, an event, an experience?
motivation_questions.answer: My worldview has become more open as I met classmates from different regions at NIS. Engaging with diverse perspectives made me realize there are multiple ways to approach problems, not only the logical-mathematical one. For example, some classmates would suggest creative or artistic solutions, which was unexpected for me. This understanding was deepened through reading 'Thinking, Fast and Slow' by Daniel Kahneman, making me think more about intuition. Before, I mainly trusted slow, analytical thinking, but now I pay more attention to my first impressions and feelings about situations. Sometimes, I try to notice when I am making quick judgments, or when I am overthinking. I think these experiences have made me more flexible in my views, even if I still prefer logic.

motivation_questions.question: What do you think Kazakhstan will look like in 20 years - and what role do you believe your generation will play in shaping it?
motivation_questions.answer: I think Kazakhstan will be more technologically advanced, hopefully with more local innovation. My generation will likely be responsible for driving scientific and technological progress. Personally, I hope to contribute by engaging in research or possibly teaching, though I am not sure what area exactly. Maybe in twenty years, we will have more startups and home-grown companies, which could make us less reliant on imported technology. I imagine there will be more focus on STEM education in schools, and that young people like me will be encouraged to try new ideas or even fail sometimes, which isn’t always the case right now. To be honest, I don’t know how big my personal impact will be, but I want to be the kind of person who helps Kazakhstan move forward, even in small ways.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: During a class debate, I argued in favor of stricter academic honesty policies even though most of my classmates thought they were too harsh. I truly believed that integrity in learning is essential, even at the cost of popularity. Some of my friends looked disappointed, and later a few of them asked why I would want to make things harder for everyone. It made me feel a bit isolated, but I stood by my belief. I explained that cheating hurts not only the person doing it, but also everyone else, because it makes grades less meaningful. I remember thinking that if I just agreed with them, maybe things would have been easier socially, but I couldn’t do it. In the end, it was uncomfortable, but I learned that sticking to my values matters more than fitting in.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: I helped my classmate who was struggling with algebra for several weeks after school. I volunteered because I noticed she was falling behind, and I remembered how lost I felt with math a year before. We sat together in the library, going through homework problems step by step. Sometimes it took an hour just to explain one concept, like quadratic equations, but I didn’t mind. I realized that it’s not just about knowing the material—it's about finding ways to explain it so it makes sense to someone else. This experience taught me patience and improved my communication skills, and it felt rewarding to see her finally start enjoying math instead of fearing it. I was happy when she got a better grade, but even happier when she smiled and said math wasn't scary anymore.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer: My core values are perseverance, honesty, and responsibility. For perseverance: I kept working at Olympiad problems even when I failed, sometimes spending hours on a single question and not giving up even after not making it to the next round. For honesty: I once corrected a teacher when I received too many points on a test, even though it meant a lower grade for me, because I knew it was the right thing to do. For responsibility: I always meet my deadlines for assignments and never submit late work, even if it means staying up late or waking up earlier. I think these values are important not just in school but also in life outside class, and I try to keep them in mind.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: I have not organized any large events, but I coordinated small study sessions for classmates before exams. What worked was preparing sample problems and solutions in advance, which helped us focus on the most difficult topics and made the sessions efficient. Sometimes, though, it felt like I was just explaining everything, and other students were mostly listening instead of joining in the discussion. If I could do it again, I would try to involve more students in leading discussions or maybe rotate who presents solutions. That way, it could be less of a lecture and more interactive—maybe even more fun. Also, I think having snacks or a short break would help people stay focused for longer sessions.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: Last summer, I tutored two secondary-school students in math and science. I developed custom worksheets and met them via Zoom. It was my first time trying to earn money by teaching, so I had to figure out what topics they struggled with and then make exercises for those areas. Sometimes it was hard to keep them motivated, especially online, but I tried different approaches, like adding short quizzes or letting them choose which problem to solve first. The result was both students improved their grades, and I earned enough to buy a new calculator and some books for myself. Teaching also made me realize how challenging it is to explain concepts in a simple way, and I think I learned a lot too.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: I would purchase math and science books and organize a free summer camp for local students interested in Olympiads, especially those from underrepresented backgrounds. This could help them access resources and create a community of learners. Many students in my area want to participate in Olympiads but do not have enough preparation materials or guidance, so I think this camp could give them a place to study together and share ideas. I would try to invite a few university students or teachers to give talks or help with problem-solving sessions. If possible, I would use some of the money for snacks, so it feels friendly and welcoming. I hope this could also encourage more students, especially girls and students from rural areas, to feel confident and motivated about science and math, and maybe even continue supporting each other after the camp ends.

text_inputs.interview_text:
My reason for applying to inVision U is to experience a different kind of academic environment which includes interdisciplinary studies and practical real-world project experiences. My background is in mathematics, with a focus on analytical problem solving and Olympiad-level thinking, but I recognize that I often stay in my comfort zone and have not attempted many projects or collaborations. What I am hoping to gain is not only deeper subject knowledge but also growth in my ability to work with others, try new things, and take on leadership or creative roles.

For example, in high school, most of my time was spent preparing for competitions and working independently. I realize now that I rarely participated in group projects or activities that required collective effort, except maybe for organizing small study groups before exams. Looking back, I see how this limited my perspective and sometimes made me hesitant to share ideas or ask for help. I believe that the structure at inVision U, with its emphasis on teamwork and interdisciplinary challenges, will encourage me to step beyond these habits and learn to communicate and collaborate better.

While I am confident in my academic discipline and my results show consistent work, my aspirations for the future are still developing. I think this program could help me discover other interests and connections between fields which I may not have considered, such as how mathematics can support innovations in technology or social sciences. I am open to the idea that my career path might change as I explore these options.

Overall, I am motivated by curiosity and a desire to contribute to Kazakhstan’s development, especially in science and education, though I do not have concrete plans yet. I hope that by joining inVision U I can find both inspiration and a clearer direction for my goals. Thank you.

scenario_meta.notes: High intrinsic motivation and real-world problem-solving potential, but awkward and scattered expression, some weak or vague parts.

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
To whom it may concern,

My main reason for applying to inVision U is that I want to work on things that really matter and affect people and the environment around me. I have lived in Taraz my whole life, and here many people talk about how the river is less clean than when our parents were young. I read about water quality and pollution online a lot, and I started to do very small experiments to check how dirty the water really is in the canal near our house. I collected samples and tried to use testing strips I got from a pharmacy, once even borrowing a microscope from my biology teacher, although mostly it was not very clear.

But my school does not have strong resources or encouragement for things outside standard lessons, so after a while I started to feel stuck and was not sure who to ask for help. You can learn theory all day but if you want to do practical things or ask why things are not improving, sometimes teachers just say it is not our problem. But to me this is a problem, both for people and for animals. I have tried to let neighbors know about garbage issues in our area, and together with two friends, we began weekend cleanups along our street, though it is not organized as anything official—mostly just us and sometimes little kids who want to help. It matters to me because my younger sister has allergies, and I wonder if pollution makes it worse.

What attracts me to inVision U is the way it supports students who want to make real projects, and not just follow instructions. I’d like to try working on more serious research and learn methods for collecting and organizing information—not just guessing. In my free time I read a lot about biology, and online I found a project from Uzbekistan where young people built small home filters from sand and plastic bottles. I tried something similar—ours was not very effective, but it made me think perhaps we can improve it with more knowledge.

I do not know if I am a good leader because I mostly just try things and see who joins me, but I really like thinking of ways to solve problems that are ignored. I hope at your university, I can meet mentors and other students who also care about changing things that look permanent. Thank you for considering my application. I want to make things better, and I want to understand more about science and teamwork.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: Last year my family had to move houses because our apartment started to have mold and my sister got sick a lot. We could not figure out the cause, and doctors said maybe it was from the dampness and outside dust. I felt scared for her and for myself. I coped by staying with cousins for a few weeks, and I read a lot about air quality and how to clean up mold, even though nobody else in the family listened much. After this period, I realized you can’t just ignore problems because they feel too big. It made me braver and more stubborn about trying to solve things, even when adults say it’s not possible.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: I started keeping a notebook of questions—things I don’t understand about biology or local environmental issues. Every day I try to answer at least one, either by looking up articles (sometimes in Russian, sometimes in English) or asking my teachers. It helps me not forget questions and to keep a personal archive. I picked this because so many times, ideas just get lost otherwise. For example, last month I wrote down a question about river pollution in my city, and after some searching, I found a local study I wouldn’t have come across otherwise. Sometimes, I don’t find the answer right away, but just writing the question makes me notice related things in conversations or class. I think this routine makes me more curious and organized.

motivation_questions.question: Was there a time when you worked toward a goal for a long time but never reached it? What did you take away from that experience?
motivation_questions.answer: Yes, I wanted to set up a compost bin for our neighborhood, so that people didn’t throw food waste in the garbage. I talked to neighbors and put up a paper sign, but only a few people used it. Over time, people threw plastic into it, and it didn’t work. Sometimes I would check the bin and find not just plastic, but even things like glass bottles or wrappers. I realized that not everyone understood how composting works, or maybe they just didn’t care. I tried to explain a few times, but I felt awkward and some people ignored me. I learned that you have to plan more, get more people involved, and it is more difficult to change habits than I thought. But I’m not giving up. I still want to try again, maybe with more preparation.

motivation_questions.question: Describe a project, idea, or solution you came up with entirely on your own initiative - not as an assignment. Where did the idea come from, and what was the outcome?
motivation_questions.answer: I tried to build a small water filter using sand and charcoal in a plastic bottle after reading about it online. I wanted to see if it improved water clarity from the irrigation canal near my home. I collected the materials myself, cutting the bottle and layering sand and crushed charcoal as I had seen in a diagram. When I poured the canal water through, it looked better—less murky and with fewer visible particles. However, I couldn’t test for bacteria or chemicals, so I told people not to drink it, just in case. Even though it wasn’t really safe, it was fun to experiment, and it made some people more interested in water quality with me. My younger cousin even asked if we could try adding more layers or different materials next time.

motivation_questions.question: How do you usually make important decisions - do you rely on intuition, data, or advice from others? Walk us through a specific real example from your life.
motivation_questions.answer: Mostly a mix—I start with intuition, check if there’s real info available, then ask someone older. For example, when thinking if I should join a biology club online, I asked my older cousin if it was safe, checked reviews, and then tried it out. I also sometimes read comments in forums to see if people had good or bad experiences, because sometimes official reviews are not enough. If it feels wrong, I usually back out quickly, but I’m learning to collect more data before acting. Sometimes I realize later that I could have avoided a problem by researching a bit more or asking more than one person. It’s not always easy, but now I try to slow down and consider more sources before making a decision.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever faced a situation where you had to choose between what was efficient and what was right? What did you do, and why?
motivation_questions.answer: Once, during a group cleanup in our neighborhood, a neighbor suggested that we just sweep the trash under the bushes so we could finish faster and go home. I told everyone we should collect all the trash and throw it away properly, even if it took more time and effort. Not everyone agreed with me; some people just wanted to get it over with. But I felt it was right to do the harder thing, because I have seen what happens when people ignore trash—animals can get hurt, and children play in those areas. Once, I found a cat tangled in plastic by the bushes. That memory made me push for doing the proper cleanup, even if it was less efficient.

motivation_questions.question: Is there a problem in your city, country, or the world that genuinely troubles you? Why that one specifically - and what do you think is its root cause?
motivation_questions.answer: Water pollution in and around Taraz troubles me. Some places smell bad, and people litter a lot. I think it’s because there’s not enough education about environment, plus not enough waste bins or city investment. If people saw more real effects—like sick animals or kids—they might care more. For example, there’s a river that passes near my house, and sometimes it’s full of plastic bottles and bags. I’ve seen stray dogs drinking from it, and sometimes the water looks strange, like there’s oil or chemicals in it. My neighbors complain about the smell, but most people just ignore it. I feel like if schools talked about pollution more, or if there were more trash cans in public parks, things would improve at least a bit.

motivation_questions.question: How has your worldview shifted over the last 2-3 years? What drove that change - a book, a person, an event, an experience?
motivation_questions.answer: It shifted a lot when I realized you can’t wait for adults to solve problems. Seeing my sister suffer from allergies because of mold and dust made me start reading science for myself. I remember looking up why certain things cause allergies and trying to find ways to reduce dust at home. Also, meeting a teacher who actually listened to student ideas, that felt rare and important. She would ask us what we thought before giving her own answer, which was new for me. Now I question more, not just accept. I notice I don’t just take what people say as true anymore, even if they’re older. Sometimes I even annoy my parents by asking too many questions, but I think it’s worth it.

motivation_questions.question: What do you think Kazakhstan will look like in 20 years - and what role do you believe your generation will play in shaping it?
motivation_questions.answer: I hope there will be more green spaces and less pollution. My generation should be the one to fix the environmental neglect, or at least start seeing things as everyone’s responsibility, not just ‘for the government.’ Maybe I could help invent something that helps water or air quality, even small scale. For example, if more people planted trees in their neighborhoods or pushed for recycling programs in schools, it could slowly improve things. In 20 years, I imagine Kazakhstan with cleaner rivers and cities where people are more aware of how their actions affect nature. I think my generation can encourage each other to care more, maybe by sharing information online or creating small community projects. Even if it’s just a few people at first, it can start to change habits for the better.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: During a cleanup, when I insisted that we separate plastics and paper even though others said it was pointless, people laughed at me. I did it because I read that sorting makes recycling easier, even if it’s extra work. It felt weird, but I didn’t want to take the easy way. I remember standing there, picking bottles out of a big messy trash bag, trying to explain that mixing everything together actually makes it harder for recycling centers. Some of my friends just rolled their eyes and told me it wouldn’t matter in Kazakhstan because nobody cares. I still kept separating anyway, even when they went to sit down. Looking back, I know it seemed small and maybe unnecessary, but I just couldn’t ignore what I’d learned. Even if it made me look stubborn, I guess I felt responsible for trying, at least a little, to do what I thought was right.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: I sometimes help the old man across our street with grocery shopping when he can’t go out, and sometimes we talk about what the city used to be like. He tells stories about the river being clean, or how the park had more trees before I was born. Sometimes he asks for onions or milk, and I look for the brands he likes, which took me a while to remember. I think helping neighbors matters, though I don’t do it for a big reason. I just noticed he was alone a lot, and it felt natural to offer help. It made me realize connection can start with small things, like carrying bags or listening to someone’s memories, and maybe that’s more important than I thought.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer: Curiosity—I keep a notebook where I write down science questions that come to my mind, like why the sky is different shades of blue or how plants adapt to harsh winters in Kazakhstan. Sometimes I look for answers in books or online, but other times I just leave the questions there and come back later. Responsibility—organizing cleanups on my street. Last spring, I noticed a lot of trash near our bus stop, so I asked my neighbors if they wanted to help. Only a few people joined, but we filled two big bags together. Openness—trying the online biology club even though most students were older and from other cities. I was nervous to speak up, but I learned a lot and even made one new friend.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: I started a little weekend cleanup with friends. What worked was doing it regularly and inviting everyone, even little kids for fun, because they actually got excited about picking up trash and finding weird things. What didn’t work: adults weren’t interested, and we would run out of supplies like bags and gloves really quickly, especially if more people showed up than expected. Some people also forgot to come after the first few times, so our group got smaller. Next time, I’d try to ask more people for help or maybe talk to a local shop for gloves and bags, or just plan ahead and bring extras. I also think maybe making a poster or group chat could help keep people involved for longer.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: I haven’t earned much money myself. Once, I repaired broken chargers and headphones for neighbors for small fees. I learned basic things from YouTube and bought a cheap soldering kit. Sometimes it worked, sometimes not, but people appreciated it, and I got lunch money. I remember one time, I managed to fix a friend’s headphones after trying for more than an hour, and he was really surprised because he thought they were impossible to fix. There were also times when I couldn’t solve the problem and had to return the item, which was frustrating, but I still learned something new. It was never a big business, but it was nice to be useful and to see that even small skills can help others.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: I'd use the money to make and distribute simple water testing kits and fun lessons about pollution to local schools and homes. If kids see for themselves how water quality changes, maybe they’ll care earlier, and parents might listen. I think this could actually change some behaviors. For example, students could bring samples from their own taps or rivers, run the tests, and compare the results in class. The kits wouldn’t be expensive—just some strips and instructions, maybe with a chart to mark results. With $1,000, I could probably reach a few dozen schools if I keep it simple. I would also try to invite someone who knows about water safety, maybe a university student, to talk to the kids. I hope that even a few families start thinking differently about dumping waste or using filters. It’s not a huge thing, but it could be a start.

text_inputs.interview_text:
I am not great at talking about myself. I mostly just try things when I see a problem, not because I think I can fix everything, but because I don’t like waiting for someone else to act. I know I don’t have all the skills now, but I am always gathering little bits of knowledge from anywhere I can find—articles online, teachers, neighbors. Sometimes it’s messy and not very organized, and it doesn’t always work out. People sometimes ignore me or say I ask too many questions. But I care about the world and about not letting problems stay problems. I joined inVision U’s application mostly because I heard students get to actually work on problems, not just memorize facts. I think I could do more if I could learn better ways to organize projects or talk to people who are also not satisfied with the status quo.

Sometimes I am nervous to speak up, but if the topic is something I care about (like environment or people getting sick from pollution), I can’t stay silent. I want to learn to be more effective in groups, maybe even lead a real project someday, especially if it helps make my city or country healthier. I don’t know if I will be the best student, but I know I will keep trying, even if things are not always easy for me or clear from the start.

scenario_meta.notes: Some elements strong, others contradictory or weak, uneven tone, self-presentation varies, uses Russian.

content_profile.adversity_pattern: some family struggles, not fully acknowledged

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
Уважаемая приемная комиссия,

Меня зовут Айдана, я из Костаная. Мне всегда были интересны разные социальные процессы — почему люди ведут себя определённым образом, как меняются города, что действительно влияет на решения отдельного человека. Я много времени провожу в интернете, в группах про психологию и урбанистику, иногда участвую в обсуждениях, устраивала опрос среди знакомых о том, чего не хватает в нашем районе (больше зелёных зон, общественных лавочек и т.д.). Это не было чем-то масштабным, но мне было важно понять, что людям реально важно.

Когда я гуляю по нашему району, часто замечаю, как многое зависит не только от власти, но и от самих жителей. Например, у нас есть небольшой сквер, который долгое время был заброшен, а потом несколько мам с детьми начали собираться и вместе благоустраивать территорию — приносили саженцы, кто-то привозил старые покрышки, делали клумбы. Я тогда просто наблюдала, но это был мой первый реальный пример, как коллективные усилия могут менять пространство вокруг. После этого я стала больше думать о том, как инициативы "снизу" могут работать. В интернете я читала истории про общественные проекты в других городах, и даже пыталась предложить в чате двора провести субботник, правда, тогда почти никто не откликнулся, но один сосед всё-таки помог вынести мусор. Это было не супер-успешно, но мне было важно попробовать.

Про университет inVision U я узнала от старшей сестры, она учится в Алматы и рассказала, что подход к обучению здесь другой: можно заниматься проектами, а не только зубрить теорию. Меня это зацепило, потому что в школе часто акцент только на экзамены и оценки, а мне бы хотелось учиться через практику, узнавать, как применяются знания на деле. Я сама не всегда активна в оффлайн-инициативах, иногда стесняюсь или просто не знаю, с чего начать, но когда есть вдохновение, могу подключиться и довести дело до конца. Иногда мне сложно долго поддерживать мотивацию, особенно если не вижу быстрой отдачи. Например, когда мы с подругой пытались организовать книжный обмен во дворе, в первые дни никто не приносил книги, и я почти бросила идею, но потом одна женщина всё-таки принесла целую стопку, и стало понятно, что иногда просто нужно подождать.

Я хотела бы глубже узнать, как можно менять свою среду через коллективные проекты, пробовать себя в командной работе (у меня не всегда получается легко выстраивать отношения в группе), научиться применять знания из разных областей — психологии, экономики, городской среды. Думаю, inVision U — это уникальный шанс понять, как идеи становятся реальными изменениями, познакомиться с другими ребятами из разных регионов и, возможно, реализовать полезный для людей проект. Я бы хотела, чтобы в Костанае стало больше пространства для свободного общения и творческих инициатив от молодёжи. Например, небольшие фестивали или мастерские, где подростки могли бы делиться своими увлечениями.

Не могу сказать, что уже сейчас знаю, кем хочу быть через 10 лет. Иногда кажется, что я не совсем на своём месте, но я точно ищу ответы и хочу разобраться в том, как устроено общество и как я могу на него влиять. Мне кажется важным не бояться делать что-то новое, даже если не всё получается с первого раза. Я надеюсь, что учеба в inVision U поможет мне лучше понять себя и окружающий мир, а ещё — найти единомышленников. Спасибо за внимание.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: Сложно выделить один период, но, наверное, когда мои родители временно разъехались, мне было непривычно и тревожно. Я старалась не показывать это друзьям, хотя иногда они замечали, что я стала более замкнутой. Чаще всего я уходила в учебу, делала домашние задания дольше обычного, чтобы отвлечься, и много общалась онлайн — мне казалось, что так проще не думать о происходящем дома. Иногда я просто слушала музыку и читала, чтобы не чувствовать одиночество. Со временем стало проще, я научилась больше опираться на себя и быть внимательнее к чувствам других, потому что поняла, что у каждого могут быть свои трудные периоды, даже если это не видно сразу.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: Я завела привычку фиксировать мысли и идеи в заметках на телефоне: если приходит какая-то идея, я сразу записываю и потом возвращаюсь к этому. Это помогает не забывать свои задумки и, если надо, начать действовать. Иногда я перечитываю эти заметки спустя несколько недель и понимаю, что какие-то мысли уже неактуальны, а какие-то становятся полезными. Например, у меня была идея провести маленький проект для друзей, и я нашла её в старых заметках, что помогло воплотить её в жизнь. Также раз в неделю я планирую, что хочу сделать, распределяя задачи по приоритетам. Это не всегда работает идеально, но помогает не теряться в делах и видеть, что важно на этой неделе.

motivation_questions.question: Was there a time when you worked toward a goal for a long time but never reached it? What did you take away from that experience?
motivation_questions.answer: Да, я долго пыталась собрать кружок по психологии в нашей школе, разговаривала с одноклассниками, учителем, но никто особо не заинтересовался. Я старалась объяснять, почему это интересно, делала презентации, даже предлагала провести парочку небольших игр, связанных с психологией, но всё равно люди не откликались. В итоге я просто читала и делилась материалами онлайн, иногда отправляла одноклассникам ссылки, но больше для себя. Поняла, что не всегда твои интересы разделят, и надо быть готовой идти одной. Иногда поддержка не приходит, и это немного грустно, но всё равно можно продолжать заниматься тем, что нравится, даже если никто не рядом.

motivation_questions.question: Describe a project, idea, or solution you came up with entirely on your own initiative - not as an assignment. Where did the idea come from, and what was the outcome?
motivation_questions.answer: Я сама придумала провести микроопрос среди соседей, чтобы узнать, чего им не хватает во дворе. Сделала Google-форму, раздала ссылки нескольким семьям, анализировала результаты. Вопросы были простые: что им неудобно, чего не хватает, что бы они хотели изменить. Некоторые соседи отвечали устно, потому что не все пользуются интернетом, и я записывала их ответы вручную. Выяснилось, что людям не хватает лавочек и нормального освещения, особенно для пожилых и родителей с детьми. Я оформила основные пожелания и поделилась результатами с местным акиматом, написала обращение на их сайт. Реакции пока не было, но некоторые соседи сказали спасибо за инициативу, и мне стало немного спокойнее, что я хотя бы попыталась что-то изменить.

motivation_questions.question: How do you usually make important decisions - do you rely on intuition, data, or advice from others? Walk us through a specific real example from your life.
motivation_questions.answer: Больше доверяю интуиции и предварительному сбору информации, но могу посоветоваться с сестрой. Обычно, когда мне нужно принять важное решение, я сначала ищу разные источники информации, читаю отзывы и стараюсь понять общую картину. Например, при выборе университета я провела много времени, сравнивая сайты вузов, изучала их программы и читала мнения студентов на форумах. Иногда я записываю плюсы и минусы разных вариантов на листке бумаги, чтобы увидеть всё перед глазами. После этого обсуждала свои сомнения с сестрой, которая уже учится в другом городе и могла поделиться своим опытом. В итоге выбрала подачу в inVision U из-за универсальности программы и возможности попробовать себя в новых направлениях.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever faced a situation where you had to choose between what was efficient and what was right? What did you do, and why?
motivation_questions.answer: Однажды в проектной работе мы с командой столкнулись с выбором: чтобы ускорить процесс, некоторые предложили просто «скопировать» презентацию из интернета и немного изменить оформление. Это выглядело гораздо проще и, казалось, сэкономит кучу времени, ведь срок сдачи уже поджимал. Я настояла, что лучше сделать презентацию полностью самостоятельно, даже если это займет намного больше времени, потому что так честнее и спокойнее на душе. Нам пришлось переработать материал, распределить задачи, и иногда казалось, что мы не успеем. Но я понимала, что, если сделать по-своему, будет проще объяснить проект и отвечать на вопросы. В итоге работа заняла больше времени и сил, но я была уверена в результате и не переживала, что нас уличат в нечестности.

motivation_questions.question: Is there a problem in your city, country, or the world that genuinely troubles you? Why that one specifically - and what do you think is its root cause?
motivation_questions.answer: Меня волнует отсутствие удобных публичных пространств для молодежи в Костанае. Мало мест, где можно спокойно общаться и заниматься творчеством. Например, если хочется поиграть на гитаре с друзьями или просто почитать книгу на свежем воздухе, почти негде это сделать без помех. В основном, все кафе или парки маленькие, часто переполнены или не предназначены для подобных встреч. Думаю, корень в недостатке финансирования и нерегулярном диалоге власти с жителями. Часто решения принимаются без учета мнения самой молодежи, и получается, что те редкие пространства, которые есть, не отвечают нашим потребностям. Хочется, чтобы такие вопросы обсуждались открыто и больше ресурсов выделялось на создание комфортной среды.

motivation_questions.question: How has your worldview shifted over the last 2-3 years? What drove that change - a book, a person, an event, an experience?
motivation_questions.answer: За последние пару лет я стала меньше бояться пробовать что-то новое, чаще высказываю свои мнения. Это случилось после нескольких неудачных попыток — когда не получалось, оказывалось, что не так страшно, как казалось сначала. Например, раньше я стеснялась участвовать в школьных дебатах, думала, что если ошибусь, все будут смеяться. Но однажды я все же попробовала, и даже когда мой аргумент был слабым, никто не осудил меня, наоборот, поддержали. После этого стало легче говорить свое мнение и в других ситуациях — на уроках, в разговорах с друзьями. Кажется, что страх ошибиться не так мешает, если уже пару раз ошибался и ничего ужасного не произошло. Думаю, именно эти маленькие неудачи научили меня быть смелее и принимать себя, даже если что-то не получается с первого раза.

motivation_questions.question: What do you think Kazakhstan will look like in 20 years - and what role do you believe your generation will play in shaping it?
motivation_questions.answer: Надеюсь, Казахстан станет более открытым для инициатив, появится больше проектов от самих граждан. Мне кажется, что сейчас уже видно, как люди начинают больше интересоваться общественными вопросами, участвовать в волонтерстве, обсуждать важные темы в социальных сетях. Моё поколение может стать тем, кто будет создавать такие пространства, задавать вопросы и предлагать новые правила игры. Я считаю, что мы можем научиться не бояться ответственности, даже если иногда нет четких инструкций или поддержки сверху. Возможно, появятся новые формы сотрудничества между людьми — не только в больших городах, но и в регионах. Хочется верить, что мы сможем делать свой вклад, пусть даже небольшой, чтобы страна стала более активной и открытой.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: В классе однажды была история, когда девочку хотели не взять в команду для совместной работы. Я сказала, что это несправедливо, и настояла, чтобы её приняли. Не все были рады, и были недовольны моим «упрямством», но мне показалось правильным её поддержать. Я понимала, что ей и так было сложно, и если бы все отвернулись, ей стало бы ещё хуже. Некоторые одноклассники потом говорили мне, что я зря вмешалась, что это не моё дело, но мне казалось, что если промолчать, то становится участником несправедливости. Я не люблю конфликты, но иногда молчать просто невозможно. Позже я увидела, что эта девочка раскрылась в команде и даже стала более уверенна. Думаю, я бы поступила так же, даже если бы мне пришлось снова спорить со всеми.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: Помогала подружке справиться с тревожностью перед экзаменами — пересматривали программы, разговаривали, я искала техники релаксации. Мы вместе составляли расписание повторения, делали перерывы на чай, иногда просто гуляли, чтобы отвлечься. Я не была уверена, что смогу чем-то помочь, но хотела, чтобы ей стало легче, поэтому читала статьи о тревожности и предлагала попробовать дыхательные упражнения. Мне понравилось, что могу быть полезной, и увидела, что поддержка даже в быту многое меняет. После экзаменов подруга сказала, что мои советы и просто присутствие рядом помогли ей не сдаваться. Этот опыт показал мне, что даже простые действия и внимание могут оказать реальное влияние на состояние другого человека.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer: Искренность: никогда не скрываю своих чувств, даже если это невыгодно. Например, однажды на школьном обсуждении я открыто сказала, что не согласна с решением учителя, хотя знала, что это может повлиять на мою оценку. Справедливость: настаивала на участии девочки в командной работе. В нашем классе часто выбирают одних и тех же ребят, но я убедила команду взять девочку, которую обычно игнорируют, и она действительно хорошо справилась с задачей. Любопытство: постоянно ищу новые подходы в учебе и личных проектах, даже если не всегда довожу до конца. Например, недавно пыталась изучать программирование через онлайн-курсы, но бросила на середине — всё равно мне было интересно попробовать.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: Пробовала запустить челлендж по сбору пластика среди знакомых — первые дни был интерес, потом затухло, потому что никто не следил за процессом. Я просто написала в общий чат, что давайте собирать пластик, и мы даже обменялись парой фото с бутылками, но на этом всё и закончилось. Думаю, стоило придумать заметную мотивацию и вовлекать людей через соцсети. Например, можно было бы сделать таблицу, где видно, кто сколько собрал, или даже придумать какой-то маленький приз. Ещё, наверное, если бы я сама показывала пример, выкладывала бы сторис или делала напоминания, люди бы не забывали. В следующий раз попробую организовать всё более открыто и регулярно, чтобы был виден результат.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: Я делала макеты плакатов и брошюр для школьных мероприятий на заказ — за это мне давали небольшую оплату. Обычно работала с Canva, обсуждала с заказчиком, потом дорабатывала. Быстро поняла, что лучше сразу уточнять детали и сроки. Иногда было сложно понять, что именно хотят организаторы — бывало, что приходилось переделывать работу несколько раз из-за мелких замечаний или изменения темы мероприятия. Например, однажды я делала афишу для школьного концерта и после утверждения макета выяснилось, что поменялась дата и часть участников, пришлось срочно всё менять. Это научило меня задавать больше вопросов в начале, чтобы не тратить лишнее время потом. В целом, мне было интересно видеть, как мои работы используются на реальных событиях, и получать за это пусть небольшое, но настоящее вознаграждение.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: Я бы организовала бесплатные мастер-классы по современным профессиям для подростков Костаная: дизайну, урбанистике, экологии. Это дало бы подросткам ориентиры — у нас не хватает примеров нестандартных путей. Мне кажется, часто в нашем городе подростки не знают о существовании новых профессий, и у многих есть ощущение, что есть только стандартные пути, как медицина или юриспруденция. Я бы пригласила местных специалистов или даже онлайн-спикеров, чтобы они рассказали о своей работе, провели простые задания и поделились своим опытом. На тысячу долларов можно арендовать помещение, купить материалы для мастер-классов, сделать простую рекламу в соцсетях и напечатать буклеты. Мне бы хотелось, чтобы такие встречи вдохновили кого-то задуматься о будущем шире, чем обычно.

text_inputs.interview_text:
Моя мотивация не всегда постоянна — бывает, я вспыхиваю, завожусь какой-то идеей, но потом в обычной рутине теряю запал. Я часто замечаю, что вначале могу работать очень энергично, буквально не спать ночами, если что-то действительно захватило, но через пару недель возвращаюсь к привычным делам, и энергия рассеивается. Всё-таки хочется научиться завершать начатое и видеть реальный результат. Думаю, что именно поддержка единомышленников и работа в команде могут помочь не бросать начатое на полпути. inVision U кажется такой средой, где идеи не просто обсуждаются, но и реально реализуются, потому что есть структура и атмосфера, в которой не стыдно ошибаться или просить совета.

Что я могу дать? Открытость, честность, умение анализировать ситуации с разных сторон. Я не лидер по натуре, но часто задаю правильные вопросы и не соглашаюсь на полумеры. Например, на школьных проектах часто замечала, что многие готовы "сделать для галочки", а мне всегда хочется разобраться до конца, даже если это занимает больше времени или вызывает споры. Иногда мне сложно выразить мнение в большой группе, но я стараюсь быть тем человеком, кто замечает детали и подмечает то, что другие пропускают. Мне важно учиться работать в группе, а не только самостоятельно — в школе и на внеурочных занятиях я была скорее наблюдателем, чем активным участником, но именно поэтому сейчас хочу выйти из зоны комфорта. Я верю, что смогу стать частью интересных проектов, и найти здесь своё место, хотя не обещаю быть всегда самой активной — но обещаю быть вовлечённой и искренней.

Спасибо за внимание.

scenario_meta.notes: Well-written, confident, but actual experiences are shallow or less impactful than described.

content_profile.adversity_pattern: minimal real adversity, overcome in story

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
С самого детства я любил наблюдать за людьми вокруг себя — их общением, реакциями, как они строят отношения. Даже на семейных праздниках я часто замечал, как один человек может разрядить напряжённую обстановку, а другой — наоборот, случайно всё испортить. В школе я часто становился тем, кто помогает одноклассникам решать конфликты, объяснял сложные вопросы и поддерживал даже тех, с кем мало общался. Иногда это было непросто: бывало, что я не знал, как подобрать нужные слова или боялся, что вмешаюсь не вовремя, но со временем понял, что даже простое участие может изменить ситуацию. Мне всегда было интересно, почему одни люди могут вдохновлять окружающих, а другие — нет. Именно поэтому я решил развиваться в области коммуникаций, ведь эффективное общение может изменить не только отдельного человека, но и всю команду, даже общество.

Моё увлечение ораторским искусством началось ещё в младших классах — я участвовал в школьных дебатах, вел праздники, а потом и сам организовал команду, чтобы научиться работать вместе. Первый наш совместный проект был довольно простым — мы подготовили небольшое представление для начальных классов. Несмотря на простоту, я впервые почувствовал ответственность за других, ведь мне пришлось объяснять участникам, что делать, и поддерживать их, когда они волновались. Именно через эти активности я почувствовал эффект командной работы и понял, как важно уметь слушать и слышать других. Не всегда получалось идеально: порой наши идеи не принимали или мы спорили по пустякам. Например, однажды мы целый час спорили, какую песню выбрать для выступления, пока я не предложил каждому высказаться по очереди, чтобы учесть все мнения. В такие моменты я учился идти на компромисс и объяснять свою позицию так, чтобы убедить без давления.

В 10 классе я предложил сделать цикл встреч «Общение без границ» — мы приглашали специалистов, которые рассказывали о невербальных сигналах, о том, как оставаться уверенным. Этот проект был проведён просто по моей инициативе: я увидел, что ребята боятся выражать свои мысли, и решил это изменить. Мы столкнулись с трудностями: не все преподаватели были сразу согласны отпустить учеников, а некоторые одноклассники стеснялись задавать вопросы. Тогда я решил начать сам — задал вопрос первому спикеру, чтобы «разморозить» атмосферу. После этого многие стали активнее выступать, у них появилась уверенность. Мне было приятно, что простой проект может так повлиять. Некоторые одноклассники потом подходили ко мне и говорили, что впервые почувствовали себя услышанными.

Я выбрал inVision U, потому что здесь ценится желание создавать что-то новое и работать вместе, а ещё важно реально видеть результат своих идей. Я не супер-отличник, но мне кажется, что иногда главное — это умение вдохновить людей и не бояться браться за сложные задачи. Я хочу научиться не только теории, но и практике, чтобы в будущем уметь реализовывать проекты, которые могут объединять людей с разными взглядами. Хочу расти не только как специалист, но и как человек, который может объединять других для чего-то большего. Для меня важно чувствовать, что мои действия помогают не только мне, но и окружающим. Я надеюсь, что смогу использовать свои навыки для развития проектов, которые изменят наш город и страну. Мне хочется, чтобы мои идеи и энергия нашли применение именно там, где это даст реальный результат.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: Самым сложным для меня был момент, когда я впервые столкнулся с сильным конфликтом в классе — мои близкие друзья поссорились, и атмосфера была тяжёлой. Тогда мне пришлось быть посредником между ними. Я созвал небольшую встречу, где мы открыто обсудили накопившиеся обиды. Было сложно слышать неприятные слова, особенно когда они были адресованы друг другу или даже мне, но я понимал, что честность — лучший путь. Оказалось, что проблемы были из-за мелочей, которые просто копились со временем. После этого я понял: не нужно бояться разговаривать открыто, даже если страшно и есть риск обидеть кого-то. Теперь в сложных ситуациях я чаще беру инициативу на себя, стараюсь не откладывать разговоры и не избегать трудных тем. Это многому меня научило, например, слушать других и не делать поспешных выводов.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: Я стал записывать свои задачи на день в блокнот — даже самые маленькие — и вечером отмечаю выполненное. Этот простой способ помогает не забывать важное и чувствовать удовлетворение от сделанного. Иногда я даже записываю такие мелочи, как "ответить на письмо другу" или "убрать на столе", чтобы не перегружаться и видеть, что двигаюсь вперёд, даже если день был сложным.
