motivation_questions.answer: I began mentoring middle schoolers in my old neighborhood last year, focusing on study skills, emotional well-being, and coping with family difficulties. I saw my younger self in them: bright but anxious, sometimes overwhelmed by things at home. I wanted to offer support I had sometimes lacked. Making time for them wasn’t always easy, but it was deeply rewarding. I learned that helping isn’t about having all the answers — often, it’s just about listening and believing in someone’s potential. It gave me more confidence in my ability to make real, personal impact, and it fueled my motivation to work in youth leadership.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer: My three core values are integrity, empathy, and resilience. Integrity: I refused a lucrative sponsor for my youth events project because they demanded biased coverage, staying true to our values rather than taking the easy path. Empathy: I mentor students struggling in their families, offering listening and support even when my own schedule is tight. Resilience: I managed to maintain my studies, work part-time, and build new routines during my mother’s illness, bouncing back from setbacks instead of giving up. All these values are woven into how I try to lead, learn, and contribute.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: Yes, I’ve co-organized several pop-up clean-ups and cultural meetups in Almaty. What worked: using existing Telegram groups and influencers to spread the word, which brought a diverse crowd. What didn’t: not allocating clear roles in my first event — it led to confusion and some volunteers leaving early. If I did it again, I’d build a small organizing team first, clarify responsibilities, and create a digital checklist for logistics. I also learned that even well-intentioned events can fail without detailed planning and contingency preparation. Now, I see that successful projects are built on both passion and procedural discipline.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: To support my family, I tutored English and basic math to younger students in my neighborhood. I developed flyers using Canva, advertised on VK and neighborhood chats, and offered free first lessons, which helped build trust. Over eight months, I worked with 12 students, most from families who couldn’t afford big centers, and tailored sessions to their needs. I learned how to communicate complex topics simply, and grew in patience. The financial result helped my family with bills and hospital costs, but more importantly, I built relationships and saw firsthand the difference accessible tutoring can make for kids facing difficult circumstances.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: I would create a micro-grant program for underprivileged students in Almaty to run their own small community improvement projects: planting trees, peer tutoring, or art in public spaces. I’d use a small online application and pick the ten best ideas, giving each $100 for materials and mentoring from local activists. This would empower youth to become agents of change, develop their skills, and see immediate results in their environments. I believe the real value is not just in the projects themselves, but in building the confidence and experience of a new generation of leaders for Kazakhstan.

text_inputs.interview_text:
Thank you for the opportunity to introduce myself further. When people ask what motivates me, I often say it’s the sense of possibility I see in Kazakhstan, especially among young people. My experiences — and my family’s ups and downs — taught me that real change starts in small communities and grows outward. I want to use my time at inVision U to both learn and contribute right away: participate in interdisciplinary team projects and also start new civic initiatives. I’m genuinely excited about the program’s project-oriented approach, as I believe challenges in Kazakhstan will be solved by those who know how to move ideas from inspiration to action, and not only by those who achieve perfect grades.

I know my strengths lie in connecting people, organizing small but impactful actions, and persisting even when things seem tough. I also recognize my weaknesses — sometimes I take on too many responsibilities or hesitate to ask for help until it’s almost too late. But I believe the environment at inVision U will help me manage this by working alongside equally driven people who can teach and challenge me. I especially look forward to the chance to work in mixed teams and to learn more about technology’s role in social change. Ultimately, I want to come out not just with knowledge, but already having made visible, positive changes in at least one local community, and the skills to do much more.

scenario_meta.notes: Candidate from Semey, creative, some gaps in essay, ideas not fully developed, visible real potential.

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
Здравствуйте, меня зовут Марат Маханов, and I am from Semey. Я вырос в многодетной семье в небольшом микрорайоне, где все знают друг друга but often nothing changes. Sometimes it feels like people get used to things just being the way they are, not expecting much, and not wanting to stand out. I am applying to inVision U because I want to believe my ideas — about art, about communities, about changing how we see our neighbors — can matter, even if not on a huge scale at first.

When I was younger, I was really shy, sometimes almost invisible at school. Teachers never remembered my name, and I often felt more comfortable sitting in the back, drawing in my notebook instead of talking. But I always had this urge to draw or make little models from whatever I found — matchsticks, clay, even old cardboard boxes. Once my cousin asked me why I was wasting my time, and I realized I wanted to do something with my skills, but I didn’t know what yet. I tried to copy cartoon characters or build tiny houses, but I never showed them to anyone because I was scared people would laugh.

A year ago, after our street lights kept breaking (and no one cared), I painted little murals on the light poles with positive messages, some in Kazakh, some in Russian. At first, people laughed, but then some younger kids started copying — the light poles looked a bit brighter, and the old people in our building said they liked seeing something cheerful. I remember one grandmother saying, “Теперь, когда иду в магазин, улыбаюсь, даже если устала,” and that made me realize small changes can matter. After that, we organized a mini art day in our yard. It wasn’t perfect — some paint got spilled, and a neighbor complained — but I felt proud. It was the first time I saw people working together on something creative, even if it was messy. We didn’t make a masterpiece, but for a week, everyone talked about it.

inVision U attracts me because I’ve tried to study different things (I read about urban design, I sketch, I do some 3D modeling on my phone), and I want to mix these ideas into something useful. Sometimes I try to imagine how our yard or our school could look if we changed just a few things — more color, more places to sit, maybe spaces for people to talk. I know I lack some technical skills, and my English is not perfect, but I want a place where people seriously care about projects, not just exams. When I tried to learn 3D modeling, I watched videos in English and kept pausing to translate, and it was slow, but I enjoyed it. I hope I can learn to bring ideas to life more confidently, work in teams, and maybe finally finish something big, not just start. Я не знаю точно, кем стану, но знаю, что не хочу оставаться незамеченным или ничего не делать. If I can make even a small difference in my neighborhood or for someone who feels invisible like I did, I think it will be worth it.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: The most difficult period for me was when my father lost his job during the pandemic. Our family had to cut back on everything: internet, some meals, even electricity got cut off once. I felt ashamed when friends invited me out and I couldn’t go. To cope, I started volunteering to help my mother with her tailoring side jobs, sometimes even delivering finished clothes to customers. It made me realize how much my parents sacrificed, and gave me patience and humility. I also learned to find small joys — like sketching with pencil stubs, or organizing small courtyard games for kids. After that, I stopped being so embarrassed and started focusing more on how to help others around me. I’m still learning how not to give up when things seem unfair.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: I try to wake up before my siblings, so I can have some quiet minutes to think or sketch. Sometimes I write down my ideas or things I want to try. I started this as a way to have my own space, since in a big family it’s not always possible. It helps me feel calm and a bit more organized. Also, I try to go for a walk every day, even in bad weather, so I can see how the neighborhood changes. It helps me get new thoughts for drawings or little art projects. These habits were chosen not because someone told me, but because without them I start getting lost in daily routines and feeling like nothing will change. They help me believe that I can create even something small that matters.

motivation_questions.question: Was there a time when you worked toward a goal for a long time but never reached it? What did you take away from that experience?
motivation_questions.answer: Yes, last winter, I tried to organize a small exhibition of community art in our school hall. The idea was to collect works from different kids, even those who were not usually considered ‘talented.’ I spent months talking to teachers and students, but in the end, the school said there was no budget for anything extra, and most students were busy with exams. I felt disappointed, like maybe my ideas were too weak. But I realized that not everyone is ready to try new things, and sometimes good ideas need more support or better timing. I learned that failure doesn’t mean I should stop suggesting things — maybe I need to be more persistent or try a smaller scale first.

motivation_questions.question: Describe a project, idea, or solution you came up with entirely on your own initiative - not as an assignment. Where did the idea come from, and what was the outcome?
motivation_questions.answer: The murals on our light poles came completely from my initiative. I got the idea while reading a book about public art in other countries and noticed how gray everything looked outside after winter. I used leftover paint from my mother’s tailoring supplies and started with one pole. At first, people just stared or laughed, but after a few days kids from the next подъезд helped, and people started leaving small thank you notes. Eventually, the city cleaned over some of the murals, but a few signs remain. The outcome was small, but it gave me confidence — people can see beauty in small things, and sometimes one person’s action is enough to start small talk about change.

motivation_questions.question: How do you usually make important decisions - do you rely on intuition, data, or advice from others? Walk us through a specific real example from your life.
motivation_questions.answer: Mostly, I rely on a mix between intuition and advice from my older sister. For example, when deciding whether to spend some saved money on better art supplies or contribute to household food, I talked to her honestly. She said the family came first but also found a way to buy me extra sketch pads from her sewing money. I try to look at what feels right but also ask someone who knows me well. Some decisions are difficult — like whether to quit chess club to have more time for art, which I did after considering how much I actually enjoyed each activity. Usually, I listen first to what my heart says, then try to balance with what is practical for my family.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever faced a situation where you had to choose between what was efficient and what was right? What did you do, and why?
motivation_questions.answer: Yes, once during a school clean-up, some students wanted to skip the hardest parts and just sign the attendance list. I could have done the same to save time, but it felt wrong. Instead, I stayed to clean up the most littered part behind the gym, even though it was tiring. A few others stayed when they saw me doing it. Later, a teacher noticed and thanked us. It would have been easier to take a shortcut, but I remembered how I feel when people ignore problems. I think choosing what is right, even when it’s harder, fits my values more than pretending or taking easy paths.

motivation_questions.question: Is there a problem in your city, country, or the world that genuinely troubles you? Why that one specifically - and what do you think is its root cause?
motivation_questions.answer: The lack of safe and inviting public spaces for kids and teens bothers me in Semey. After school, there’s nowhere really to go except for a few playgrounds, and often they are run down or full of trash. This means young people mostly stay at home with their phones or hang out in подъезды. I think the root cause is the city not investing in youth spaces, and maybe also not believing kids will use them well. If there were more attention to youth voices and the ideas they suggest, things might change slowly. I wish more young people could take some responsibility for making public places friendlier.

motivation_questions.question: How has your worldview shifted over the last 2-3 years? What drove that change - a book, a person, an event, an experience?
motivation_questions.answer: In the last few years, I started believing more in the power of small actions. Early on, I thought only big, official changes mattered, but after painting murals and organizing the art yard day, I see that ordinary people can change the feeling of a place. Also, meeting a university student who returned to Semey and started a youth reading group inspired me. I realized you don’t need to wait for permission — you can do something now, even if it’s simple. My worldview is now a bit more optimistic, even if my city has many problems. I believe more in action than in only dreaming.

motivation_questions.question: What do you think Kazakhstan will look like in 20 years - and what role do you believe your generation will play in shaping it?
motivation_questions.answer: I think Kazakhstan will have more technology, maybe more tall buildings, but I hope it will also be more colorful and open for all people, not just the rich. My generation needs to make sure cities are not only for cars and adults but for kids, for artists, for people with different ideas. I think we can show that being active in your neighborhood matters, even if you never get famous for it. I want to help create examples of small changes that show everyone can do something, no matter where they were born.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: I remember when some boys in my class mocked a new student because of his strong accent from the village. Most others kept silent or laughed, but I invited him to join our courtyard football game. Some friends thought I was odd, but after a few weeks he became part of our group. I did it because I know how bad it feels to be invisible or excluded. Maybe I lost some approval from classmates, but I felt proud I tried to change at least one person’s experience at school.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: I helped my neighbor’s grandson, who was struggling with reading. His parents left for work abroad, and he was living with his grandmother. I offered to read comics with him after school. We spent months doing this and, slowly, he improved. I did it because I remembered what it was like learning to read as a child, and no one forced me to do it. The experience gave me patience and taught me that helping someone is not always about big gestures, but about consistency. It also helped me feel that even someone who isn’t a teacher can make a difference.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer: Creativity — I paint, make models, and try to make public spaces a bit more cheerful, like my work on the light poles in my neighborhood where I helped design and paint colorful patterns last summer. Responsibility — I helped my mother with her tailoring business, even when I wanted to spend time drawing or out with friends. For example, I would organize fabric and sometimes talk to her customers, which taught me patience. Kindness — I stood up for classmates who were left out, and taught my neighbor’s grandson how to read better. I would read with him twice a week, even when I felt tired. All these aren’t big achievements, but I know they match my values and remind me what kind of person I want to be.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: Yes, the yard art day in our microdistrict. What worked: getting kids involved, using leftover materials, and inviting elders for tea at the same time. It was nice to see different generations talking together, and it made the event feel more like a community thing. What didn’t: not preparing for complaints or bad weather — the rain almost ruined some of the drawings, and a neighbor was upset about the chalk mess. If I do it again, I would check the weather forecast, ask building management for permission, and maybe prepare some indoor activities just in case. I also learned to plan for feedback, both positive and negative. Next time, I’d probably make a flyer explaining the idea beforehand to avoid misunderstandings.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: To earn money, I sometimes sold small painted magnets and bookmarks at the school fair. I designed each one by hand, using scrap materials. I had to negotiate prices, which was uncomfortable at first, but I learned how to talk about the value of my work. I didn’t make much money, but a few teachers and parents asked for custom work, so it grew a little. It wasn’t a business, really, but it gave me confidence to try selling my creativity. The result was I felt a bit more independent and realized my skills could help my family.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: I would organize a free art and reading camp for local kids who usually can’t afford extra classes. I would invite older students as volunteers and use part of the money for supplies and healthy snacks. The camp could be in our building’s basement or nearby school gym. I think this could help kids find new hobbies, maybe feel less bored or lonely in the summer. I want to do this because I didn’t have these chances growing up, and I want the next generation to see they can make and learn things, even when there isn’t much money for it.

text_inputs.interview_text:
To be honest, I still have doubts about myself. I never led any big project or won city competitions, but I always try to do something for my street or my neighborhood. What I hope for at inVision U is to learn how to finish what I start, and to find group members who also care about projects, not only tests. Sometimes, I am slow to share my ideas because I think they might not matter, but when people notice what I do — like painting, like helping neighbors — it gives me motivation to try more.

My weakness is sometimes I plan too much but act too little, or I get scared if people don’t support me. I hope university will teach me new skills and make me more brave, especially using technology, which I do not know well yet. I want to take what I learn back to my hometown and show others they don’t need lots of money or approval to do something useful.

My dream is not to be famous but to create something that surviving in my district, even when I am gone. That could be art, reading clubs, or just giving kids a place to express themselves.

scenario_meta.notes: Candidate from Aktobe, mix of strong and vague answers, some contradictions, variable self-awareness.

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
Меня зовут Айзат Кожахметова из Актобе. I’m applying to inVision U потому, что мне важна не столько профессия, сколько возможность постоянно меняться и помогать другим меняться. My journey to this point hasn’t been straightforward. I started out thinking I’d be an engineer, then switched to volunteering, then got interested in tutoring and education. Я росла в достаточно обычной семье, где всегда поощряли учебу, но никто не настаивал на какой-то одной профессии. Я пробовала разное, и до сих пор не уверена, что нашла именно свое направление. 

It’s hard to sum up my motivation in one way, because I’m still exploring different things. For example, I co-founded a project preparing kids from small settlements for city school exams. At first, I honestly didn’t think it would be a big deal — just helping a few kids with math homework over WhatsApp. But then their parents got interested, and more kids started joining. We had to organize online meetings; I had to figure out how to explain topics I didn’t always remember myself, and sometimes I would spend evenings looking up solutions before our calls. I thought it would be just a short-term thing, but now it has grown. It’s not perfect — sometimes some students just stop coming, or internet connection is bad, or I feel like I’m not doing enough. On the other hand, some projects I started didn’t last — like an attempt to do a recycling campaign at our school, which faded after two months. I thought people would be interested, but most students just ignored the boxes, and I didn’t know how to motivate them. That experience disappointed me, but it also made me think about what makes some social projects “work” and others disappear. Maybe I need to learn more about organizing and motivating people.

I was attracted to inVision U for the freedom to choose diverse courses, and the idea that students can design their own contributions to society. Sometimes I feel trapped by the idea that I have to pick just one path, but inVision U seems to encourage curiosity and experimentation. I like working in groups, though honestly sometimes I get frustrated if there isn’t a clear leader or plan, and I’m not always that leader myself. For example, in my volunteering group, we often have long discussions but not much action unless someone pushes. I’m trying to learn how to support others and not just criticize when things go slow. My city, Aktobe, has many problems, like inequality between districts and lack of creative spaces. For instance, in my neighborhood, there are almost no libraries or clubs, and teenagers spend time in shopping malls or just on the street. I hope to get the skills at inVision U to build better social initiatives, especially for young people at risk. I have some ideas, like organizing weekend workshops or peer-to-peer tutoring, but I don’t have the knowledge or network to make it real yet.

I’m not sure what I want to specialize in — sometimes I think about digital business, other times about education programs for rural youth. My interests are still changing, and I don’t want to pretend I have a clear plan. But what I do know: I want my work to really help people, not just sound good. I believe in learning by doing, and I hope this university will help me finally connect my ambitions with long-term results. Even if I fail sometimes, I want to be in a place where I can try, reflect, and try again. That’s why I hope to join inVision U — to find a community of people who also want to experiment, contribute, and grow step by step.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: The hardest period was during 11th grade when I lost motivation completely. My grades dropped, I clashed with friends, and I didn’t want to join in family activities. It was probably a mix of school pressure and not knowing what I wanted. I coped by taking a break from social media and walking a lot outside. I also tried journaling, which I hadn’t done before. Surprisingly (for me), I started to see patterns and realized I needed to try new things — that’s when I started volunteering at the youth center. Afterward, I became more open to exploring, less afraid to fail. But sometimes I fall back into old cycles if I get bored or stressed again.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: I try to spend the first 30 minutes of every day offline, reading something not related to school. Sometimes this is a book, sometimes just a long article. I chose this because when I look at my phone first, I get distracted all day. The habit doesn’t always work — when I have a lot of tests, I skip it. When I do keep it, it helps my attention and gives me new ideas for projects or discussions. Another habit is making to-do lists, but honestly, I often forget to check them later, so maybe this is a habit that needs change.

motivation_questions.question: Was there a time when you worked toward a goal for a long time but never reached it? What did you take away from that experience?
motivation_questions.answer: Yes, last year I wanted to make a successful after-school program for girls interested in STEM in my city. We got five students and ran a few sessions, but then interest faded and I couldn’t keep people motivated. I realized it’s not easy to keep projects alive when it’s all volunteer-based, especially when people have exams or family commitments. Sometimes students just stopped coming without explanation. What I learned is that next time I should survey people’s interests better and maybe get some official support, like from a school or a local organization, to make it feel more “real.” I also learned to accept that not all ideas are as urgent for others as they are for me. It was disappointing, but I still think it was worth trying.

motivation_questions.question: Describe a project, idea, or solution you came up with entirely on your own initiative - not as an assignment. Where did the idea come from, and what was the outcome?
motivation_questions.answer: I started a tutoring circle for kids from nearby villages who would come to Aktobe every weekend. The idea came when I met a girl on the bus who felt lost in city school exams. I recruited my friend, found a classroom at the youth center, and started free sessions. At first, it was chaotic, but over time some students improved a lot — a few passed their scholarship exams. The outcome: more confidence for me and for the students. I realized that even if it was small, it mattered for somebody. But I also struggled to keep it regular, especially during exam season.

motivation_questions.question: How do you usually make important decisions - do you rely on intuition, data, or advice from others? Walk us through a specific real example from your life.
motivation_questions.answer: To be honest, I rely a lot on how I feel, but then talk with my older cousin for advice. Like when I had to decide whether to focus on my final school exams or continue the prep project for village students. My heart said keep doing both, but my cousin convinced me to reduce volunteering for a while, so my grades wouldn’t fall lower. I was doubtful but followed the advice. In the end, my scores improved, but I felt guilty about leaving some kids out. So, I usually end up somewhere between my instinct and what people who care about me suggest.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever faced a situation where you had to choose between what was efficient and what was right? What did you do, and why?
motivation_questions.answer: Yes, during the tutoring project, I could have just given students past exam answers, which would have been much faster and more efficient. But I decided to teach them the logic behind the problems, so they would actually learn and understand the material, even though this took much more time and meant we couldn’t cover everything we planned. Some students got frustrated and told me they just wanted quick results and to pass the exams, but I stood my ground because I believed it was more important for them to build real knowledge, not just get scores. I remember one student in particular who kept asking for shortcuts, and it was tempting to give in, but I kept explaining why understanding was better. Sometimes it’s not popular, and people might not agree, but I try to live with my decisions even if they are difficult.

motivation_questions.question: Is there a problem in your city, country, or the world that genuinely troubles you? Why that one specifically - and what do you think is its root cause?
motivation_questions.answer: One major problem in Aktobe is inequality in educational access. Kids from remote villages or poor neighborhoods start at a huge disadvantage. The root cause is complex — money, lack of teachers, and sometimes government not listening to young voices. For example, some schools in the city have new computers and great teachers, while village schools might have just one math teacher for all grades. I think the system rewards already-privileged students and leaves others feeling education isn’t for them. This really bothers me because I see wasted talent every day, and I believe with better support or even peer groups, more kids would succeed. Sometimes I wonder how many future scientists or artists never get the chance to discover their abilities simply because of where they were born.

motivation_questions.question: How has your worldview shifted over the last 2-3 years? What drove that change - a book, a person, an event, an experience?
motivation_questions.answer: Two years ago, I didn’t really care about social issues. But after working at the youth center and meeting kids with real struggles, I became more aware. For example, I met a boy who had to skip school to help his family, which made me realize how different people’s challenges can be. Also, reading "Educated" by Tara Westover opened my eyes to the importance of education and resilience. Before that, I thought success was mostly luck — now I see it needs both support systems and personal effort. My attitude toward helping others became more serious, though sometimes I still feel I’m not doing enough. Sometimes I wonder if small efforts matter, but I try to keep going.

motivation_questions.question: What do you think Kazakhstan will look like in 20 years - and what role do you believe your generation will play in shaping it?
motivation_questions.answer: Kazakhstan will change a lot, maybe become more modern and connected with the world. But I worry about keeping equality; technology and development might leave some behind, especially in rural or smaller towns where not everyone has the same opportunities. My generation will have to fight for inclusion and better education for all regions, not just in the big cities like Almaty or Astana. We’ll also have to fix environmental issues, though I’m less involved in that area and sometimes don’t know where to start. For example, the air pollution in some cities or the problems with water in villages could get worse if we ignore them. I hope we won’t just copy other countries, but actually create our own models that fit Kazakhstan’s mix of urban and rural life, traditions, and values.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: I remember once in my class, most students wanted to skip a presentation to go outside, but I stayed behind to help the teacher, who was clearly overwhelmed. My friends made fun of me, calling me a ‘zavuch-liker,’ which basically means someone who always tries to please teachers. It was a bit embarrassing, and I felt awkward standing there alone at first, but I didn’t feel right just leaving. I did it because I saw teachers as humans who need help sometimes, not just authority figures. I helped carry some materials and set up the projector. After a while, a few others started helping too, maybe because they saw me or just felt guilty. I realized sometimes going against the crowd, even for small things, can make a difference, even if nobody really says thank you.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: I spent a summer helping at a local orphanage, running English classes for kids. I joined because I wanted the volunteer hours, but ended up connecting with the kids. They surprised me with their curiosity and humor—some would ask questions way beyond the lesson, like what life was like in other countries, or what my favorite book was. I kept returning, even after finishing my required hours, just to hang out or play soccer with them. The experience taught me to look beyond labels (like ‘orphans’ or ‘poor kids’) and see people as individuals with their own personalities and dreams. It also left me feeling both grateful for my own opportunities and a bit frustrated at the limits of what I could do to help. Sometimes I wished I could do more than just teach a few classes.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer:
Persistence: When I failed to get enough interest in the STEM club, I still finished the curriculum for the few girls who stayed, even though I felt like stopping. I remember one meeting when only two people showed up, and I wondered if it was worth it, but I realized I didn't want to let them down.  
Fairness: In debate club, I argued in favor of including less popular students, not just ‘stars.’ For example, I suggested we rotate team leaders so everyone gets a chance, even if they were less vocal. It sometimes made the meetings slower, but I think it was more fair.  
Curiosity: I’m always trying out new clubs or projects, even if I quit some of them early. Sometimes this is a weakness too, but I think it makes me adaptable. For instance, I joined an art workshop even though I had no experience, mostly just to see what it would be like.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: Co-founding the rural tutoring circle was the biggest. What worked was starting with people I already knew from volunteering, because we already trusted each other and it was easier to organize initial sessions. What didn’t work was relying on everyone’s free time — attendance dropped during holidays and exam periods, and sometimes tutors cancelled last minute. If I did it again, I’d try to partner sooner with an official school or get outside funding to pay volunteers, since we struggled to keep people motivated without any compensation. I also would set clearer schedules so students and tutors could rely on consistency, maybe by using a shared calendar app or having a fixed weekly meeting spot, instead of always changing times and locations.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: I tutored English for two middle schoolers in our apartment building. I started because their parents couldn’t afford extra help, so I charged less than city rates to make it possible for them. I made weekly lesson plans and sometimes borrowed worksheets or games from my own school materials to keep things interesting. Both students had different strengths, so I had to adapt each lesson—one struggled with writing, the other with speaking. By the end, both students improved a lot, and their parents recommended me to others in the building, though I couldn’t take on more students when my own studies became overwhelming. The value wasn’t just earning some money, but realizing I can teach and adapt to different needs, which gave me confidence.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: I’d use the money to set up a series of weekend workshops for rural students coming to Aktobe: topics would be study skills, self-confidence, maybe some coding basics. I’d hire a few university mentors and cover transportation for students living far. My goal: help students see new paths for themselves and not be limited by where they were born. Sometimes big programs are too slow, so small workshops can make a fast local difference. Even just showing someone a new way to organize notes, or introducing a basic coding game, can change how they see their own abilities. I think a lot of rural students don’t get to ask questions in a relaxed way to older students, so having mentors from the university could make things more approachable and less intimidating for them.

text_inputs.interview_text:
I guess it’s hard for me to explain my full motivation because, as I said, I change interests sometimes. It’s not that I’m not serious, but my curiosity pulls me in different directions. But when I’m really involved in projects helping others, especially young people, I feel the most alive and useful. For example, last year I volunteered at a local youth center, helping teenagers with basic computer skills. Seeing them gain confidence made me feel like I was actually contributing something, even if it was just small. 

Sometimes I struggle with follow-through — I get excited at the start, then energy drops when it’s routine or when I hit obstacles. I try to push through, but honestly, I don’t always succeed. Other times, I can be too stubborn or try to do things my own way, which can annoy teammates or slow things down. I’ve noticed this especially when working in group projects at school. I want to get better at listening and not just assuming I have the best idea.

What I want from inVision U is not just knowledge, but structure and ongoing challenges, so I don’t get bored or lost. I want to learn from people who are better at finishing things, and maybe find my real focus. My strengths are flexibility, creativity, and willingness to try new things—even if I sometimes overcommit. My weaknesses: being scattered, sometimes not listening enough, always starting more than I can handle. But I am genuinely committed to making a difference, and I hope this university will give me the right environment and support to finally bring my ideas to completion and learn how to be more consistent.

scenario_meta.notes: Candidate from rural Turkistan region, strong underlying capability, modest, underplays achievements, writing is indirect and sometimes vague.

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
Меня зовут Данияр Кунаев, живу в селе недалеко от Сарыагаща, Туркестанская область. To be honest, I don’t have experience of big city life or big achievements. My parents are farmers, and I help as much as I can. Most of my life, I thought only city students or rich people could get a good education, but my village teachers supported my curiosity for natural sciences and sometimes English.

I want to apply to inVision U because I feel our rural problems are not being solved by anyone from outside. The city rules don’t really work here. I started by trying to help my family’s small бизнес: experimenting on compost, making schedule for irrigation, and reading about climate adaptation. For instance, last spring I tried adding eggshells and old potato skins to our compost pile after reading about it online, and it really seemed to improve the soil for our tomatoes. Then I tried to help neighbors when frost or dry weather threatened harvests. Once, I organized a small discussion circle in the school library about what kind of new crops we could try. I was nervous, but it was okay, some classmates joined, and even a teacher said it was useful. We talked about sunflowers and chickpeas, because they use less water and might survive if summers get hotter. It was a small step, but it felt like maybe even school students could think about solutions.

I don’t see myself as a leader, but more as someone who notices problems and tries something. For example, when our village well system broke, I looked up repair manuals online, asked an older neighbor for advice, and together we fixed the main pump. It is not something big maybe, but the fact that kids could help adults surprised even me. Sometimes, after this, some younger neighbors started asking me if I could show them how to look things up on the internet for their own questions. I like this – sharing what I learn, even if it’s just small things.

I love reading about new farming methods, about how rural life can become better for everybody, not just business owners. Sometimes I find videos in Russian or English, and I try to write down the main ideas in a notebook. For example, I learned about drip irrigation and simple greenhouse techniques. We cannot always afford new equipment, but sometimes we can build something similar from old plastic bottles or leftover materials. My dream is that one day our village will not just survive, but become a place where young people want to stay, not just leave for the city.

My school is not special, but our teachers are very dedicated. I sometimes help other students after class, especially with math and chemistry, because I find it interesting and want others to understand too. My English is still improving, but I read articles online to keep up. Sometimes I use a dictionary or ask my teacher for help with hard words. inVision U interests me because here, maybe I can learn about how to make agriculture more sustainable, how to bring ideas home, and how to show others in the village that it’s possible to create changes. I’m not sure if my way of thinking is interesting for you, because there are many smarter and louder applicants from cities, but I really want to try. I believe even small steps can make a difference, and I hope to learn how to take more of them.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: The hardest time was last year, when my father got sick during the main planting season. My older brother was working far away, so I had to take much more responsibility in the fields and at home. It was stressful, as the weather was also unpredictable, and it felt like too much. I coped by talking to neighbors and asking some local elders for advice. I made a daily list to manage everything — feeding livestock, helping my younger sister, finishing my studies late at night. What changed is that now I believe I can handle difficult things if I ask for help and break tasks down. It made me more confident and also more understanding if people need support.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: I started making to-do lists every evening, because with farm work and school, it is easy to forget something important. Usually, I write down things like homework, feeding animals, or even small tasks like fixing a fence. I also created a habit to read at least 15 minutes about something new (science, weather, English language) every night before sleep. Sometimes I read news articles or listen to an English podcast. These habits help me not get overwhelmed and improve myself slowly, since there is not much opportunity for courses here. I chose these because sometimes I saw my classmates forgetting homework or missing livestock chores, and I did not want the same thing to happen to me. With a plan, I feel less stress and can help others better, for example, reminding my younger brother about his chores.

motivation_questions.question: Was there a time when you worked toward a goal for a long time but never reached it? What did you take away from that experience?
motivation_questions.answer: Yes, I wanted to start a school garden with drip irrigation, but the school administration said there was no budget for it. I tried to collect pipes and leftover materials from neighbors, but it was not enough. We only managed a small patch, not a real garden like I had imagined with many types of vegetables and flowers. The lesson I learned is that sometimes you must accept limits, but even small tries can show others what’s possible. Some classmates who saw our little patch became interested, and a few even helped water it. Next time, I would try to organize more people before starting, so that there is more support or even outside help. Maybe we could have done a fundraiser or asked local businesses for materials.

motivation_questions.question: Describe a project, idea, or solution you came up with entirely on your own initiative - not as an assignment. Where did the idea come from, and what was the outcome?
motivation_questions.answer: I decided to test homemade compost to improve soil for our family’s tomato field. I read several guides online and watched YouTube videos to figure out the process, since nobody in our village tried it before. I collected kitchen scraps, leaves, and manure, then waited and turned the pile every week. After three months, the tomatoes grew bigger and healthier, which surprised my parents. Some neighbors noticed the change and got interested in doing the same, asking me questions about the method. The outcome was not just better vegetables, but learning that small changes can have visible results even if they seem unimportant at first. It encouraged me to keep experimenting and share the results calmly, even if at first people doubt it or think it’s strange.

motivation_questions.question: How do you usually make important decisions - do you rely on intuition, data, or advice from others? Walk us through a specific real example from your life.
motivation_questions.answer: Usually, I try to collect information first (from Internet, books, and older people) and then see what feels most realistic. For example, when deciding whether to buy new seeds for our fields, I compared prices online, asked a neighbor who tried them before, and discussed with my mother. We decided together to buy only half, just to test before using more money. The result was mixed, but by asking and searching before acting, we didn’t lose much money, and we learned a lot. So, I believe it’s better to mix advice, research, and intuition.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever faced a situation where you had to choose between what was efficient and what was right? What did you do, and why?
motivation_questions.answer: Last autumn, when there was not enough water for all families’ gardens in our village, some people suggested we should just water only our own plot and not help others. It would have been easier and probably safer, because then there would be less arguing or responsibility. But I thought it was wrong to just look out for ourselves, so I helped organize a schedule so each family could take turns using the well. This was more complicated and took extra time because we had to talk to everyone, make a list, and sometimes remind people of their turn. But in the end, more families had enough water, and it kept good relations in the village. I think supporting the community is more important than only efficiency, even though it’s not always convenient.

motivation_questions.question: Is there a problem in your city, country, or the world that genuinely troubles you? Why that one specifically - and what do you think is its root cause?
motivation_questions.answer: In our village and many nearby, water and soil quality are very big problems. This troubles me because it affects everyone — food supply, family incomes, and even if young people stay here or leave. For example, sometimes the crops don’t grow well, or families have to buy bottled water, which is expensive. The root cause is, I think, both bad infrastructure and not enough knowledge on how to adapt to climate changes. Older people often talk about how things used to be better, but now the rivers are lower and the soil is not as rich. Sometimes authorities do not ask locals what is really needed, so solutions don’t always work. I want to be part of solving this through science and local action, even if it’s just small steps.

motivation_questions.question: How has your worldview shifted over the last 2-3 years? What drove that change - a book, a person, an event, an experience?
motivation_questions.answer: I used to think only city people or officials could change things. But after reading articles about farmers in India and Africa making local solutions—even though they didn’t have much—they still figured out ways to improve their communities. That made me question why I thought change only came from the top. Also, seeing how our own compost experiment worked at home, even in a small way, pushed me to think that rural youth in Kazakhstan also have ideas worth trying, and maybe we just don’t notice them enough. Now, I feel more responsibility, not just to complain or wait for someone else to fix things, but to look for solutions together with others here. Sometimes it feels slow, but at least trying is better than nothing.

motivation_questions.question: What do you think Kazakhstan will look like in 20 years - and what role do you believe your generation will play in shaping it?
motivation_questions.answer: I hope Kazakhstan will not be just more developed in cities but will have better life in villages too. My generation will probably need to bring new methods, mix traditional knowledge and science, and show that rural life can be modern, interesting, and valuable. For example, maybe we can introduce new technology in farming or help build better schools and clinics in small towns. I think we will have to solve many problems ourselves, because help from outside is not always fast or reliable. Sometimes, even simple things like internet access or clean water take too long to arrive. We can make rural places more attractive and strong for the next generations if we work together, share our ideas, and don’t give up easily.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: When I suggested rotating water schedule instead of just priority for strongest families, some people were angry — they thought it was unnecessary. Some neighbors even stopped talking to me for a while, and I could feel the tension whenever I walked by. But I stood by my idea because I saw that some old people and families with small children were struggling. I remembered seeing my grandmother wait for hours, and it felt unfair. It was not easy, but I tried to explain my reasons at the community meeting, even if my voice shook. After two weeks, the new system worked, and now more people agree it was better. It was not comfortable, but I wanted fairness for everyone, and I think it was worth it.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: I help my younger sister with her homework almost every night, especially when our parents are tired from work. I do it because I want her to have more chances in life, and I remember how hard it felt for me when I was younger and didn’t have someone to explain things. Sometimes it’s just small things, like checking her math exercises or helping her memorize English words, but I see her get more confident. I also sometimes help other students from nearby grades with math, mostly when they come to me before a test. This experience gives me patience and the feeling that even if you help just a few people, it can matter in the long run. It also made me less shy about sharing what I know, and I realized teaching is not as easy as it looks.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer:
Responsibility — taking care of farm work when my father was sick. I had to get up early and make sure the animals were fed, and the fields were watered, even if I was tired or had homework.  
Fairness — organizing the water schedule so every family had a chance. Sometimes people got upset if they thought someone else was taking more, so I tried to listen and make a fair plan, even if it took more time.  
Curiosity — always looking for new farming techniques and reading about other rural innovations. I follow some blogs and ask questions to older farmers, and sometimes I try small experiments, like planting a new vegetable in our garden. These are not big, but I try to live by them.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: I initiated a school library group to talk about farming innovations. What worked: some students came after lessons, and even a teacher joined to share resources, which helped keep discussions interesting. We talked about things like drip irrigation and composting methods. What did not: after the first month, attendance dropped a lot because it was busy farm season and many students had to help their families in the fields. It was hard to keep people interested when everyone was tired. Next time, I would try to organize meetings during winter, when farm work is less, or maybe find online ways to communicate, like using a group chat or video calls, so more people can join even if they are busy outside school.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: Mostly, I helped my family farm (no salary), but last year I tutored two local kids in math for a small fee. I prepared my own worksheets and explained problems step by step, even when I was tired from chores. At first, they struggled with fractions and basic algebra, so I tried to use real-life examples, like dividing apples or money, to make it more understandable. Both improved and thanked me, and one of them even showed me his improved grades, which made me feel useful. Also, I sold a few kilos of tomatoes from our field at the market, which made me feel proud. It is not big money, but it’s honest and from my efforts. I learned the value of patience and small achievements.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: I would organize a water management workshop for my village, inviting an expert if possible, and buy some basic equipment (pipes, measuring tools) for community experiments. Many families here lack information or resources on how to improve irrigation. This would help everyone save water and get better harvests. I think real value is when the whole village can learn something and share experience for the future, not only improve for one family. I would also try to include some simple demonstrations, like showing how to build a small drip irrigation system from cheap materials, because many people here have never seen these techniques in action. Maybe after the workshop, we could leave the equipment for people to borrow and test in their own gardens. Even if only a few families start using better irrigation, they could show the results to neighbors next year, so the knowledge spreads naturally.

text_inputs.interview_text:
I am not very good at speaking or writing about myself, but I want to become someone who brings knowledge home. I do not wish to leave the village forever — maybe I will work elsewhere to learn, but I want to return and help others. My main strength, I think, is not giving up even when it looks like nothing will change. For example, when our school internet broke down for weeks, I still organized a study group using printed materials, even though it felt pointless at first. My weakness is sometimes I do not ask for help early or share my ideas loud enough. Sometimes I am afraid that what I say is too simple, or that it will not be understood, so I keep it to myself and try to solve things alone.

At inVision U, I hope to learn from people with different backgrounds, and to see how small ideas can become bigger projects. I am especially interested in hearing how students from other countries solve problems in their own communities, and maybe I can share our methods too, even if they are basic. Maybe I can show that even quiet village students have ideas worth trying. I want to become more confident in speaking up, and maybe find new ways to support rural youth like myself. My dream is not only for myself, but for the possibility that rural Kazakhstan can grow stronger from inside, not always waiting for help from big cities. I hope one day I can start a project in my village, maybe a small technology club or library, and inspire younger students to believe they can make changes, too.

scenario_meta.notes: Almaty-based, clear focus on tech and civic action, English fluency, strong critical thinker, highly aware.

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
Dear inVision U Admissions Committee,

My journey as a learner and as a citizen in Almaty has been shaped by both the vibrance and contradictions of my city. Since a young age, I have been fascinated by the intersection of technology and social change—witnessing rapid modernization and digitalization alongside unresolved civic problems like traffic congestion, air pollution, and educational inequality. My drive to contribute to solutions crystallized during secondary school, when my family faced a severe financial crisis after my father lost his job. This difficult period taught me the importance of resilience, adaptability, and working not only for personal advancement, but also for shared goals.

My core experiences revolve around harnessing technology for tangible local impact. The project I am most proud of was developing a web platform that aggregated air quality data and provided actionable suggestions—such as commuting alternatives—directly to users. I initiated this independently, sourcing data from open APIs and designing the interface to be intuitive for all age groups. At first, I struggled to make sense of the different data formats and had to learn basic data cleaning and visualization on my own. I spent many late nights reading online forums and experimenting with different libraries until I got the graphs to display meaningful trends. The feedback from my peers, teachers, and even city officials demonstrated that young people can drive real change by connecting diverse disciplines. One teacher told me that she started using the platform in her classroom to discuss environmental issues, which was a small but meaningful sign that my work mattered. I learned that even a simple tool, if made accessible, can empower people to make better decisions.

Beyond technology, I have been committed to building inclusive communities. I co-founded a mentorship initiative for younger students interested in robotics and programming, especially those from less-resourced schools around Almaty. Our group started with just three volunteers meeting in a borrowed classroom, but eventually grew to over a dozen regular participants. Organizing weekly meetups and workshops not only grew my leadership capacity but also showed me the value of collaboration in overcoming complex obstacles, from resource limitations to cultural bias around technical fields. For example, we sometimes had to improvise with outdated equipment or make do with open-source software when proprietary tools were unavailable. Seeing students who had never coded before complete their first basic robots, and hearing them talk about their future ambitions, was one of the most rewarding experiences I’ve had. It also made me realize how important it is to create opportunities where none exist.

My academic record reflects my curiosity and perseverance, but what excites me most about inVision U is its mission to transcend academic achievement and nurture proactive problem-solvers. I am eager to deepen my skills through interdisciplinary collaboration, to question assumptions, and to co-create projects with peers from diverse backgrounds. I am particularly interested in the hands-on labs and community engagement programs I read about on your website. The idea of working on real-world problems, side by side with people who have different perspectives, appeals to me more than abstract classroom exercises.

What makes me a strong fit for inVision U is my readiness to lead with empathy, to innovate responsibly, and to turn challenges into learning opportunities. I want to learn from faculty and students who, like me, see Kazakhstan’s current issues as sources of inspiration rather than barriers. My broader goal is to help foster a generation of leaders who are equally comfortable with data analysis, creative thinking, and community engagement. I believe inVision U is the ideal platform for actualizing this vision.

Thank you for your consideration.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: The most difficult period was when my father lost his job during my second year of high school. Suddenly, my family’s financial security was gone. My mother had to take on extra work, and I started tutoring younger kids in math to help out. It was a time of anxiety and uncertainty, but also of growth. I learned self-reliance and discovered how to adapt by focusing on what I could control—like my studies and small but meaningful ways to contribute. I also became more empathetic, realizing that many classmates and neighbors faced hidden struggles. This experience motivated me to help create support mechanisms for students in tough situations, ultimately shaping my worldview toward one of collective responsibility.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: I keep a detailed journal where every evening, I spend 15 minutes reflecting on the day—what I learned, what frustrated me, and moments of gratitude. This habit helps me clarify my goals and spot patterns in my thinking. In the mornings, I dedicate 30 minutes to learning something outside my official curriculum (for example, reading articles on urban planning or watching tutorials on open source software). I chose these routines because they help me combine reflection with curiosity. The impact is tangible: I find myself more resilient when setbacks happen, and more creative when facing new problems. These habits give me a sense of ownership over my learning path, which I consider as important as my school grades.

motivation_questions.question: Was there a time when you worked toward a goal for a long time but never reached it? What did you take away from that experience?
motivation_questions.answer: In tenth grade, I wanted to qualify for the national robotics team. I spent months developing a prototype and spent countless weekends working in my school’s lab. Despite all the effort, I didn’t make the final cutoff. Initially, I was disappointed and questioned my abilities. But reflecting later, I realized how much I had learned: debugging complex mechanisms, collaborating closely with peers, and handling pressure. The most valuable lesson was that not all effort guarantees a win, but every attempt contributes to growth. Since then, I approach long-term goals with more focus on the learning process rather than just the final outcome.

motivation_questions.question: Describe a project, idea, or solution you came up with entirely on your own initiative - not as an assignment. Where did the idea come from, and what was the outcome?
motivation_questions.answer: Seeing how hard it was for ordinary citizens to understand Almaty’s worsening air pollution, I initiated a web platform that combined real-time air quality data with actionable personal recommendations (like low-pollution hours to exercise outdoors). The idea came from conversations with my grandmother, who suffers from asthma, and I realized that public data should be more accessible. I sourced open data, taught myself basic web design, and involved a biology teacher for health insights. The project reached over 500 active users in its first month, and I received invitations to present at a local youth forum. It taught me that initiative and empathy can combine for practical solutions.

motivation_questions.question: How do you usually make important decisions - do you rely on intuition, data, or advice from others? Walk us through a specific real example from your life.
motivation_questions.answer: I try to balance data and advice, then trust my intuition to make the final call. For example, deciding whether to take on the leadership role in our robotics club, I listed pros and cons, sought advice from a mentor, and looked at my current workload. Even after careful analysis, I felt a strong internal motivation that this was the challenge I needed. It turned out to be the right choice—I learned managerial skills and gained confidence. This approach helps me feel responsible for my decisions, rather than just following others or being swayed by incomplete information.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever faced a situation where you had to choose between what was efficient and what was right? What did you do, and why?
motivation_questions.answer: During a group project, a teammate suggested using data that was accurate but sourced without proper permission. It would have saved us a lot of time and effort, but I felt uneasy about it. I insisted we find an officially available dataset instead, even though it slowed us down and made the project more complicated. Some teammates were frustrated in the moment, and there were arguments about how strict we should be. Still, I explained that using data without permission could have serious consequences, both ethically and academically. Afterwards, a couple of teammates thanked me for maintaining integrity, saying it made them think more carefully about these issues in future projects. I believe what is right—especially respecting privacy and copyright—outweighs convenience. Upholding these principles builds trust and accountability, which matter more than short-term wins or faster results.

motivation_questions.question: Is there a problem in your city, country, or the world that genuinely troubles you? Why that one specifically - and what do you think is its root cause?
motivation_questions.answer: Air pollution in Almaty troubles me profoundly because it affects everyone, regardless of background or status. For instance, I’ve seen firsthand how my older relatives suffer health consequences, especially during the winter when the smog is thick and people are advised not to open their windows. The root causes are complex: outdated transportation, weak enforcement of regulations, reliance on coal heating, and lack of accessible public information about air quality. Many people feel powerless because they don’t see how their individual choices can matter, and sometimes they aren’t even aware of the impact. I think raising awareness and pressuring policymakers for greener infrastructure is crucial; but also, there’s a need for community-scale solutions that empower citizens directly. Even small steps, like neighborhood tree-planting or sharing air quality updates, could help people feel less helpless.

motivation_questions.question: How has your worldview shifted over the last 2-3 years? What drove that change - a book, a person, an event, an experience?
motivation_questions.answer: My worldview shifted after reading Yuval Noah Harari’s books and through volunteering with a youth activism group. I used to see problems as either personal or too big to touch. But those experiences taught me how interconnected everything is: history, technology, climate, policy. For example, when I read about how agricultural changes affected societies, or when my group discussed how local recycling habits connect to global pollution, I started seeing links everywhere. It’s no longer enough for me to just do well individually; I feel compelled to contribute to collective progress, even if it’s in small ways like organizing clean-up events or having difficult conversations with family members. This realization has made me more patient, less judgmental, and more committed to learning from diverse perspectives, even when it feels uncomfortable.

motivation_questions.question: What do you think Kazakhstan will look like in 20 years - and what role do you believe your generation will play in shaping it?
motivation_questions.answer: In 20 years, I hope Kazakhstan is known for sustainable urban centers, transparent governance, and vibrant civic spaces. My generation will shape that future by harnessing technology but staying rooted in empathy and responsibility. I believe we must challenge the status quo, advocate for ethical reforms, and stay open to global collaboration while preserving our unique identity. For example, I imagine more young people launching local initiatives or participating in environmental projects, which could slowly change attitudes in our communities. It might not be easy because there is resistance to change, but even small steps, like volunteering or creating discussion groups, can matter. If we do this, Kazakhstan will be a leader in Central Asia for both innovation and social justice.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: In a school debate, my assigned team unanimously agreed on one approach, but I felt the opposing view deserved representation. I stood up in front of the teachers and my friends to present the counter-argument. Initially, there was resistance—even some ridicule. But my belief in the value of open dialogue drove me. It turned out that my stance encouraged others to reconsider their assumptions, and we ended up having a more honest discussion. That moment reminded me of the value in standing up for thoughtful disagreement, even if it’s unpopular.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: Last summer, I volunteered to organize digital literacy sessions for elderly neighbors who struggled with using smartphones during the pandemic. I wanted them to stay connected to family, access health information, and avoid isolation. Sometimes, even simple things like teaching someone to send a photo or join a video call took a while, but I didn’t mind repeating instructions. Seeing their confidence grow was incredibly rewarding, especially when one woman told me she could now video chat with her grandchildren in another city. The experience helped me develop patience, appreciate intergenerational learning, and reinforced my commitment to making technology accessible for all demographics. It felt like making a tangible difference in everyday life, and I still sometimes help them when they have questions.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer:
Empathy—When my classmate’s mother was sick, I organized a rotating group to deliver notes and homework so she wouldn’t fall behind, making sure she didn’t feel alone. I also checked in with her regularly, just to listen, which I think helped her feel supported. It made me realize how much small actions can mean when someone is struggling.

Integrity—Refusing to use improperly sourced data for a group project, even when pressured, and insisting on transparency. It felt uncomfortable to go against the group, but I knew it was the right thing. We ended up getting a lower grade, but I learned that my values shouldn’t depend on convenience or results.

Curiosity—Starting my morning self-study routine, which allowed me to explore new domains like urban planning and design outside my standard curriculum. I tried online courses and read articles in topics I’d never considered before, like public spaces and city layouts, just because I wanted to understand how people shape their environments.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: I co-founded a robotics mentorship initiative for students from under-resourced schools. What worked was personalized, hands-on activities and ongoing support, which kept participants engaged. For example, we let students build simple robots, and some even brought their own creative ideas, which made sessions lively. What didn’t work was the initial lack of structured feedback; we didn’t check in often enough, so some students drifted away without us noticing at first. Sometimes we assumed everyone was keeping up, but later realized a few were confused or falling behind. If I could do it again, I’d establish a schedule for checking progress, gather more feedback early, and train additional mentors to ensure every participant feels seen and supported, not just the most outspoken ones.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: I started tutoring secondary school students in math and basic coding after school. At first, I posted flyers in my neighborhood and offered lessons at a low cost to attract students—especially those whose parents couldn’t afford full-price private tutors. Some parents called to ask about my background and approach, and I explained that I was a student myself and would adjust my teaching to each child. I designed interactive worksheets adjusted for their skill levels, which brought positive feedback and more referrals from families who appreciated my effort. I sometimes met students in person at their homes, and sometimes we did lessons over WhatsApp when meeting was difficult. The results were tangible: improved confidence and grades for my students, and for me, a sense of financial independence and experience in tailoring solutions to people’s needs. This experience also taught me patience when students struggled, and how to explain concepts in different ways until they understood.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: I would use the funds to design and launch a mobile-friendly web tool that helps citizens monitor air quality and receive health guidance in real time—especially targeted for at-risk groups like the elderly and children. I’d use part of the budget for translation and accessibility features, and another part for outreach workshops in local schools and community centers. This would create lasting value by raising awareness, promoting healthier behaviors, and empowering community action. Clean air is a basic right, and I want to make sure tech solutions are accessible to everyone, not just the tech-savvy.

text_inputs.interview_text:
The reason I am especially eager to join inVision U is that it seems to offer both the structure and freedom to pursue meaningful interdisciplinary learning with practical community impact. I have always felt that Kazakhstan needs more youth leaders who are comfortable crossing boundaries—between engineering, social sciences, and creative industries. InVision U’s project-driven curriculum stands out to me because it simulates the complexity of real-world challenges. In Almaty, I have noticed that solutions rarely come from one discipline alone. For example, my air quality project wouldn’t have been effective without inputs from biology, software, and community feedback.

What makes me unique among applicants is my blend of technical capability with empathy and an ability to communicate across generations and social backgrounds. I believe inVision U will allow me to expand these skills by working closely with peers who have equally ambitious but different perspectives.
