Installing the operating system was a bit tricky because of driver issues, so I spent a lot of time on forums trying to solve the problems. After a few days, both laptops were running well enough to use for basics like browsing and word processing. I listed them on a local website and managed to sell each for a small profit. I didn’t make much, but it was enough to buy some screwdrivers and a soldering iron for future repairs. The main value for me was learning how to figure things out step by step, and it felt good to actually finish something useful.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: Я купил бы солнечные панели и попробовал установить их на крыше соседнего дома, чтобы проверить, сможет ли дом получить часть энергии автономно. Это дало бы реальный опыт в практике и, возможно, вдохновило бы других жителей искать новые решения для энергосбережения. Я думаю, что даже если сумма небольшая, можно начать с одной-двух панелей и посмотреть, как работает система в реальных условиях нашего климата. Может быть, я бы пригласил соседей посмотреть на процесс установки, объяснил бы им, как всё устроено. Мне кажется, это бы показало, что такие проекты реально осуществимы, даже если они небольшие. А ещё я бы написал короткую инструкцию или снял видео о том, что получилось, чтобы поделиться опытом с другими.

text_inputs.interview_text:
Honestly, I am not very good at interviews or talking about myself. I prefer to show what I can do with real projects or technical things. That is why I think inVision U suits me. I want to be with people who also work on solving practical problems, not only those who are best at writing or public speaking. I feel more comfortable when I am actually working on something, like building a small device, fixing an old laptop, or helping my younger cousins with their math homework. These activities are more natural for me than speaking in front of a group or explaining my achievements.

Sometimes I hesitate to apply to universities because I think maybe I am not smart enough or not ready. But my friends and family say I should try because I have a good mind for fixing things and for coming up with solutions in our everyday life. For example, when the power went out in our neighborhood last winter, I figured out how to set up a simple battery-powered light system for our house. I learned a lot on my own, because not all teachers in our school could help with technical hobbies. Often I ask questions in online forums, mostly in English, which helped me improve my language. It was not easy at first, but slowly I got better at understanding technical explanations and even answering some questions myself.

If I am admitted, I hope to use what I learn to help my area, not just for myself. I will be happy to help other students with hands-on, technical projects, even if sometimes I am not the one leading the presentation. I care more about building something real than looking impressive. I believe that by working together and sharing practical skills, we can solve many everyday problems, and I look forward to contributing in this way.

scenario_meta.notes: Candidate from Atyrau; initially struggled academically, but shows significant trajectory in self-organization and local impact; high self-awareness, narrative is clean and personal, moderate confidence.

content_profile.essay_style: reflective, moderately polished, some emotional touches
content_profile.impact_pattern: directed at school & local environmental awareness

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
Меня зовут Дарья, мне 17 лет, я родилась и учусь в Атырау — на стыке Каспийского региона, где особенно остро ощущаются все экологические проблемы Казахстана. Здесь часто говорят о загрязнении нефтью, о проблемах с чистой водой, и даже простая прогулка по набережной напоминает о том, как важно сохранять окружающую среду. Всё это сильно повлияло на мои взгляды и интересы.

До 9 класса я не отличалась особой организованностью: постоянно опаздывала, забывала сдавать работы. Из-за этого оценки были средние, а учителя относились скептически. Иногда я чувствовала, что мне просто не хватает внутренней дисциплины, и это мешало мне раскрыть свой потенциал. Но во время пандемии я впервые по-настоящему задумалась о смысле своей учёбы и о том, почему многие мои сверстники опускают руки. Мы сидели дома, занятия были онлайн, и стало особенно заметно, что многим ребятам не хватает мотивации — мы просто не видим реальный смысл теории. Я тоже себя так ощущала: зачем мне эти формулы, если я не понимаю, как они могут пригодиться за пределами школы?

Я хотела изменить это сначала для себя и подруг, а потом и для других ребят в классе. Начала с привычки заранее записывать задачи на неделю вперед — оказалось, что даже простое планирование помогает осознанности. Например, я стала тратить меньше времени впустую, а когда видишь выполненные пункты в списке, появляется чувство удовлетворения. Позже я предложила друзьям вместе читать статьи про Каспий и нефтяное загрязнение, обсуждать, как мы можем повлиять на ситуацию хотя бы в пределах школы. Иногда это были просто короткие заметки из новостей, иногда — научные публикации, которые мы пытались разобрать вместе, даже если не всё понимали. Были споры, кто-то говорил, что «ничего не изменится», но мне казалось важным хотя бы обсуждать такие вопросы. С этого момента начался мой рост: я стала увереннее говорить о проблемах, не боялась высказывать мнение, и даже учителя заметили, что я стала проявлять больше инициативы.

Сейчас я выступаю за раздельный сбор мусора в школе, участвую в обсуждении локальных городских инициатив. Я не всегда знаю, как правильно организовать процесс, иногда сталкиваюсь с непониманием, но всё равно продолжаю, потому что считаю это важным. Моё самое гордое достижение — организация мини-проекта "Чистый двор" с одноклассниками: мы собирали отходы и делали стенгазету о важности заботы о природе родного региона. Было сложно договориться, чтобы все пришли на уборку, кто-то из одноклассников сначала смеялся, но потом всё-таки присоединились. Могло быть больше охвата, но получилось вовлечь не только детей, но и нескольких родителей. Одна мама даже предложила сделать такую акцию ежегодной. Это показало мне, что маленькие шаги могут менять отношение людей.

inVision U кажется мне уникальным местом, где ценится не фактическое положение "отличника", а желание решать реальные задачи. Для меня важно, что здесь поддерживают инициативу и стремление к изменениям, а не только академические успехи. Я хочу расти дальше: учиться у сверстников из разных городов, узнать больше о современных подходах к экологии, может быть — инициировать проект по устойчивому развитию собственного района. Мне бы хотелось попробовать реализовать идеи по экопросвещению среди молодёжи или сотрудничать с местными организациями. Я не всегда уверена в своих силах, но мне интересно пробовать, ошибаться, и искать единомышленников.

Мне важно быть частью среды, где не боятся меняться и помогать меняться другим. Я верю, что даже небольшие усилия, если их поддерживает команда, могут привести к реальным переменам. Надеюсь, что смогу принести пользу своему городу и найти новых единомышленников в inVision U.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: Самым трудным для меня был переходный период после 9 класса: я оказалась на грани отчисления из-за низких оценок и плохой посещаемости. Сначала было стыдно, не хотелось говорить с родителями, пряталась от учителей. Я часто пропускала занятия, просто не могла собраться с мыслями, казалось, что ничего не получится изменить. В какой-то момент подруга помогла мне начать составлять расписание на неделю, и этим простым методом получилось вернуться к учебе. Иногда мы вместе делали домашние задания, и это тоже помогало. Я пересмотрела своё отношение к ошибкам и стала более уверенной в себе. Сейчас уже я сама помогаю школьникам, которые оказались в похожей ситуации, например, объясняю им, как планировать время и не бояться просить помощи.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: С 10 класса я каждый вечер записываю 3 главные задачи — не только по школе, но и личные мелочи, например, написать другу или разобрать папки на компьютере. Иногда это даже что-то вроде «прочитать 10 страниц книги». Утром стараюсь их перечитать и сразу сделать самое неприятное, чтобы не оставлять до вечера. Например, если среди задач есть подготовка к контрольной, начинаю именно с неё, хотя хочется отложить. Такая структура помогает справляться с прокрастинацией и уменьшает тревожность — когда вижу, что хотя бы эти три вещи сделаны, ощущаю прогресс. Я выбрала этот подход, потому что без него мои дни проходили хаотично и задачи накапливались, и в итоге чувствовала себя уставшей и недовольной собой.

motivation_questions.question: Was there a time when you worked toward a goal for a long time but never reached it? What did you take away from that experience?
motivation_questions.answer: Я несколько лет пыталась участвовать в олимпиадах по биологии, но так и не прошла дальше городского этапа. Каждый раз я готовилась по учебникам, решала задания прошлых лет, даже ходила на дополнительные занятия, но всё равно не удавалось пройти дальше. Сначала очень переживала и считала себя неумной, особенно когда видела, как мои одноклассники проходят дальше или получают грамоты. Было чувство, что все усилия напрасны. Но потом поняла, что могу реализовать знания иначе — например, рассказывать о науке младшим школьникам через интерактивные занятия. Я начала помогать учителю по биологии организовывать кружок, придумывала простые опыты и объясняла ребятам, зачем нужна наука в жизни. Теперь получаю больше радости от участия, чем от призовых мест, потому что вижу, что могу быть полезной и интересной для других, даже если сама не выигрываю.

motivation_questions.question: Describe a project, idea, or solution you came up with entirely on your own initiative - not as an assignment. Where did the idea come from, and what was the outcome?
motivation_questions.answer: Я придумала создать стенгазету ко Дню Каспия, чтобы рассказать ученикам о ситуации с загрязнением и исчезновении рыб. Идея появилась, когда я увидела, что большинство просто проходит мимо мусора у школы, как будто это что-то обычное. Меня это задело, и я решила, что нужно что-то сделать, чтобы привлечь внимание к проблеме. Мы с ребятами сделали яркие фотографии пластиковых бутылок и пакетов рядом с территорией школы, нарисовали карту загрязнения Каспия, где показали, какие участки страдают больше всего. Также мы провели опрос среди учеников о том, что они думают об экологии и знают ли они о рыбах Каспия. После этого учителя стали чаще говорить о сортировке мусора на классных часах и даже предлагать идеи для уборки территории. Для меня этот проект стал первым опытом организации чего-то важного своими силами.

motivation_questions.question: How do you usually make important decisions - do you rely on intuition, data, or advice from others? Walk us through a specific real example from your life.
motivation_questions.answer: Я стараюсь собрать как можно больше данных, а потом обсуждаю с мамой или подругой. Например, когда поступал вопрос об участии в летнем лагере по экологии: сначала собрала информацию о программе, почитала отзывы, написала организаторам вопросы. Иногда даже смотрю фотографии прошлых лет и ищу мнения в социальных сетях. Мама обычно задаёт мне дополнительные вопросы, на которые я сама не обратила бы внимания, а подруга помогает оценить, насколько мне это действительно интересно. Только после обсуждения решила поехать. Такой подход помогает снизить тревожность и сделать выбор более уверенно. Хотя иногда это занимает больше времени, мне важно быть уверенной, что приняла верное для себя решение.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever faced a situation where you had to choose between what was efficient and what was right? What did you do, and why?
motivation_questions.answer: Да, когда мы готовили экологическую акцию, учитель предлагал просто купить готовые плакаты, чтобы сэкономить время. Но мне казалось, что важнее сделать их своими руками и привлечь больше учеников. Мы выбрали дольше, но в итоге больше ребят заинтересовались и остались довольны результатом. Для меня было важно не просто выполнить задание, а вовлечь других. Я помню, как мы собирались после уроков, обсуждали, какие лозунги написать, кто будет рисовать, кто вырезать буквы. Было немного хаотично, кто-то уставал, но в процессе многие начали предлагать свои идеи. Это заняло почти неделю вместо одного дня, но наши плакаты были ярче и уникальнее. Я понял, что иногда правильнее выбрать путь, который объединяет людей, даже если это менее эффективно по времени.

motivation_questions.question: Is there a problem in your city, country, or the world that genuinely troubles you? Why that one specifically - and what do you think is its root cause?
motivation_questions.answer: Меня беспокоит проблема загрязнения Каспийского моря и невнимания к вопросам экологии в нашем регионе. Корень — в недостаточной информированности людей и отсутствии контроля со стороны предприятий. Например, многие рыбаки и жители знают, что вода становится грязнее, но не знают, почему это опасно для них самих и будущих поколений. Предприятия тоже часто не соблюдают экологические нормы, потому что штрафы низкие или проверок почти нет. Думаю, если бы больше школьников и родителей знали о последствиях, отношение бы менялось. Например, на уроках биологии почти не обсуждают реальные экологические проблемы нашего региона. Если бы об этом говорили чаще и приводили конкретные примеры, люди стали бы внимательнее относиться к природе.

motivation_questions.question: How has your worldview shifted over the last 2-3 years? What drove that change - a book, a person, an event, an experience?
motivation_questions.answer: Сильно повлияла поездка на экскурсию на нефтяной завод: увидела весь цикл и последствия для природы. Было неожиданно увидеть, как много отходов и выбросов производится, и как это сказывается на окружающей среде и людях вокруг. После этого стала искать способы применения знаний об экологии на практике, а не просто изучать теорию. Например, вместе с друзьями мы начали сортировать мусор в школе и обсуждать, как сократить использование пластика. Начала ценить командную работу — раньше думала, что важнее только свои усилия, но теперь вижу, что даже маленькие действия в группе могут начать перемены. Этот опыт заставил меня задуматься о повседневных привычках и о том, что ответственность за природу — это не только для специалистов.

motivation_questions.question: What do you think Kazakhstan will look like in 20 years - and what role do you believe your generation will play in shaping it?
motivation_questions.answer: Я верю, что Казахстан станет более осознанным в вопросах экологии и устойчивого развития. Моё поколение — те, кто уже готов дискутировать, не боится новых подходов и умеет работать вместе. Думаю, если мы начнём с маленьких шагов — раздельный сбор, энергоэффективность — через 20 лет это станет нормой для всех. Например, сейчас среди моих знакомых уже обсуждают, как уменьшить использование пластика или экономить воду, хотя ещё недавно такие темы казались странными. Я думаю, что в будущем будет больше инициатив, связанных с зелёными технологиями, и, возможно, даже маленькие города будут внедрять солнечные панели или велосипеды для общественного транспорта. Моя надежда — что мы не потеряем этот интерес и научимся влиять на решения на более высоком уровне, чтобы Казахстан стал страной, где устойчивое развитие — это не просто слова.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: На одном классном собрании я предложила отказаться от пластиковых стаканчиков на школьных праздниках. Многие сначала смеялись и говорили — это мелочи, что это ничего не изменит и никому не нужно. Некоторым даже показалось, что я зря трачу время и усложняю подготовку. Но я настояла, объяснила аргументы: пластик загрязняет природу, мы сами можем начать с чего-то малого. Я привела пример, как дома мы уже используем многоразовые вещи, и это не так сложно, как кажется. В итоге решили попробовать многоразовые кружки. Это был маленький шаг, но я гордилась, что не побоялась высказать непопулярное мнение и увидела, что иногда перемены начинаются с простых вещей, даже если сначала никто не поддерживает.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: Я помогала однокласснику, у которого были трудности с русским языком. Просто объясняла темы после уроков, не ради галочки — мы садились за парту, я разбирала с ним домашку, иногда рисовала схемы, если он что-то не понимал. Не всегда получалось легко, иногда приходилось повторять одно и то же несколько раз или искать новые примеры. Через пару месяцев он стал увереннее, даже начал участвовать в обсуждениях на уроке, а однажды сам объяснил что-то другим. Мне это дало ощущение, что я могу менять чужую жизнь к лучшему, даже без официальной позиции наставника. Поняла, что поддержка — это не всегда что-то большое, и она может заметно повлиять на человека.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer: Мои ценности: ответственность (беру на себя задачи по организации проектов — например, когда в школе нужно было подготовить мероприятие, я согласилась быть координатором, хотя переживала, справлюсь ли), уважение (всегда слушаю чужое мнение, даже если не согласна — например, в группе по биологии у нас часто были споры, я старалась не перебивать и учитывать аргументы других, даже если мне казалось, что я права), развитие (постоянно учусь и пробую новое, даже если страшно, как в истории с олимпиадами — сначала я боялась участвовать, потому что думала, что не получится, но всё равно попробовала, и это дало опыт и уверенность продолжать дальше).

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: Я с одноклассниками провела акцию по уборке школьного двора: привлекла 15 человек, сделали газету. Получилось организовать, но плохо продумали информирование — пришли только те, кого знала лично. Мы развесили пару объявлений в коридоре и рассказали в нашем чате, но оказалось, что многие одноклассники просто не увидели сообщения или не поняли, насколько это важно. Газету мы сделали сами, без помощи учителей, и она получилась немного неаккуратной, но нам всё равно было интересно попробовать. Если бы я повторяла акцию, я бы заранее обратилась к классным руководителям, чтобы они рассказали о мероприятии на уроках, а ещё, возможно, написала бы пост в школьной группе и попросила бы других ребят помочь с подготовкой. Думаю, тогда участников было бы больше, и уборка прошла бы веселее.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: Я помогала вести соцсети знакомой мамы, у которой небольшой магазин косметики. Мы вместе учились делать хорошие фотографии товаров, пробовали разные ракурсы и освещение, иногда даже придумывали необычные подписи к постам. Я выкладывала посты, следила за комментариями и иногда отвечала клиентам, если они задавали вопросы про продукты. Это было не всегда просто — иногда не знала, что написать, или боялась ошибиться в ответе, но со временем стало легче. Заработала немного денег, но главное — почувствовала, что могу создавать для других ценность, а не только для себя. Было приятно видеть, когда после моего поста кто-то делал заказ, и понимать, что мой вклад реально помогает.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: Я бы провела серию мастер-классов по раздельному сбору мусора для детей и родителей в Атырау. На выделенные $1,000 я бы закупила контейнеры для разных видов отходов — пластика, стекла, бумаги — и разместила бы их в школах или дворах. Также я бы подготовила простые брошюры с картинками и основными советами, чтобы даже маленькие дети могли понять, что и куда выбрасывать. Пригласила бы местных специалистов, которые уже занимаются экологией, чтобы они поделились своим опытом и рассказали реальные истории. Это помогло бы формировать полезные привычки у самых младших, а через них — у их семей. Возможно, кто-то из родителей впервые задумается о том, сколько мусора можно перерабатывать. Думаю, что даже небольшой шаг — научить детей сортировать отходы — может постепенно изменить отношение к экологии в нашем городе.

text_inputs.interview_text:
Мне кажется, поступая в inVision U, я смогу выйти за рамки узкой школьной программы и начать действовать более масштабно. Я уже убедилась на собственном опыте, что привычка к планированию, даже простая привычка делать каждый день маленькие шаги, способна полностью изменить отношение к учёбе и жизни. Например, когда я решила улучшить свою успеваемость по химии, я просто завела блокнот, где отмечала маленькие ежедневные задачи. Через месяц я заметила, что стала увереннее отвечать на уроках, и моя оценка повысилась. Это был не быстрый результат, но он показал, что даже небольшие изменения в подходе могут привести к успеху.

В моём городе ощущается нехватка инициатив «снизу»: подростки редко верят, что от них что-то зависит. Но как только ты пробуешь — даже просто создать газету или собрать людей на уборку — появляется ощущение, что ты не один и реально можешь повлиять на окружающих. Недавно я попробовала организовать небольшой книжный клуб для своих друзей, и хотя нас было всего пятеро, это помогло мне понять, как важно уметь вдохновлять и объединять людей вокруг идеи. Я бы хотела научиться у других ребят эффективнее организовывать проекты, лучше убеждать людей, искать поддержку у взрослых.

Мне не хватает коммуникационных навыков, я стесняюсь выступать перед большой аудиторией, но мне хочется научиться этим вещам, чтобы мои идеи звучали громче. Я верю, что ваш университет поможет не только придумать проект, но и реализовать его так, чтобы он приносил реальную пользу — не только школе, но и целому городу. Я хочу научиться брать на себя ответственность за перемены, пусть даже маленькие, и видеть, как они постепенно становятся чем-то большим.

scenario_meta.notes: Candidate from rural settlement outside Pavlodar; limited extracurriculars, underwhelming academic record; unconfident, scattered, low detail; essay and answers are hesitant and somewhat repetitive.

content_profile.adversity_pattern: chronic low confidence, no standout event

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
My name is Yerlan. I live in a small village not far from Pavlodar. I want to study in inVision U because I think maybe this is my chance to do something good and be better. I have not been best student, and truthfully, I don't really know what I will do, but I want to try.

In my school we do not have much. The classes are not big, and most people, including me, just try to finish with normal grades and help at home. Sometimes I help my uncle with work in the fields. I am not very social, and I do not think I have leadership. But I sometimes think a lot about how the village is not changing at all, and new things do not come. I wanted to do more in my life, but everything seems far.

I think a lot about how my life could be different if I learn something new. Most of my classmates plan to stay or go to Pavlodar city, but I do not know. I want to see maybe there are other things possible. Sometimes I feel afraid because it is not easy to leave everything you know, but I also feel excited when I think of seeing a new place or meeting people from outside my village. At home, we do not talk much about going far away, and sometimes my family is not sure if it is good. But I want to try because I do not want to regret not trying.

I tried to read about inVision U and from what I understood (maybe not everything is clear for me), it is about finding solutions to real things and working in teams. I do not have experience with that, but I want to try. At school, we do not really do projects or teamwork. Usually, we just do what the teacher says and then go home. But sometimes, when we have to clean the classroom or organize a small event, I try to do my part. Once, I helped my classmate with math homework because he was struggling, and we both got better marks after working together. It was a small thing, but it made me think that maybe I can do more if I am in the right place.

I am not sure what my domain will be. I am ready to learn if I get the chance. I do not have achievements but I always try to help my family when is difficult. For example, when my mother was sick last winter, I woke up early to get water from the well and looked after my younger brother. It was hard, but I think it made me more responsible. Sometimes I feel it is not enough just to help at home, and I want to learn how to help in bigger ways.

I hope to have new experience and meet other people who can teach me something. I do not promise big things but I want to work and maybe find my way. I think inVision U might be the place for this. Even if I am not the most confident or the best student, I want to try because I feel if I do not try now, maybe I never will. Thank you.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: I think my life was not very easy, but there was not a big event, only many small things happening over time. For example, in winter sometimes we did not have good heating, and it was very cold inside the house. My parents both work a lot, so I have to do some things in the house, like cooking food sometimes or going to the shop for groceries, even when I am tired after school. It was not so hard maybe like for others, but sometimes I feel alone, especially when I do things and nobody notices or says thank you. After these experiences, I started to feel more strong inside, even if nobody says thank you or sees my effort. I learned I can handle small difficulties and just keep going every day.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: My habit is to wake early, because my uncle always said that is important in village. I wake before 7 every day and help with some work, sometimes bring water, sometimes prepare firewood. It makes me feel helpful at least. I think these habits help me not to be lazy. Sometimes, especially in winter, it is difficult to get up, but I still try because I remember my uncle’s words. Even if I am tired from school, I do these things, and I think it teaches me discipline. I do not have other special routines, because school and house is most of my time. Maybe in future I can add something else, but for now this is what I do.

motivation_questions.question: Was there a time when you worked toward a goal for a long time but never reached it? What did you take away from that experience?
motivation_questions.answer: Yes, I wanted to pass the school exams well so I can get a city scholarship. I studied hard for few months, but my scores were not enough. I was very disappointed because I really wanted to help my family with the scholarship money and prove I could do it. I learned that not always hard work is enough, sometimes you need other resources or better teaching, or maybe just more time. Some of my classmates had tutors, or extra classes, but I could not afford that. After the results, I felt lost for a while, but I did not give up completely, I just accepted maybe my way will be different. I still try to find new opportunities, even if they are not exactly what I first wanted.

motivation_questions.question: Describe a project, idea, or solution you came up with entirely on your own initiative - not as an assignment. Where did the idea come from, and what was the outcome?
motivation_questions.answer: I do not have a big project. Once, when water was cut in the village for two days, I tried to organize with my friends to bring buckets from the well for old neighbors. It was not my idea only, but I started asking them to help. At first, some friends were not sure, but I convinced them by saying our grandparents would also need help if they were alone. We took turns carrying the heavy buckets because it was quite far to the houses, and it took almost the whole afternoon. In the end we brought to three houses. I think it made some small difference and old people smiled to us. It was tiring, but I felt glad we did something for them.

motivation_questions.question: How do you usually make important decisions - do you rely on intuition, data, or advice from others? Walk us through a specific real example from your life.
motivation_questions.answer: I usually ask my mother or sometimes my uncle if I do not know what to do. When I thought to go to Pavlodar college or try for university, I talked a long time to them. They said try university if you want. It was hard to decide because nobody in my family goes to university. My mother was worried about if I would be lonely or if it would be too hard for me, but she also said maybe it is good to try something new. My uncle asked me about what I want to do in future. I did not have a clear answer, but I realized I wanted to see what university is like. Finally, I decided to try for inVision U even if I am not sure. I just thought maybe why not to try. I think I use advice from family most, and sometimes just decide by feeling if there is no clear answer.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever faced a situation where you had to choose between what was efficient and what was right? What did you do, and why?
motivation_questions.answer: One time at school, my classmate wanted to copy homeworks from me so she does not get bad marks. I know it was faster to just give her, but I did not want teachers to think I cheat, so I did not give my work. She was not happy, but I thought it is better to be fair. It was awkward because she was my friend, and I didn’t want to upset her. I tried to explain that if I helped her like this, maybe both of us could get in trouble. Also, I think if I just gave her answers, it would not help her learn. Even though it was uncomfortable, I still feel it was the right thing to do, even if it was not the easiest.

motivation_questions.question: Is there a problem in your city, country, or the world that genuinely troubles you? Why that one specifically - and what do you think is its root cause?
motivation_questions.answer: I think many people in village drink too much and do not try to go forward in life. This is sad because young people also lose motivation. I think cause is no opportunities and not enough work. If there was more for youth, maybe it would be better. For example, in my village, I see older men sitting outside the shop every day, drinking and talking, but not working. My cousin, who is only seventeen, also says he feels bored and sometimes skips school because he does not believe it will change anything for him. There are not enough clubs or jobs or things to do, so people just follow what others do. I think if there were more activities or some jobs, maybe people would have more hope.

motivation_questions.question: How has your worldview shifted over the last 2-3 years? What drove that change - a book, a person, an event, an experience?
motivation_questions.answer: I think before I did not believe I can change anything. I felt like maybe things just happen and you just follow along. Now after finishing school and doing more things in house, like helping my family with small chores or trying to plan my own schedule, I start to think maybe it is possible to learn and get better, but it is slow. I see this after watching some documentaries online about young people making something in big cities. For example, there was a film about a girl who started a small business from her apartment, and it surprised me. It made me realize you don’t always need to wait for someone else to tell you what to do. I still don’t feel confident, but my thinking is not the same.

motivation_questions.question: What do you think Kazakhstan will look like in 20 years - and what role do you believe your generation will play in shaping it?
motivation_questions.answer: Maybe after 20 years, life will be easier and villages will have more technology. I hope young people from villages will become more educated and not just go to city. I think it would be good if people could stay close to their families instead of moving far away for work or university. I do not know what is my role, but maybe I can help my family more. Maybe I can try to bring something new if I learn. For example, if I study something useful, I can share it with my younger relatives or neighbors. If I see a solution for a problem in my village, I want to try and talk to others about it, even if I’m not sure how.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: One time my friends wanted to skip school and go fishing at the river. They were excited and tried to convince me, but I said I want to go to class instead. They laughed and called me boring, and one of them even said, “You always follow the rules too much.” But my parents always say education is important, and I also felt nervous about missing lessons, so I decided to go to school. It was not easy because I worried they would be angry. After that, sometimes they did not invite me to things, and I felt left out a bit, but I did not change my mind. I thought maybe in future they will understand why I chose this.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: I try to help my grandmother often. She is old so I bring water, chop wood for her when she asks. Sometimes I also help her with groceries or just sit and talk with her when she feels lonely. I know it is not very special but I do it because I want her to be happy. I remember once she wanted to fix a broken chair and I tried to help, even though we didn’t really manage to fix it, but she smiled and said it was okay. It gives me feeling I am useful, even if nobody else notices. I think these small things matter, and I feel closer to her every time. It also reminds me to be patient and to care for others.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer: My values I think are honesty (for example, once my classmate wanted to copy my answers during an exam, but I refused to help cheat, even though it felt awkward), hard work (like when I spent the summer working in the fields with my uncle, waking up early every day, doing physical labor, and not complaining even when I was tired), and respect (I always try to listen to adults, like my grandparents or teachers, and do what my family asks me, even small things like helping my younger sister with homework or chores). These things are just normal for my life, not something I think about much, but I guess they show what kind of person I try to be.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: I did not organize big things. Maybe only the water buckets for neighbors. It was not big, but we helped three houses. We noticed some families were struggling with water when the pipe broke in our street. I asked my friends if they could help carry water from the store and we brought buckets together. What worked is that I asked my friends directly, not waited for teacher. They said yes because it was quick and we knew each other. But not everyone in the neighborhood knew what we were doing, so maybe more people could have helped if I explained more or made a small plan. If I do again, maybe I would ask more people or tell more neighbors.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: I sometimes get paid for helping neighbors with carrying things or cleaning the yard. For example, I helped an old woman carry groceries to her apartment when the elevator was broken, and she gave me a little money. Last summer I helped an old man to collect potatoes in his garden, and he gave me some money and potatoes too. Sometimes I also water plants or help to shovel snow in winter. It is not much, but it helped the family. My mother was happy because we could cook with the potatoes, and I could buy some school supplies with the money. I feel good when I can do something useful. It makes me feel part of the community.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: I would fix the village playground, because most things are old and dangerous. With $1,000 I could buy paint and wood and maybe hire someone to fix broken slides. The swings are also rusted and sometimes the ropes break, so I would replace those too. Maybe I could talk to some parents or teachers to help clean up, or plant some trees so there would be more shade in summer. The children would be safe and have more fun. When I was younger I remember falling because the ladder was loose, so I know it matters. That is more important than earning money. I think if the playground is nice, people will take better care of it, and it could help bring the village together.

text_inputs.interview_text:
I do not have experience with interviews. I am shy to speak out. I do not know really what to say, but I hope to try new things and maybe learn teamwork or find my strength if I am in inVision U. Sometimes in class I have ideas but I am afraid to raise my hand, and I often just listen instead of talking. My teachers say I should participate more, but it is difficult for me. I think if I can be part of group projects or discussions at inVision U, it will help me slowly become more confident and not worry so much about making mistakes.

Many times I feel I am left behind because I do not have big achievements like others. Sometimes I feel not good enough, but I want to change. I want to meet students from other places, maybe city or other countries, and learn from them. I think if I hear their stories, maybe I will find something in common or learn new ways to see my own life. In my village, most people don’t go far to study, so I do not have many examples. I do not want to only work in field all my life. My dream is just to be more useful and help my family and maybe my village if possible. My parents always say education is important, but sometimes it is hard to believe I can do more.

I am not sure of my future direction, but I hope if I study more, something will be clear. If I am accepted, I will try not to disappoint. Sorry if my English is not very good, I will work to improve. I hope I can learn different skills, maybe computers or languages, and find some purpose. Even if I struggle, I want to try.

scenario_meta.notes: Candidate is from Taraz, demonstrates average grades but considerable self-driven leadership, acts on local community needs.

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
Dear inVision U Admissions Committee,

I’m writing from Taraz, a city that is not usually in the spotlight when it comes to educational opportunities, but it’s where I learned how challenging and exciting it can be to try to make an impact with limited resources. My academics have always been steady but not extraordinary. But I’ve realized in the past years that making a difference isn’t just about grades—it often means acting when you see something wrong or missing.

Last year I noticed how frustrated younger kids in my neighborhood were with homework and lacked anyone helping them at home, as many parents work late. I was never the best in every subject, but I decided to organize weekend group homework sessions in our courtyard, using whatever school books we had. My idea wasn’t a school project, just something that felt needed. To my surprise, within two months, more than fifteen kids were showing up. Some parents started helping, bringing food or books. This showed me how one idea can grow, even from an average student like me.

At first, I was worried I wouldn’t be able to explain math or science topics well. Sometimes I had to look up the answers myself or admit when I wasn’t sure. But the sessions became less about having the right answer and more about figuring things out together. I saw kids become more comfortable asking questions and less afraid of making mistakes. I think that’s partly because I was honest about my own struggles and didn’t pretend to know everything. It also made me realize how much I could learn by teaching, even when I was only a little ahead of the others.

I believe inVision U is the perfect place for someone like me, who wants to turn ideas into real projects, learning from people who come from totally different backgrounds. I am also very attracted to the interdisciplinary style because I’ve always tried to combine practical skills—like organizing or fixing things around the house—with bigger questions like, "How can I make my street, if not my city, better?" For example, I helped my grandfather repair old bicycles and we donated a few to families nearby who couldn’t afford them. It was a small gesture, but it taught me the value of small actions and practical skills.

Another important aspect for me is building teamwork and leadership skills—not in the sense of always being the best, but being the one willing to start something, even if it’s small. For example, I started a clothes swap event with a few friends after seeing how much goes to waste. That, too, grew slowly, teaching me patience and the value of grassroots efforts. It wasn’t a big event, just a few racks set up in a neighbor’s garage, but I realized how organizing something simple can reduce waste and help others save money. Sometimes I got discouraged when only a few people showed up, but I kept going, and over time more people brought clothes and joined in.

While I may not have high academic prizes, I have energy and the patience to keep trying. I want to learn to make projects better, scale up ideas, and get more knowledge, especially from people outside my comfort zone. This is why I want to study at inVision U—so I can learn new methods and perspectives that will help me serve my community, and hopefully, one day, Kazakhstan on a larger scale. I hope to find mentors and friends who care about the same things, and to bring back what I learn, not just for myself but for my neighborhood and city.

Thank you for considering my application.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: The most difficult period in my life was when my father lost his job. For nearly a year, my family struggled financially. I felt pressured to help at home, so I took on responsibilities like caring for my younger siblings and finding ways to save money. School wasn’t easy then either, because it was hard to concentrate. What helped me cope was talking with my mother and being open with my teachers, explaining my situation. It made me realize I am not alone and that people can be understanding if you are honest. After this period, I became more mature and learned to rely less on complaining and more on finding solutions, even when they are not perfect. It also made me value small successes, like helping my brother pass his math test or organizing our family budget.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: I started waking up early, around 6:30 am, to help my family in the morning and have time to quickly review my school subjects. I also ended the day by reflecting on what went right and wrong (I write three things in a little notebook before bed). I chose these habits to stay organized and to avoid feeling overwhelmed. The early mornings help me have some peace before the day gets busy, and writing reflections gives me a sense of progress, even if it’s small steps. These routines make me more focused and aware of my actions, so I can be a more reliable person for myself and others. I noticed my stress has reduced since I made these changes.

motivation_questions.question: Was there a time when you worked toward a goal for a long time but never reached it? What did you take away from that experience?
motivation_questions.answer: Yes, I tried to improve my English grades to get above 7.0 in IELTS but only managed 6.5 after two attempts. I spent a lot of evenings practicing speaking and watching English videos, but progress was slow. I felt disappointed at first because I had set this goal as my ticket to more opportunities. From this experience, I learned that sometimes progress is not as fast as you hope, and even if the outcome is not ideal, you can still benefit from the process. I also realized that being persistent has value, and not achieving something exactly as planned doesn’t mean you failed—it just means you might need to go a different way or accept your current results and build from there.

motivation_questions.question: Describe a project, idea, or solution you came up with entirely on your own initiative - not as an assignment. Where did the idea come from, and what was the outcome?
motivation_questions.answer: Last summer, I noticed children in my apartment complex sitting outside bored, especially during school holidays. I came up with the idea to organize a reading club for children between 7 and 11. It wasn’t for school credit, just something I felt could help them and improve their reading skills. I asked parents for old books and set up a simple reading corner each Saturday afternoon. At first, only three kids came. But after a few weeks, the group grew to nine. By the end of the summer, the kids had gotten more comfortable with reading aloud, and some even brought friends. It taught me that small ideas can create small impacts, but these can grow. The project is now continued by one of the mothers.

motivation_questions.question: How do you usually make important decisions - do you rely on intuition, data, or advice from others? Walk us through a specific real example from your life.
motivation_questions.answer: I usually combine advice from others with my own feelings. For big decisions, I first talk to my older sister or my best friend, then I think for a while about how each option makes me feel. For example, when I was deciding whether to switch from my school’s physics track to literature, I asked my teachers for feedback and talked with family. I made a list of pros and cons for each, but finally, I followed my intuition about where I could have more impact and be happier. I chose literature and don’t regret it, since it allowed me to do more community-related projects.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever faced a situation where you had to choose between what was efficient and what was right? What did you do, and why?
motivation_questions.answer: Once, during my reading club for kids, there was a suggestion to include only those who could read fairly well since it would make sessions go faster. But I realized that would leave out the children who needed the most help. Even though it made meetings slower and more difficult to manage, I decided to include everyone. As a result, sessions were less efficient, but I felt it was the right thing because the whole idea was to support those struggling. Looking back, I’m glad I made that choice because the least confident readers are now able to join in.

motivation_questions.question: Is there a problem in your city, country, or the world that genuinely troubles you? Why that one specifically - and what do you think is its root cause?
motivation_questions.answer: A problem in Taraz that really troubles me is the lack of safe and inclusive places for children after school. Many parents work late, and the only spaces are sometimes not safe or are too far. For example, my younger cousin has to wait alone at home because there is no nearby center or club he can go to. I think the root cause is that investments often go toward infrastructure or big projects, but not enough on community-level resources that help families. It feels like the needs of everyday people are not being prioritized. This causes stress for both children and parents, because kids might be bored or lonely, and parents worry about their safety. I wish there was more focus on small community projects, and I want to contribute more ideas in this direction.

motivation_questions.question: How has your worldview shifted over the last 2-3 years? What drove that change - a book, a person, an event, an experience?
motivation_questions.answer: My worldview shifted a lot after I started the children’s reading club. Before, I thought only teachers or officials can make change, but I saw how anyone can take small steps to help others. For example, at the club meetings, some of the older kids would help the younger ones with difficult words, and even parents who were shy at first started bringing snacks or suggesting books. Seeing parents and kids come together showed me that community is not about leaders only, but about everyone taking little actions. This experience made me more optimistic about people’s willingness to help. I realized that even if an idea feels small, it can start something larger, just by encouraging others to join in, even in simple ways.

motivation_questions.question: What do you think Kazakhstan will look like in 20 years - and what role do you believe your generation will play in shaping it?
motivation_questions.answer: I hope Kazakhstan will have more regional development and not just focus on big cities. More support should go to small towns like Taraz, so young people can build a good life without moving away. For example, better schools and local businesses could help people stay. I think my generation will be the one to demand more fairness and create new solutions locally, not just wait for central government. We can use technology to connect and bring the benefits to our own communities, making the country more balanced. Maybe some of us will start projects or online platforms that help small towns share resources or ideas. I also believe we will talk more openly about problems and try to solve them together, even if the process is slow.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: When I decided to include younger, less skilled readers in the children’s club, some parents and older students thought it would slow everything down. They said that the meetings would become less interesting, and one parent even told me privately that I should focus only on “gifted” kids. But I insisted on making the group open because I remembered how hard it was for my younger brother in school when he was left out of activities just because he struggled with reading. What drove me was remembering my own feelings of being excluded—sometimes at school events, sometimes even at family gatherings. Even though it made my job harder, like needing to prepare simpler materials and spend more time helping the new members, I felt it was the right thing, and I’m glad I pushed for it.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: Apart from the reading club, I sometimes help elderly neighbors by shopping for them or just visiting to talk. I do this not for any reward, but because they seem lonely or need help with simple things. For example, last winter I helped Mrs. Nurkeeva carry groceries when the roads were icy, and sometimes I sit with Mr. Bekzat to listen to his stories about his youth. What it gives me is a feeling that I belong to a community, not just as a student but as someone who can make a positive impact. It’s also taught me to be more patient and understanding with older people. Sometimes they repeat stories or forget things, but I see how much they appreciate company and small help, and it’s made me feel more connected to them and to my neighborhood.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer: My three core values are empathy, perseverance, and fairness. Empathy—for example, I listen to others and try to understand what they need, like when I included struggling kids in the reading club. Sometimes, I noticed people were quiet or left out, so I’d ask them directly if they wanted to join or needed help. Perseverance—I kept running the club even when turnout was low, believing it would pick up. There were weeks when only two people showed up, but I still prepared activities and didn’t cancel, because I thought maybe someone would need the club that week. Fairness—I make sure opportunities are open to everyone, even if it’s more work, because I know what it’s like to feel left out. For example, I invited students from different grades, not just my friends, which meant more organizing, but it felt right.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: Organizing the clothing swap was successful because we partnered with a local market and advertised in our neighborhood, which brought in more people than I expected. We made posters and shared the event in a WhatsApp group, so word spread pretty well. What did not work was trying to organize it too quickly—we didn’t have enough volunteers for sorting, and it got a bit chaotic when people started arriving with bags of clothes at the same time. If I could do it again, I’d plan more time for organizing and involve more people from the start, maybe by making a schedule for volunteers and reaching out to friends earlier. I learned that starting small is fine, and asking for help early is important. Even with small events, planning details like sorting and setup matters a lot.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: I’ve occasionally earned money by tutoring younger students in Russian and literature. After running the reading club, a parent recommended me to others, which surprised me at first, since I never really saw myself as a tutor. I made a plan for each lesson, identified their struggles, and used fun activities to build confidence—sometimes games, sometimes reading aloud together. It was not big money, but enough to buy books for myself and for some children in the club. What mattered most was seeing kids improve and enjoy learning more. Sometimes, a student would finally understand a poem or write a story they were proud of, and that felt more rewarding than the payment. I learned that patience and creativity can make a difference.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: I would use the money to set up a free after-school club for children with books, games, and simple snacks in my neighborhood. I’d recruit older students to volunteer, and maybe bring in a local teacher. Many kids lack a safe, interesting place to go after school, so this would have real value by supporting their learning and socializing. I’d document the progress and share the model so maybe other neighborhoods could do the same. For example, I could start by buying some secondhand books and board games, and ask a nearby bakery if they could donate snacks. The club could meet three times a week in a community room or even outside, depending on the weather. I would keep a diary about what activities were most popular and how the children responded, and maybe ask parents for feedback at the end of the month. Even if it was only for a short time, it could help the kids feel more included and inspired, and maybe the idea would last after the month was finished.

text_inputs.interview_text:
My main reason for applying to inVision U is to gain the knowledge and connections that I cannot access locally in Taraz. Here, most opportunities feel limited, and I often feel like I have to figure things out on my own. I’m excited about the idea of working on real projects with others who have different backgrounds, because in my own experience, even just collaborating with students from other schools in Taraz has taught me how much you can learn from people with different perspectives. For example, when I worked with a group on a local volunteering initiative, I saw how someone’s small suggestion could change the whole direction of our project for the better. I want to be the kind of leader who doesn’t just take charge but listens and acts together with others, because I’ve seen that leadership is not only about giving instructions, but also about understanding the strengths and ideas of each team member.

I also want to improve my project management and learn how to scale small ideas into bigger ones, since most of my projects so far have stayed pretty local and small. At inVision U, I hope to find mentors who’ve faced similar challenges but learned to create real, lasting changes, not just quick fixes. I know my academics are not top level, but I believe that with proper guidance and teamwork, I can learn faster and make a greater impact, especially if I can observe and practice with people who have more experience than I do. My dream is to return to Taraz with new skills and show other students here that starting small is not a weakness—it’s just the beginning, and even if you come from a smaller city, you can achieve something real. That’s why I really want to be part of your university.

scenario_meta.notes: Candidate from Astana, language profile is mixed, writes impressively but achievements don't back substance.

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
Dear inVision U Selection Committee,

Growing up in the capital of Kazakhstan, I have been constantly inspired by Astana’s mix of global ambition and deep cultural roots. Even as a student, I have always positioned myself as someone who builds bridges between people—be it through language, culture, or ideas. My biggest dream is to not just witness Kazakhstan’s international rise, but to help shape it as a leader who understands both the local and global perspectives.

My school life has not been full of traditional academic awards or olympiad victories. However, I have focused on developing communication skills that let me inspire and motivate those around me. I take the initiative to organize debates in English and Kazakh, and I often volunteer to represent my class at events, even if I am not the expert in the topic. Sometimes things don’t go perfectly, but I try to be the person who keeps everyone united. For example, during our city’s youth festival, I helped bring together students from different schools to co-create a multicultural performance. It was a challenge because opinions clashed, but I facilitated compromise and made sure every voice was included, which resulted in a memorable show appreciated by our community.

I think these experiences have shaped my view of leadership. For me, leadership is not about being the loudest or the most knowledgeable, but about making sure everyone feels heard and valued. In our debate club, for example, I noticed that some students were shy to speak in front of others, especially in English. So I started organizing small practice sessions where we could support each other and give feedback in a relaxed setting. Over time, I saw some of these classmates gain confidence and even volunteer for bigger school events, which made me realize the importance of encouragement and patience.

What excites me most about inVision U is the focus on real-world projects and teamwork. I strongly believe that diverse groups can only succeed if there is someone to connect the dots—this is where my strength lies. I am not always the technical expert, but I make sure the group is motivated, that ideas do not get lost in conflict, and that the result is greater than the sum of its parts. I remember during a group science project, our team struggled because one member was more interested in design while another preferred calculations. I suggested we split the tasks so each person could focus on their strengths, and then we met to combine our work. It was not only more efficient, but everyone also felt proud of what we had achieved together.

My experiences with international guests, student exchanges, and volunteering have broadened my worldview. I value the interdisciplinary approach, as I see it as essential in a fast-changing world. Once, I hosted a group of students from Turkey for a cultural exchange. At first, it was awkward because of language barriers, but by organizing games and sharing stories, we found common ground. This made me realize that real connections go beyond language and nationality. These experiences have motivated me to keep learning about different cultures and to look for new ways to bring people together.

inVision U would give me the environment to learn from the best and to contribute my people skills, intercultural awareness, and my drive to unite Kazakhstan’s youth for something greater. I am eager to meet other students who are passionate about building a better future and to become part of a community that values both innovation and collaboration.

Thank you for your consideration.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: One of the most difficult periods was when I struggled academically in ninth grade and almost failed my math class. Мой преподаватель был очень строгий, и я чувствовал себя неуверенно рядом с одноклассниками, которые сдавали олимпиаду. I coped by asking for help from friends, attending remedial classes, and practicing more on my own. Although I did not become the best student in math, I learned not to avoid my weaknesses and to ask for support. It made me realize that there is value in perseverance and that it is okay to rely on a community. My confidence in myself grew after that, even if the problem did not disappear entirely.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: Я веду ежедневник — записываю в него все планы и мысли. Each morning I write out three things I want to achieve, and in the evening I reflect on whether I reached them and how I felt. This habit helps me stay focused and not get overwhelmed, especially when I have many social or school commitments. It also makes me more self-aware and organized. I chose this approach because I realized that sometimes ideas and responsibilities can overwhelm me, but writing calms me and gives direction. It has made me better at setting priorities and communicating my needs to others.

motivation_questions.question: Was there a time when you worked toward a goal for a long time but never reached it? What did you take away from that experience?
motivation_questions.answer: I spent almost a year preparing for an English language competition, dreaming to participate internationally, but I was not selected in the final round. Это было очень обидно, так как я чувствовал, что вложил много усилий. I remember practicing late at night, writing essays, and even asking my friends and teachers to listen to my speeches and give me feedback. However, looking back, I understand that the process improved my English and my confidence for public speaking. I became less afraid to speak in front of others, and I learned to organize my thoughts better. The lesson is that sometimes, you become stronger even if you do not win the prize itself. Что важнее — это умение продолжать стараться, и не терять веру в себя, даже если результат не тот, на который ты надеялся.

motivation_questions.question: Describe a project, idea, or solution you came up with entirely on your own initiative - not as an assignment. Where did the idea come from, and what was the outcome?
motivation_questions.answer: I created an "International Culture Day" at my school after noticing how every ethnic group celebrated apart. I wanted to break this division. Я подошел к администрации, предложил идею, нашёл волонтеров и организовал совместную программу. We set up food stands, performances, and workshops—each group could present something unique, like traditional dances or crafts. The event brought together students and teachers from over ten backgrounds, and it was the first time many of us tried food and music from other cultures in our school setting. Some people were hesitant at first, but by the end, everyone was laughing and sharing stories. It was so impactful that the school decided to do it every year. I learned that sometimes even a small initiative can change the atmosphere in a community.

motivation_questions.question: How do you usually make important decisions - do you rely on intuition, data, or advice from others? Walk us through a specific real example from your life.
motivation_questions.answer: Обычно я совмещаю советы друзей с личной интуицией. For example, when I had to choose between staying in my comfort group or joining a new debate team, I talked with my closest friends and my family. Some of them thought I shouldn’t risk and advised me to stay where I was, others encouraged me to try something new. I listened carefully to their opinions and tried to imagine how each choice would affect me. But in the end, the final decision was based on my gut feeling that I needed new challenges and experiences. It was not easy, and I doubted myself at first, but joining the debate team helped me grow and meet people who changed my outlook. So I think both rational advice and feeling matter to me, and I try to balance them when making important decisions.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever faced a situation where you had to choose between what was efficient and what was right? What did you do, and why?
motivation_questions.answer: During the youth festival rehearsal, it would have been faster to cut out minority acts that needed more practice. Но я настоял, чтобы все участвовали, несмотря на возможные задержки и трудности. I believe that inclusion is more important than saving time. Some of the other organizers worried that the audience would get bored or that the show might seem less professional, but I explained that every group deserved a chance to be seen. I took extra time to help prepare their parts, sometimes staying late after the main rehearsal to go over small details with them. Although the program ran longer than planned, it was much more meaningful for everyone, especially for those who might not otherwise get the chance to perform. I still think about this choice and feel it was the right thing to do.

motivation_questions.question: Is there a problem in your city, country, or the world that genuinely troubles you? Why that one specifically - and what do you think is its root cause?
motivation_questions.answer: In Astana, one issue is that people from different cultures and backgrounds often do not fully engage with each other, despite living in close proximity. Я думаю, корень проблемы — это стереотипы и страх неизвестного. Sometimes, for example, I notice that at school or in public places, students tend to stay with others who share their language or culture, even if they are curious about others. It saddens me to see missed opportunities for understanding and collaboration. This makes it harder to build trust or friendships, and I think it affects how people feel about their city. I believe that more events and open communication, especially among youth, can help bridge these gaps. Even small shared projects or discussions could make a difference.

motivation_questions.question: How has your worldview shifted over the last 2-3 years? What drove that change - a book, a person, an event, an experience?
motivation_questions.answer: A big shift happened after I joined a student exchange program with students from Turkey and South Korea. После этой поездки я понял, насколько мало знал о жизни других, и стал смотреть шире на мир. Before that, I mostly saw things from my own small city’s point of view, but meeting people from these countries, hearing their stories, and even just sharing meals together made me aware of all the differences and similarities in how we think. I started to read more about their cultures and talk with them about things like family, school, and even food traditions. Now I realize that international perspective is crucial, not only for career, but for personal growth and empathy. It made me question my assumptions and try to be more open-minded in daily life.

motivation_questions.question: What do you think Kazakhstan will look like in 20 years - and what role do you believe your generation will play in shaping it?
motivation_questions.answer: Kazakhstan will be even more global and diverse. Я верю, что молодёжь будет строить мосты, не только внутри страны, но и между странами Центральной Азии. I think in 20 years, there will be more collaboration with neighbors, and cities will become more multicultural. Our generation must be the one that learns to communicate and solve complex global problems through unity and understanding, not only technical skills. For example, working on environmental issues like water sharing or climate change will need cooperation, not competition. Maybe we will create more student exchange programs or volunteer together on cross-border projects. I feel that young people in Kazakhstan will help break stereotypes and become more open to new ideas, which is very important for the future.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: Когда я настаивал на равноправном участии всех групп в фестивале, некоторые быстро разозлились — считали, что это мешает успеху. Например, организаторы хотели выделить больше времени только популярным коллективам, а остальные просто поставить фоном. Я спорил с ними и предлагал расписание, где каждый коллектив получает шанс проявить себя. I spoke up because I believe no one should be left out, even if their group is less known or less liked. Было непросто — друзья говорили, что я все только усложняю и портю атмосферу подготовки. Though it was hard, the outcome proved it is better to stand for inclusion, even when it’s inconvenient for the majority. В итоге все группы выступили, и некоторые даже удивили всех своим талантом.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: I volunteered to help international students adjust to life in Astana, showing them around, helping with paperwork, and sometimes even language. Я делал это, потому что сам однажды чувствовал себя чужим. I remember meeting a student from Turkey who was lost at the bus station and didn’t know how to buy a ticket. We figured it out together, using a mix of English, Russian, and gestures. Sometimes I translated documents or explained how dorm rules work. It was never a big deal, but I saw how even small things can make a big difference. This experience gave me not just friends, but also a sense of responsibility to make my city more welcoming for everyone. It made me notice how many people feel out of place and how much a little help matters.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer: Inclusion: I made sure all students had a place on the youth festival stage, even if it complicated things. For example, I convinced organizers to let a classmate with a stutter perform, which made the schedule harder to manage, but it was worth it to see everyone included. Curiosity: I joined debate clubs and international programs to learn about new perspectives. I often asked questions that others didn't and sometimes felt out of place, but I think this helped me grow. Support: When friends struggled, I always volunteered to help with their studies or problems, even when I had my own issues. For instance, I spent evenings tutoring a friend who was failing math, even though I was worried about my own grades.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: I organized our school’s “International Culture Day” and a charity food drive. What worked: using social media to get the word out, and involving many volunteers from different classes, which made things smoother. We made posters and shared updates in class group chats, which really helped with participation. What did not: sometimes we started too late with planning, and some activities ended up being rushed, like running out of time to set up some stands or forgetting to assign someone to pick up donated food on time. If I did it again, I would create a more detailed timeline, delegate tasks earlier, and maybe hold short meetings to check progress so that nothing gets forgotten. I also learned it helps to write lists for each event.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: Last summer I tutored junior students in English and helped run a small online marketplace for selling handmade crafts with friends. Мы начинали через Instagram, делились задачами — я вел коммуникацию, другие писали посты и отвечали на сообщения. Sometimes I also helped with packaging and figuring out delivery, which was harder than I expected because we had to coordinate with buyers and sometimes they changed their minds. It didn’t bring much money, but it taught me the basics of sales and online teamwork. I learned how difficult it can be to organize people and how important it is to respond quickly to customers. Even dealing with small misunderstandings between us was an experience. I think these things will help me later.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: I would organize a city-wide "Unity Through Culture" youth forum in Astana, inviting speakers, artists, and youth leaders from different ethnic groups and backgrounds. The event would include workshops, performances, and networking spaces. Я считаю, что именно такие проекты сближают людей и формируют культуру доверия. For example, there could be a panel where students from Kazakh, Russian, Korean, and other communities share personal stories about growing up in Kazakhstan. Workshops might include traditional crafts or cooking classes, where participants learn from each other. The $1,000 would help cover venue rental, simple materials, and maybe small honorariums for speakers. I think that even one day of honest conversation and shared experience can start to break down stereotypes. This is especially important in a city like Astana, where so many cultures meet.

text_inputs.interview_text: inVision U для меня — это не просто возможность получить образование, но и шанс попасть в среду, где ценятся разные точки зрения и умение видеть связи между дисциплинами. Я считаю, что сейчас, когда в мире так быстро меняются технологии и социальные процессы, особенно важно учиться быть гибким и открытым. I see myself as a future diplomat or connector, helping Kazakhstan become more open and creative. Уже сейчас я стараюсь быть таким человеком: например, я участвую в школьных дебатах, где приходится защищать позиции, отличные от моих собственных взглядов, и это учит меня слушать и уважать других. My strength is inspiring teams, rallying people around shared goals and helping them see the strengths in their diversity. Иногда мне не хватает технических знаний, особенно в IT или аналитике, и я иногда ощущаю неуверенность, когда речь идёт о сложных цифровых инструментах. Но я стараюсь компенсировать это, объединяя людей с разным опытом и помогая им работать вместе. I want to learn from bright minds, develop leadership, and participate in projects with real social impact. Мне нравится придумывать нестандартные решения и вдохновлять других на совместную работу — например, в прошлом году я организовал школьный проект по волонтёрству, и нам удалось вовлечь учеников из разных классов и культур. Я считаю, что мои навыки презентации, межкультурной коммуникации и страсть к объединению людей пора дадут плоды. I’m ready to grow, contribute, and represent my country on a new level at inVision U.
