Я понимаю, что будет трудно, и, возможно, придётся преодолевать языковой барьер и адаптироваться к новым методам обучения, но именно это меня и мотивирует: выйти за привычные рамки и стать частью глобального сообщества.

scenario_meta.notes: Candidate from a mono-town in Karaganda region, education limited, good self-awareness but uneven communication, language profile is mixed.

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
Dear Admissions Committee,

Меня зовут Арай, я из небольшого города Абай в Карагандинской области, где жизнь не всегда быстрая и доступ к возможностям ограничен. Я не отличник, честно, у меня средние оценки, но всегда старался быть полезной для своей школы и двора. I like to start things that I feel are really needed even if no one asks for it. For example, when we had the problem with no working computers in the youth center, I tried to collect old devices from the neighborhood and fix them with my uncle, so at least some kids could use the Internet for homework. It wasn’t easy—some computers were too old, and sometimes parts were missing, but we managed to get a few working. I remember how happy my younger brother was when he could finally use a computer to do his math homework, not just his phone. Even though it was a small step, it made a difference for a few kids.

To be honest, I often feel that big universities are for smarter students than me. But I see inVision U as a place where initiative and desire to bring change really mean something, where you learn not only theory but also how to take real action. I am very interested in interdisciplinary projects because I see how life in Abay requires solutions that connect many fields—energy, social issues, education. For example, during winter, we sometimes have electricity shortages, which affects how students can study at home after dark. It made me think about how important it is to connect technology and social planning, maybe even simple solutions like sharing study spaces or creating small solar charging spots. My wish is to study somewhere I can learn how to make better projects for small towns, meet people with new ideas, and then bring those ideas home.

One thing I learned by living here: nothing comes quickly and you need to convince people, try different ways. I started an environment clean-up with teenagers who first said "why bother." It took weeks to show my friends that it could be fun or useful. We got local bakery to help with snacks, and even older people joined. It was messy, not perfect, but we made our streets cleaner. I remember one day it rained and almost no one came, but a few of us still picked up trash, and later others joined just because they saw our effort. This taught me that persistence sometimes works better than loud words.

I want to develop, to learn to structure my projects better, work in a team, and understand how to motivate others for the long run. My family taught me to be patient and realistic. My mom always said, “If you want something to change, start with what you can.” I know I don’t have big prizes. But I have persistence and a desire to get practical skills to help my community. I am ready to accept my weaknesses and learn from them. For me, getting into inVision U would be not just about my success, but about showing other small-town youths that you can influence your place, even with small steps. I hope to come back to Abay one day and start a center where teenagers can learn digital skills and work on their own projects, even if they don’t have perfect grades. Thank you for reading my application.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: Самое трудное для меня было, когда школу временно закрыли из-за аварии отопления в середине учебного года. Мои родители оба работали, но дома было холодно, и учиться приходилось в библиотеке. I coped by spending time with younger neighbors, helping them with homework and trying to keep study rhythm. Это научило меня брать ответственность, пусть даже просто помогать тем, кому сложнее. I learned not to give up even when resources are missing, but to find what you can do. After this, small problems seemed less scary, and I started to trust my own ability to improvise.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: I began to wake up earlier to do all personal and house tasks before school, since evenings are unpredictable in my family (может понадобиться помощь по хозяйству). For example, sometimes I need to help my grandmother with shopping or my younger sibling with homework, and I can’t plan exactly when that will happen. Also, I try to set weekly goals, not daily, because sometimes you just cannot do everything as planned—maybe I get sick, or there are last-minute chores. Эти привычки делают меня более готовой к неожиданностям и гибкой. Without routines, I felt lost in chaos, but now, even if the result is small, it’s progress. I chose these habits because my environment is not stable, so flexibility helps me stay on track.

motivation_questions.question: Was there a time when you worked toward a goal for a long time but never reached it? What did you take away from that experience?
motivation_questions.answer: I tried for several years to start an English club in our school. Мало кто хотел ходить, не было нормального учителя. I wrote to the regional resource center but they had no budget for us and couldn’t send anyone. Sometimes I tried making posters or inviting classmates personally, but still, only a few students came, and we didn’t get an active teacher. In the end, club never worked out as I wanted. But I realized it’s okay if not all projects succeed, главное — пробовать и не бояться провалов. I still improved my English, especially because I practiced making announcements and writing emails. Next time, I will start from smaller goals, like maybe just a speaking group with two or three people first.

motivation_questions.question: Describe a project, idea, or solution you came up with entirely on your own initiative - not as an assignment. Where did the idea come from, and what was the outcome?
motivation_questions.answer: When the youth center’s computers broke, I saw how some kids stopped coming. I suggested to neighbors to donate old laptops/desktops and tried to fix them. Uncle helped with tools and advice. In total, we got three old PCs working. Now, a few students can do homework online, though sometimes slow. It felt good to solve a real problem and show others you don’t need much to start. I learned a lot about computers—like how to replace RAM and reinstall Windows—even though I messed up the first time and had to start over. Some of the donated computers were really old, so I had to search forums for solutions and sometimes just experiment. I realized that even small efforts can make a difference for people around me.

motivation_questions.question: How do you usually make important decisions - do you rely on intuition, data, or advice from others? Walk us through a specific real example from your life.
motivation_questions.answer: I discuss with family, especially my mom, and then check what is really possible (resources, time). Но финальное решение принимаю сама, по-честному – часто из-за чувства, как оно отзывается внутри. For example, when choosing whether to spend summer working on the clean-up project or taking a part-time job, I decided on the clean-up, even if family said earning money was more practical. I listened but chose what felt right. It wasn’t easy, because my family had good reasons, and I did worry about money. But I kept thinking about how I would feel at the end of summer—if I’d regret missing the project. I talked to friends too, and some agreed with my mom. Still, I just couldn’t ignore that inner feeling, so I went with it.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever faced a situation where you had to choose between what was efficient and what was right? What did you do, and why?
motivation_questions.answer: During environment clean-up, one friend suggested we only do central street—it is faster, more visible. Я настояла, что надо убрать и во дворах, где больше мусора, хотя это сложнее и дольше. It was harder: fewer people wanted to go, but those who stayed, we did real change for our neighbors, not just for 'picture.' I felt that helping where others don’t see is more important. Sometimes it’s tempting to just do the easy or public part so others notice your work, but in our courtyards there was actually more trash, especially behind the garages and near playgrounds. It took us almost twice as long, and my hands were dirty and tired after, but I saw some elderly neighbors thank us, and I realized that the right thing often isn’t the most obvious or rewarded.

motivation_questions.question: Is there a problem in your city, country, or the world that genuinely troubles you? Why that one specifically - and what do you think is its root cause?
motivation_questions.answer: В Абае проблема — молодежь не верит, что здесь можно что-то изменить. Most want to move away, thinking nothing good happens here. I think it’s because opportunities are limited and there are few examples of local success. For example, when I talk to my classmates, almost everyone says they plan to study or work in bigger cities or even abroad. They feel that in Abay, no matter how hard you try, it won’t make a difference, so they give up before even starting. I sometimes feel the same way. If more people tried new things and got small wins, maybe it would change the mood. Maybe seeing even a small business succeed or a community project actually work would show that change is possible.

motivation_questions.question: How has your worldview shifted over the last 2-3 years? What drove that change - a book, a person, an event, an experience?
motivation_questions.answer: My worldview changed when I got to know one retired teacher in our town who started a small library just using her home. Она доказала, что не нужно ждать помощи – можно делать что можешь, где находишься. Before, I always thought that only big organizations or the government could help with problems, but seeing her just open her doors to kids and share her books made me realize that even one person can make a difference. Sometimes I help her organize the books or read stories to younger children. Now I try to look for local solutions instead of waiting for someone big to come fix things. Even if it’s something small, I think it’s better to try.

motivation_questions.question: What do you think Kazakhstan will look like in 20 years - and what role do you believe your generation will play in shaping it?
motivation_questions.answer: In 20 years, I hope regional towns will have more voice and people won’t rush to Almaty/Astana for a good life. Наше поколение должно учится делать жизнь лучше здесь, а не только мечтать уехать. I want to be part of this change—showing that making your own place better is possible. Right now, I see that many young people in my town just want to leave, thinking all opportunities are in big cities. But I think if more of us try to improve things locally, over time our towns can grow too. Maybe it will start with small projects, like organizing events or helping schools, but those things matter. I believe our generation can slowly change the idea that success only exists elsewhere.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: When I asked to clean not just the main street but also small yards, some students quit. Было обидно, но я всё равно настояла. I wanted to help those streets no one cared about. It wasn’t comfortable, but it was right. I remember people looking at me like I was asking for too much, maybe even wasting their time. But I thought, why should only the visible places look nice? The small yards are where kids play and old people walk. Just because everyone ignored them didn’t mean they didn’t matter. I stayed behind after others left and picked up the trash myself, and a few younger students ended up helping me in the end. It felt lonely at first, but I learned that doing what you think is right is not always popular or easy.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: I often help younger girls in our block with homework or just talking. It helps me feel useful, but also teaches patience—sometimes they don’t listen or argue. Я поняла, что быть примером – это не просто, но результат всегда стоит того. For example, last year I spent a few evenings helping one girl understand some math problems. She struggled at first and even got upset, but eventually she figured it out and was proud of herself. That made me happy too. Sometimes we just talk about school or things they worry about, and I try to listen. Now, I notice they start to help each other too, like sharing notes or explaining things, which makes our block feel more friendly.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer: 1) Responsibility—when my parents work late, I cook and care for my little brother. Sometimes this means helping him with his homework, or making sure he eats, even if I feel tired after my own classes. 2) Openness—I’m not afraid to ask for help, like when fixing old computers, I usually watch tutorials or ask my tech teacher questions, since I know I can’t figure it out alone. I try to listen to advice, even if it feels awkward. 3) Persistence—trying projects even when they fail at first, пример: English club. The first time I tried to run an English club, almost nobody came, but I kept organizing meetings and eventually a few classmates joined, so I didn’t give up.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: Организовала субботник и попыталась открыть English club. Clean-up worked because we included snacks and music, so more people wanted to join and it was just fun, not boring work. But club didn’t last. I think it was too ambitious for me alone, and I didn’t really know how to keep people interested after the first meeting. Maybe I needed a plan or more activities, not just talking in English. Next time, I’d start with smaller group and more support from teachers, step by step. I learned that even small things like music or food help, and it’s better to have a team or at least someone to help, not try to do everything myself.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: I sometimes help neighbors with cleaning, or gardening for elderly (за небольшую оплату). It’s simple work but gives trust and I learn to be reliable. Мне нравится, что это что-то реальное, а не просто учеба. For example, last summer I helped Mrs. Irina, who lives upstairs, to clean her apartment every weekend. She cannot do it herself after her surgery, so I came and helped with floors, dusting, and sometimes bringing groceries from the market. It wasn’t hard work, but I realized how much it mattered to her. Sometimes I also water the flowers and pull weeds in another neighbor’s garden. I get a little money, but also people know they can count on me. It feels good to be useful in a practical way, not only with studying.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: I would buy and fix more used computers and set up a free homework/Internet corner in our youth center. Сейчас “цифровое неравенство" чувствуется сильно, а так больше детей смогут учиться и общаться. Not every family here can afford a good computer or fast internet, so many students miss out on online classes or even simple things like searching for information for homework. $1,000 would let me get several old laptops or desktops, maybe from local offices or through online ads, and I’d try to repair them myself or with friends. We could create a small area with tables and chairs, maybe add some educational posters. I think it would help kids who don’t have computers at home feel more included, and maybe they would help each other with schoolwork or just spend time together safely.

text_inputs.interview_text: inVision U для меня — это шанс научиться делать настоящие, живые проекты, которые приносят пользу людям, особенно в маленьких городах, где часто не хватает инициатив и поддержки. Я родом из Абая, и мне хорошо знакомо, как важно не бояться начинать с малого. Например, в нашей школе мы с друзьями пытались организовать небольшие мероприятия для соседей, но часто сталкивались с нехваткой ресурсов или опыта. Я понял, что даже маленькие шаги могут вдохновить других, и теперь хочу научиться делать это более осознанно и эффективно. I am not afraid to start with small things, since that’s what I know from Abay. For me, being an organizer is not only about managing tasks but also about finding the right words to motivate people who might not believe in themselves or their ideas at first. Sometimes, I feel unsure how to do this, especially when working with people from different backgrounds. That’s why I want to see how others manage in different regions and maybe learn to motivate people better. I hope to become not just a student, but someone who brings practical solutions home and shares knowledge with my community, maybe even helps other young people to try their own projects. My weak side — I sometimes don’t finish ideas or get discouraged if I don’t see quick results, but I want to learn resilience, how to build bigger teams and plan long-term, even when things don’t work out at first. Я не боюсь сложностей, хочу пробовать, ошибаться и учиться у тех, кто тоже строит новое, а не просто говорит. Спасибо за возможность подать заявку — для меня это уже шаг вперед.

scenario_meta.notes: Candidate from Pavlodar, polished communicator, shallow depth, strong on 'soft skills' narrative.

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
Dear inVision U Admissions Team,

I am writing to you with great excitement from Pavlodar, a city that is sometimes overlooked, but full of ambitious young people like me who want to make a real impact. I believe that communication and connection between people is the most powerful tool for change. Throughout my school life, I have always been the person to bring people together, to motivate and encourage, even in tough times. My academic results have been average, but I have spent most of my time and energy on improving the community in which I live, on amplifying voices that are rarely heard, and on creating a supportive environment for those who feel invisible.

For me, the idea of community is not an abstract one. I grew up in a close-knit neighborhood where people know each other, but sometimes do not speak openly about their struggles. I remember a time when a classmate of mine was being bullied, and nobody seemed to notice. I felt frustrated, powerless at first, but then I started to talk with other students in class about how we could help. We decided to sit with him at lunch, include him in group activities, and simply ask how he was doing. It wasn’t a big intervention, but over a few months, I noticed he smiled more often and even started participating in school events. Moments like this showed me that small actions, even just a friendly conversation, can change someone’s whole experience at school.

I am inspired by the interdisciplinary learning approach of inVision U because I truly believe real solutions come from the intersection of different perspectives and skills. I have often organized and led group discussions about our city’s future, environmental issues, and how to strengthen our youth community. Though none of these was a big official project, they have had noticeable effect, as classmates have taken small steps to volunteer or spread positivity in our school. For example, after our discussion about littering in local parks, several classmates started organizing weekend clean-ups—nothing large-scale, but small groups collecting trash together. It was satisfying to see an idea actually become an action, even if on a small scale.

What makes me motivated is giving energy and hope to others. For example, I once created an encouragement campaign at school that involved leaving positive notes on lockers and inviting students to share what they liked about their peers during assemblies. It seemed like a small act, but many people said it changed the mood in our school for the better. I did not do this for grades or recognition, but because I saw a need for more kindness and understanding. I sometimes struggle with feeling unsure about my own abilities, but when I see that someone feels included because of something I organized, it makes me feel like I am on the right path.

At inVision U, I know I will discover even more ways to inspire, empower, and organize people for a stronger Kazakhstan. I am eager to learn how to turn inspiration into lasting projects, to understand social challenges in depth, and to build meaningful collaborations with people who have different stories and skills. I want to become a leader who encourages dialogue, empathy, and active participation in society. I am especially interested in how inVision U’s programs combine social sciences, arts, and practical skills, because I feel that creative thinking is just as important as logical analysis when it comes to solving real-world problems.

Thank you for considering me for this opportunity.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: My most difficult period was the transition from middle to high school. I struggled to find my place, felt average among my peers, and sometimes doubted my abilities. I remember sitting in classes, feeling invisible, and thinking everyone else had it all figured out. What helped me cope was reaching out and connecting with classmates who felt the same way. Together, we started supporting each other, creating informal study groups and listening circles where we could talk honestly about our worries or even just complain about homework. This made me appreciate the importance of community and mutual encouragement. Afterward, I became more resilient and confident about finding solutions in partnership with others, not just on my own. I still sometimes struggle, but now I know I can ask for help.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: Every morning, I write three intentions in my notebook for the day, focusing on communication, kindness, and gratitude. I also make a point to have a genuine conversation with at least one person I don’t usually speak with, even if it feels awkward sometimes. These habits improve my mood, help me make new connections, and have given me a reputation for being approachable and dependable, which I honestly didn’t expect at first. For example, last month I started talking with a quiet classmate, and now we help each other with assignments. I chose these habits to intentionally build a supportive and inclusive environment around me, something I did not always receive myself, especially during my first year at school when I felt a bit isolated.

motivation_questions.question: Was there a time when you worked toward a goal for a long time but never reached it? What did you take away from that experience?
motivation_questions.answer: I spent nearly a year preparing to start a youth podcast about local issues in Pavlodar, but I struggled to find consistent team members and technical support, so we never published an episode. At first, I was disappointed because I put in a lot of effort planning topics, reaching out to possible guests, and even writing outlines for our first few shows. But I learned how difficult sustained teamwork really is, especially when everyone has different schedules and priorities. I learned the value of small steps and became more patient with group initiatives, realizing that impact can start with simpler ideas or even just conversations. Now, even informal discussions about local problems feel meaningful, and I know not all projects will go as planned, but there is still value in the attempt.

motivation_questions.question: Describe a project, idea, or solution you came up with entirely on your own initiative - not as an assignment. Where did the idea come from, and what was the outcome?
motivation_questions.answer: I started the 'Kindness Notes' project at school, where students left anonymous supportive messages for each other on lockers and doors. The idea came after I noticed classmates feeling isolated and underappreciated, especially during exam season when stress levels were high. I made simple paper slips and set up a box for submissions. Soon, messages like “You matter” and “Good luck on your test!” started appearing. The result was a warmer, more inclusive atmosphere. Some students even told me privately that reading a kind note made their day better. Teachers commented that there were fewer conflicts that month, and people seemed a bit more open with each other. I realized a positive word at the right moment can have a far-reaching effect, even if it seems small at first.

motivation_questions.question: How do you usually make important decisions - do you rely on intuition, data, or advice from others? Walk us through a specific real example from your life.
motivation_questions.answer: I strongly rely on advice from those I trust, especially my older cousin and best friends, and then I listen to my own intuition about what feels right. For example, when I had to decide whether to attend a debate competition or help organize a community event in my neighborhood, I talked it over with my cousin, who encouraged me to think about my long-term goals, and also asked two friends what they would do. I made a list of pros and cons for both options, like teamwork experience versus public speaking practice. In the end, I chose the community event because it suited my style and values more, even though part of me was nervous to miss out on debate. I think this mix helps me make balanced choices.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever faced a situation where you had to choose between what was efficient and what was right? What did you do, and why?
motivation_questions.answer: Yes, when preparing a class presentation, it was suggested we use pre-made slides from the internet to save time. But I felt it was more ethical and respectful to create our own, even if it took much longer. I convinced my team, and though it was harder work, we felt better presenting our own ideas in the end. During the process, we struggled with designing slides and finding the right information, and at one point someone said we could just copy a chart directly. I reminded them that it wouldn’t feel honest, and we tried to make our own version instead. It taught me to stand up for what is right, even in small matters, and that sometimes doing things properly is more satisfying than just being efficient.

motivation_questions.question: Is there a problem in your city, country, or the world that genuinely troubles you? Why that one specifically - and what do you think is its root cause?
motivation_questions.answer: Isolation and lack of engagement among youth in Pavlodar worries me—the sense that their voices do not matter. I think this comes from a mix of tradition, lack of youth-centered spaces, and a culture of not expressing opinions. For example, in many schools and even in outside activities, adults tend to make all the decisions, and young people are expected just to follow along. There aren’t many places where youth can openly share what they think or actually influence things happening in the city. Sometimes even friends feel uncomfortable talking about real issues, because it’s not really encouraged. I’d like to see more platforms for genuine youth voice and participation in our city, so that young people feel seen and actually involved in shaping their environment.

motivation_questions.question: How has your worldview shifted over the last 2-3 years? What drove that change - a book, a person, an event, an experience?
motivation_questions.answer: My worldview shifted after reading 'The Art of Communication' by Thich Nhat Hanh and participating in a city youth forum. I realized that communication is not just about talking, but truly listening. Before, I thought that sharing my ideas clearly was the most important thing, but now I notice how much I miss if I don’t listen carefully. At the youth forum, there were people who didn’t speak up much, and in the past, I might not have noticed them. Now, I try to include them and ask for their opinions, both at school and at home. My relationships with my family and friends have changed in small ways because I am more patient and less quick to interrupt.

motivation_questions.question: What do you think Kazakhstan will look like in 20 years - and what role do you believe your generation will play in shaping it?
motivation_questions.answer: Kazakhstan will be more open and creative, with more interconnected communities. I believe my generation will emphasize collaboration, empathy, and social responsibility in leadership. We will hopefully create more inclusive spaces for youth and underrepresented groups to participate. I think things are already changing slowly, like with more people talking about mental health and volunteering, or new art spaces opening in Almaty. Maybe in 20 years, there will be more opportunities for young people to join decision-making, not just in politics but in smaller community projects too. There’s still a lot of challenges, like people sometimes being afraid of change, but my generation might be more willing to try new things. I hope we won’t forget about tradition even as we become more modern.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: Once, my friends made fun of a classmate for being shy. I stood up for her and explained why it was not right. It was uncomfortable and I lost popularity with my group, but I felt it was more important to be kind and fair. Some people thought I was overreacting, and I could tell they didn’t like that I spoke up. But I remembered how it felt when I was left out before, so I wanted to help her. After a while, I noticed that others in the class started including her more in conversations and group work. Seeing her smile and participate made me proud, even if things were a bit awkward with my old friends for a while.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: I started helping a classmate who had trouble speaking in public. I offered to practice presentations together after school, sometimes in empty classrooms or even outside when it was warm. At first, we were both a bit awkward, but slowly she started to speak more confidently. Sometimes I would listen and give small tips, like where to pause or how to make eye contact. In the process, I learned patience and the joy of helping quietly, without praise or anyone even noticing. It also made me realize how supportive actions, even small, can change someone’s experience of school. For me, it didn’t feel like a big deal at the time, but now I see that small encouragements can actually make a real difference.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer:
Kindness: I created the “Kindness Notes” to spread positivity by leaving small handwritten messages on classmates’ desks when I noticed someone seemed down or stressed. Sometimes I wasn’t sure if it helped, but a few people told me it made their day a little better, which encouraged me to keep doing it.  
Inclusivity: I always try to include quieter classmates in group activities, like when I noticed a new student sitting alone during science lab. I invited her to join my group. She didn’t talk much at first, but eventually she shared some good ideas.  
Honesty: I advocated for my team to use original work, not pre-made materials, in school projects, even though it was harder and took more time. Some teammates didn’t like it, but I felt it was more important to be honest.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: I organized group discussion circles and the encouragement campaign at my school. What worked was focusing on personal engagement—I tried to speak directly to people and invite them, and clear communication about what the events were for. I noticed that when I explained the discussion topics in advance, more people seemed interested. What did not work: sometimes participation was low because many students had busy schedules or overlapping commitments. A few times, only three or four people showed up, which made discussions less dynamic. Next time, I would try to better coordinate timing, maybe by checking when most people are free, and use digital communication like group chats to remind and engage more students. I think also asking people for topic suggestions could help.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: I tutored junior students in English and literature, making custom learning plans based on their strengths. The process involved getting to know each student, building trust, and adjusting to their needs. For example, some of my students struggled with grammar, while others found it hard to analyze texts, so I tried to prepare different exercises for each of them. Sometimes I used stories from my own experience to help them relate to the material. The results were measured in their improved confidence and grades—one student’s grade rose from a C to a B, which made me feel like my work mattered. This experience also showed me the value of personalized support and how important patience is when helping others learn.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: I would organize workshops on communication and leadership for high school students in Pavlodar, inviting local role models, and create resources that help amplify youth voices. This would build a supportive network and empower more youth to speak up and take action. With $1,000, I could rent a small space in a community center or school, print materials, and provide snacks to make the workshops more welcoming. I would ask teachers to recommend students who might benefit but are usually shy or overlooked. I think bringing in people from Pavlodar who have made a positive impact—like a young journalist or a social worker—would make the sessions more relatable. I hope this would not only help students develop skills but also show them that their opinions matter, even if they’re young.

text_inputs.interview_text:
My biggest motivation to join inVision U is the sense of purpose—learning how to turn positive energy and inspiration into concrete projects that make a difference. I want to understand how to create value and impact even when you start with few resources. I see myself as someone who can bring people together, motivate, and build a sense of connection and belonging. My soft skills are my greatest strength, and I am ready to develop them further into genuine leadership.

For example, in my school, I was often the one who tried to get classmates involved in group activities, like volunteering for local events or supporting each other during exam periods. But I noticed that sometimes our enthusiasm faded quickly because we didn’t know how to organize or keep things going. That made me realize I need to learn more about how to actually turn ideas into action that lasts. I sometimes struggle with turning ideas into systematic change, so I hope to learn more about project planning, interdisciplinary teamwork, and effective communication for social good. I would like to get better at taking an idea from just a conversation to something with structure and measurable outcomes, even if it’s on a small scale.

Also, I really believe that leadership is not only about giving instructions but about listening, encouraging, and being there for others. I want to show that encouragement and kindness in leadership can change communities, not just top-down decisions. I hope that at inVision U, I will meet like-minded people who also care about this. Thank you for giving me the chance to share my goals.

scenario_meta.notes: Very talented student from Taraz, strong self-driven learning but under-explains and minimizes strengths. Mixed language use, some English-Russian code switching.

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
Здравствуйте, my name is Aizada and I am from Taraz. Since childhood, I was always more interested in mathematics than in other things — not because someone pushed me, but because it just made sense to me. I remember I learned how to solve equations from an old book my grandfather had, and after that I started to look for more in internet. It was a thick, yellowed book, with some pages missing, but it was exciting for me to discover how numbers work together. But most of my school years were spent just trying to keep up, because my family was not so well-off. My parents always worked late, and sometimes I helped my younger brother with his homework, especially in math and sometimes Russian language. We often sat at the kitchen table with just one dim lamp, and I tried to explain things to him with drawings or simple examples.

Luckily, my math teacher noticed my ability and let me borrow higher-grade textbooks. I was shy to ask for help, but she encouraged me and sometimes gave me extra tasks or old magazines with math puzzles. That made me feel like maybe I was not so different. When I found online courses (like Khan Academy), I started to study in the evenings after everyone went to sleep. It wasn’t always easy, because sometimes there was no internet, but I was able to finish most of algebra and geometry on my own, in Russian and in English. I remember one night the electricity went out, so I copied problems to a notebook by candlelight to solve later. Also, sometimes I helped my classmates who had problems, but I never thought it was something special. I didn’t really win any big competitions — only participated in the city olympiad, did OK, got a certificate but nothing more.

I want to study at inVision U because I like the idea of learning not only one subject but several, and working in teams. In my school, most learning was about memorizing and repeating, but I always liked to think for myself and sometimes the teachers didn’t like my questions. Once, I asked why we solve quadratics this way and not another, and the teacher told me to “just do it like in the book.” Sometimes I didn’t even know in which language to write — Kazakh or Russian or English — so I usually mixed. Maybe that’s also why it was harder for me to really show what I know. I wish there was more understanding for students who don’t fit the usual mold. But I hope inVision U is more open to students who think differently and ask questions, not just memorize.

For my future, I want to help children like my brother, who struggle with math and self-confidence. I hope after inVision U, I can go back to Taraz and start free after-school clubs for those who cannot afford tutors. I would like to make learning less stressful and more interesting — maybe with games or small projects, because I saw how my brother improved when we tried to use real-life examples. Maybe I don’t have big awards, but I have big motivation. I want to find friends who are also interested in building new things, maybe even develop something to help schools with fewer resources, because city and village difference is very big in Kazakhstan. Sometimes I think about how many children never get a chance just because they live in a small place or don’t have money. I want to do something to change that, even if it is small. I hope you will see my real motivation, even if my words are simple. Thank you for your consideration.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: Probably the most difficult period was when my father lost his job during COVID, and my mother had to work more. The stress at home was much higher, and I was worried about our future. I coped by focusing on studies and helping my younger brother, because it made me feel useful. We didn’t have always good food, and sometimes I wanted to give up. But over time, I learned to be patient, not expect too much, and to help at home. I think I became more responsible and mature. Now, little things do not scare me, because I managed through that.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: Every evening after dinner, I make time to study a little extra — usually math or sometimes programming from free online resources. It became my habit so I don’t lose the rhythm, especially when school gets busy or when my family needs something. I chose this habit because in Taraz, there is not much after-school activity or tutoring, so I knew I had to learn by myself. Also, I have a habit to write down what I don’t understand during the day and look for answers later, in Russian or English videos.

motivation_questions.question: Was there a time when you worked toward a goal for a long time but never reached it? What did you take away from that experience?
motivation_questions.answer: Yes, I prepared for the oblast math olympiad for two years, but I could not qualify for the final stage. It was disappointing because I worked alone mostly, and sometimes I thought maybe I am not good enough. I remember practicing late at night, solving problems from past years, and still not making it. But I took away the lesson that it is better to try and fail than not try at all. I now know which topics need more attention, like combinatorics and geometry, and I learned to ask for help earlier, maybe from teachers or classmates. Not winning is not the end, I think now. Even if I didn’t achieve my goal, I gained experience and learned more about my limits and how to improve.

motivation_questions.question: Describe a project, idea, or solution you came up with entirely on your own initiative - not as an assignment. Where did the idea come from, and what was the outcome?
motivation_questions.answer: One time, I saw my brother’s friend struggling with times tables, so I made simple flashcards from old cardboard and started practicing with him after school. I didn’t get this idea from a teacher or parent; it just seemed like a way I could help. After a few days, a couple of his classmates joined us too, probably because they heard it was helpful or fun. This was my own idea, not a school assignment or anything. The outcome was positive—they started to improve in math, and I felt like I did something useful, even if it was small. Now they still sometimes ask me for help, especially before tests, and I’m planning to make more basic math club in the neighbourhood for anyone who needs it.

motivation_questions.question: How do you usually make important decisions - do you rely on intuition, data, or advice from others? Walk us through a specific real example from your life.
motivation_questions.answer: I usually first think by myself, and then talk to my mother or my math teacher. For example, when choosing which university programs to apply for, I made a list in both Russian and English of possible places. I looked at their requirements, like language exams and grades, and compared them in a table. After that, I showed my list to my teacher and asked what she thinks about my chances, and if she knows students who went there. I also asked my mother because she knows me well and understands what environment I am comfortable in. Besides the data and advice, I paid attention to my feelings—like, which place feels more friendly for students like me, or if I can imagine myself living in that city. It’s a mix.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever faced a situation where you had to choose between what was efficient and what was right? What did you do, and why?
motivation_questions.answer: Yes, sometimes classmates ask to copy my math homework because they know I usually finish early. It would be easier and faster to just give them the answers, but I think it’s better to explain step by step, even if that takes much longer. For example, last semester one friend was really stressed before a test and wanted to just see my solutions, but instead I tried to show him how to solve the problems. Sometimes they get annoyed or say it’s too slow, but I feel more honest this way. I did this because I don’t feel comfortable with cheating, even if it’s small or if everyone else is doing it. I believe understanding is more important than just finishing quickly.

motivation_questions.question: Is there a problem in your city, country, or the world that genuinely troubles you? Why that one specifically - and what do you think is its root cause?
motivation_questions.answer: Education inequality between city and village students worries me. In Taraz, some schools are OK, but rural areas have old textbooks, not enough teachers. For example, my cousin lives in a small village and her school often runs out of basic materials like chalk or paper. I think the root cause is that resources are not equally distributed, and maybe education reforms do not focus enough on small towns and villages. Sometimes I read news stories about new initiatives for schools, but usually they are for bigger cities like Almaty or Nur-Sultan. Also, digitalization is uneven; not all children have internet or computers. In my grandmother’s village, some students have to share one device for online classes, which is not fair.

motivation_questions.question: How has your worldview shifted over the last 2-3 years? What drove that change - a book, a person, an event, an experience?
motivation_questions.answer: My worldview changed a lot after COVID and after joining online communities where I met students from different cities and countries. Before, I thought everything was only about grades and getting high marks, but now I see there are different ways to learn and help others. For example, I joined a Discord server for students interested in science, and people there shared resources and advice that I never saw in my school. It made me realize learning can happen outside of classrooms too. Also, reading online stories and posts about people who overcome failures made me less afraid to make mistakes and try new things, even if I am not sure I will succeed. I think I am more open-minded now than before.

motivation_questions.question: What do you think Kazakhstan will look like in 20 years - and what role do you believe your generation will play in shaping it?
motivation_questions.answer: I hope Kazakhstan will be more modern, more digital, with less gap between regions. I think my generation will help to create more technologies for education and healthcare, and try to solve social problems. Maybe it will take time, but if more young people get access to good education, it can happen. Right now, there are still many villages and small towns where internet is slow or there are not enough teachers. Sometimes, people in big cities get more chances, but I believe we can change this by making better online platforms or even simple mobile apps for learning. My friends and I talk about these issues often, and some of them want to work in IT or medicine to help. Even small ideas can grow into something bigger over twenty years, so I think step by step, my generation can really make a difference.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: Once, I spoke out in class against a teacher who told a student he would never be good at math. Other students were silent, but I raised my hand and said everyone can improve with support. Some classmates made fun of me later, but I couldn’t keep quiet. I just felt it was wrong. I remember my heart was beating really fast, but I couldn’t just ignore what was happening. The teacher looked surprised and didn’t say anything for a while, which made the room feel tense. After class, a few friends told me I shouldn’t have said anything because it might make the teacher angry at us. But I thought about how the student who was criticized must have felt, and I hoped maybe my words helped, even a little.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: As I wrote earlier, I helped my brother’s friend with math, and later a few others joined. At first, it was just basic algebra, but then I tried to explain geometry too, which I found harder. I liked to see them understand new things, especially when someone would say, “Oh, now I get it.” It gave me more patience, because sometimes we had to repeat topics or find different ways to talk about them. I felt I could make a small difference, even if it was just for homework or test preparation. It’s sometimes tiring, but rewarding, especially when they did better in class or just felt less stressed about school. It also made me realize that teaching is not easy, but it’s valuable.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer:
1. Honesty: I never let others copy my work, even if it’s easier. For example, last year in math class, a friend asked to see my homework answers before class, but I refused because I think it’s not fair for others or for myself. Sometimes it makes me feel awkward, but I still think it’s important.

2. Responsibility: During difficult family times, I helped at home and with my brother’s school. My parents were both busy working late, so I made dinner and also checked my younger brother’s homework every evening. Sometimes I was tired, but I knew it was my duty to support my family when they needed me.

3. Curiosity: I always look for new ways to learn, for example self-studying programming even before school lessons. I watched YouTube tutorials about Python and tried small projects like making a calculator. Even when I didn’t understand something, I would search for explanations online or ask older friends. This made me realize I like discovering things by myself.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: I started an informal math club for neighbours’ children, just practicing together. It was not official – sometimes kids would not come or parents were busy, so attendance was unpredictable. What worked: simple games, flashcards, and sometimes even drawing numbers with chalk outside. The more playful the activity, the more kids seemed interested. What did not: trying to do too many topics at once, or making it too much like school. I also noticed that some kids got bored if it was too easy, while others struggled if it was too hard. Next time, I would focus on one topic per session and maybe use more digital materials if available, like short math videos or interactive websites, to keep things more engaging and flexible.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: I did some small tutoring in math for two students in my area. Parents paid me a little each week, and I used that to buy books. I made lesson plans and tried to make learning fun. Sometimes I used games or small quizzes, because I noticed the students got bored with just worksheets. It was not a big business or anything, but it helped me feel more independent and responsible. The result was that students improved, and I learned more about teaching, like how to explain things in different ways if someone didn’t understand right away. It also made me realize that earning money, even if just a little, takes effort and patience. I think the experience was valuable for me.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: I would buy basic tablets or used laptops for children in my neighbourhood who don’t have access to online learning. Then, I would organize free workshops for them to learn digital skills and basic math. I know from experience how important technology access can be for learning, so I want to give that opportunity to others. When I was younger, I struggled to keep up in class because we had only one computer at home, and it was usually busy. For some kids here, even that is not possible. With $1,000, I could maybe buy three or four devices if I look for good deals, and set up a small shared space, like in the community center, so the kids can use them safely. I would ask older students or even parents to help supervise. I think even short workshops could help spark interest in technology and make school less intimidating.

text_inputs.interview_text:
I am not very good at talking about myself, especially in formal interviews, but I will try. To describe myself, I think I am someone who quietly gets things done, even if it’s not visible to everyone. Most people don’t notice the students who always sit in the back, but I believe sometimes those students have the strongest desire to make a difference. I am not a leader in the usual sense — more the person who supports others, helps with homework, tries to solve small problems first.

In my city, not everyone cares about education, especially for girls. My own family was supportive, but sometimes I had to argue for why I wanted to study instead of do housework. That is why I am very interested in joining a university like inVision U, which values students who think differently and want to do real projects. If I am accepted, I may need help with some English words, but I promise I will learn fast.

I don’t know yet exactly what I want to be — maybe a teacher, maybe a programmer — but I do know I want my work to have positive impact for real people, even in small ways. Thank you for considering me.

scenario_meta.notes: Astana-based, strong English, over-polished but shallow real achievements. Writes in a clear, confident tone.

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
Dear inVision U Admissions Team,

I am writing to express my deep interest in joining inVision U as a scholarship undergraduate, as I strongly believe it is the perfect environment for innovative learning and real-world impact. Growing up in Astana, I have always been exposed to a vibrant, modern city filled with opportunities and inspiration. This background has helped me develop an international outlook, adapt quickly, and connect with different types of people. I often find myself reflecting on how Astana’s rapid growth and diversity have shaped my open-mindedness and willingness to try new things, whether it’s joining a club with students from different backgrounds or learning to navigate unexpected situations in the city.

My main interest is the intersection between business and communications. In high school, I actively sought out leadership opportunities: I organized school debates, managed student social media pages, and even helped set up a charity bake sale that raised money for a children’s home. Each of these experiences taught me something different. For example, when organizing the debates, I learned how to listen to opposing viewpoints and mediate disagreements. Managing the social media pages taught me about digital branding, timing, and audience engagement, though I often struggled to keep the content fresh and relatable for everyone. The bake sale was not only about raising money, but also about convincing classmates to participate and coordinate their efforts. Through these experiences, I realized the power of communication, organization, and purposeful teamwork. I thrive best when surrounded by energetic people collaborating on meaningful projects.

Although I have yet to launch something on a national scale, I believe my biggest asset is how I motivate others and bring people together. I am very comfortable communicating in English and in international contexts, and I intend to use this skill both in my studies and in future projects. Last year, I participated in a virtual exchange program where I worked remotely with students from Germany and Turkey. We created a presentation about climate change in Central Asia, and even though it was challenging to organize meetings across time zones, I enjoyed finding common ground and helping others feel included in the work. Experiences like this continue to reinforce my interest in working in diverse teams and learning from people with different perspectives.

inVision U’s interdisciplinary approach attracts me most, as I want to explore different domains — for example, applying business strategies to solve social problems, or improving regional educational access through creative campaigns and community engagement. I am especially interested in courses and projects that will push me to think critically and step outside my comfort zone. I realize that real impact often comes from combining knowledge from different fields, and I am eager to learn from professors and peers who share this mindset.

After my studies, I would like to found a social impact organization that bridges urban and rural divides in Kazakhstan, especially related to education and youth opportunity. My country is at a stage of transformation, and I believe my generation, equipped with modern skills and bold thinking, can shape the future. I do not have all the answers yet, but I am ready to ask questions, experiment, and learn from my mistakes along the way. 

Thank you very much for considering my application. I am excited to hopefully take part in the inVision U community and to make a real difference both on campus and beyond.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: The most challenging period for me was during the height of online schooling in the pandemic. I struggled with staying motivated and managing my time because I am definitely a more social learner and missed in-person connection. I coped by creating a strict daily schedule with regular breaks and organizing weekly video calls with friends where we discussed class topics and shared our struggles. This period showed me how much I value teamwork and how important it is to support one another. Since then, I have become a lot more disciplined and proactive about reaching out when things get tough.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: Each morning, I review my planner and set three key priorities for the day. I also try to exercise for at least 30 minutes, which helps my focus and energy. I started these habits after reading about productivity, and they really keep me on track. For example, if I have a big assignment due, breaking it into a priority for the day makes it less overwhelming. By being intentional about my daily routines, I have noticed I meet my goals more consistently and feel much more motivated. Sometimes I miss a day, but overall these habits help me avoid procrastination. Choosing these routines was inspired by wanting to manage my time better and perform at my best both in and out of school. I think having some structure helps me feel less stressed.

motivation_questions.question: Was there a time when you worked toward a goal for a long time but never reached it? What did you take away from that experience?
motivation_questions.answer: Last year, I tried to qualify for the national debate competition team. I trained, practiced, and studied debate tactics for months, but ultimately I was not selected. It was disappointing and frustrating, especially after spending so much time preparing and hoping for a different outcome. At first, I questioned whether the effort had been worth it. But looking back, I learned that failure is a chance for growth. I realized I enjoy debate for the learning and connection, not only for winning. Some of my favorite moments were actually the long nights discussing topics with teammates, or getting feedback from coaches, even if I didn’t make the team. I am now less afraid of setbacks and embrace trying new things, knowing that the experience itself matters. This has made me more open-minded and persistent, even when things do not turn out as I planned.

motivation_questions.question: Describe a project, idea, or solution you came up with entirely on your own initiative - not as an assignment. Where did the idea come from, and what was the outcome?
motivation_questions.answer: I noticed that many of my classmates didn’t have a central place to share notes and updates, so I started a student-run Telegram channel for our grade. The idea came after several group messages got lost or buried in our usual chats, which made it difficult to find important information. I set up the channel, created some basic guidelines—like keeping posts relevant to schoolwork and being respectful—and shared an invite link with everyone in our grade. At first, only a few people joined, but gradually more students started using it to share their own study materials and reminders about deadlines. It became really popular and even teachers started submitting announcements through it. Communication improved, and students felt more connected, especially during exam periods when everyone needed quick updates.

motivation_questions.question: How do you usually make important decisions - do you rely on intuition, data, or advice from others? Walk us through a specific real example from your life.
motivation_questions.answer: For important decisions, I usually start with research (data), then consult family or mentors, and finally trust my own intuition. For example, when I had to choose between two summer programs last year, I made a pros and cons list for each, looking into course topics, length, and cost. I also asked my older cousin, who had attended one of the programs before, for advice and insights about her experience. Besides that, I spent time reading reviews online from past participants to see what they liked or didn’t like. In the end, after gathering all this information, I noticed that one program just felt more exciting and more aligned with my personal interests in technology, so I followed my gut. This blended approach works for me and gives confidence that I’m making the right call, especially when decisions feel overwhelming at first.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever faced a situation where you had to choose between what was efficient and what was right? What did you do, and why?
motivation_questions.answer: There was a time during group project work when some teammates wanted to submit a presentation copied from the internet to save time. I insisted we create our own content, even if it took longer. At first, my suggestion wasn’t popular. Some group members argued that everyone else did it and that our grade mattered most. But I believe honesty and originality are more important than shortcuts, even if it means extra effort. We ended up producing authentic work, meeting at the library several times to brainstorm and divide tasks. It was hard and stressful because we had other assignments, but our teacher noticed and praised our integrity. I felt proud for standing up for what was right, even if it wasn’t the easiest choice.

motivation_questions.question: Is there a problem in your city, country, or the world that genuinely troubles you? Why that one specifically - and what do you think is its root cause?
motivation_questions.answer: Education inequality in Kazakhstan bothers me the most. Students in rural areas are often left behind due to lack of resources or qualified teachers. I have seen this with my own relatives who live in a small village—they don’t have access to good internet or new textbooks, and sometimes classes are canceled because there aren’t enough teachers. I believe the root cause is uneven development and insufficient investment in modernizing all regions equally. Big cities like Almaty or Astana get more attention, while remote areas seem almost forgotten. This is a fundamental issue because it prevents young people from reaching their full potential and deepens social divisions. When some children get better opportunities just because of where they are born, it feels unfair and frustrating.

motivation_questions.question: How has your worldview shifted over the last 2-3 years? What drove that change - a book, a person, an event, an experience?
motivation_questions.answer: Meeting exchange students from different countries made me realize how interconnected the world is and how diverse people’s perspectives can be. Before, I saw things mostly from a Kazakhstani view, shaped by my family, school, and local news. Talking with these students, I noticed how their experiences and opinions differed from mine, especially when we discussed topics like education, traditions, or even small daily routines. Now, I appreciate global challenges and the importance of collaboration. This shift was driven not only by conversations with these students, but also by reading more international news and articles online. Sometimes I still find it hard to fully understand other viewpoints, but I’m more open-minded and eager to learn from others than I was before.

motivation_questions.question: What do you think Kazakhstan will look like in 20 years - and what role do you believe your generation will play in shaping it?
motivation_questions.answer: In 20 years, I believe Kazakhstan will be a modern, innovative society with strong digital infrastructure and more equal opportunities. My generation will be the bridge, bringing new ideas and leading changes in education, business, and technology. We will challenge old ways, push for transparency, and work together for a fairer future. I hope to be part of that movement through social entrepreneurship and community projects. For example, I imagine more young people starting tech initiatives in smaller towns, making digital tools accessible even outside big cities like Almaty. I think we will also focus on environmental issues, since many people my age are concerned about pollution and climate change here. By collaborating and sharing knowledge, maybe we can create a society where everyone feels included, not just in cities but in rural areas too.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: During a class discussion on volunteering, many classmates thought it was boring or a waste of time. I shared my positive experience from organizing charity sales and encouraged others to get involved. Some people rolled their eyes or said it’s just something teachers make us do, but I wanted to show how volunteer work really does make a difference. For example, when I helped organize a used book sale, I saw how the funds we raised went to buy supplies for a local orphanage. That felt meaningful to me, even if it wasn’t popular. I believe it’s important to be true to your values even if others disagree. Standing up for something you care about can feel isolating, but I think it’s worth it.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: Last winter, I volunteered at a children’s home, organizing games and reading stories to orphans. I chose to do this because I wanted to brighten their holidays. I remember one moment when I read them a story about friendship, and a little girl asked me to read it again. It was truly eye-opening and helped me become more empathetic. I started to notice how much the kids valued even a little bit of attention and kindness, like when they would run up to me to show a drawing or ask for help with a puzzle. Seeing their smiles and gratitude was a reminder that even small acts can have a big impact, and it motivated me to find more ways to help those in need. I think the experience made me more patient and aware of how important it is to give time to others, especially people who might feel forgotten.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer: My three core values are integrity, initiative, and kindness. For integrity, I once refused to participate in a copied group project, even though it made things awkward with the group and I got some criticism. It felt right to be honest, even if it was uncomfortable. For initiative, I started a student Telegram channel to help my classmates keep track of announcements and deadlines, since everyone was confused by emails and missing things. I organized the group, set up reminders, and helped new students join. For kindness, I volunteer at a children’s home in my city. I also talked to classmates and invited them to come with me, hoping to make volunteering more regular and not just a one-time thing.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: Yes, I organized a bake sale for charity and managed our school’s student-run Telegram channel. The bake sale succeeded because of clear roles and wide promotion. For example, I made posters and asked classmates to share the event with their friends, and we managed to raise more money than expected. The Telegram channel was popular but sometimes got irrelevant posts; people would share memes or off-topic stuff, which made important messages harder to find. Next time, I’d set stricter rules from the start, like guidelines about what can be posted and maybe appoint moderators. I learned that clear expectations and feedback loops make all the difference in student initiatives. If I organize something again, I’d probably ask for feedback sooner and adjust as needed.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: I ran a small online shop on Instagram selling handmade phone cases. I did market research, designed a brand, and used my communication skills for marketing. Though it was not a big venture, I sold out my first stock and learned a lot about marketing and customer relations. For example, I learned how to answer customers’ questions and deal with some complaints, which was harder than I expected. Sometimes I had to remake phone cases if the customer was not satisfied, and I realized how important it is to listen carefully. It boosted my confidence and taught me the basics of small-scale entrepreneurship. I also learned to keep track of small expenses and profits, and how to present products in a way that would attract people on social media.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: I would organize a series of youth leadership workshops in rural schools with that budget: covering supplies, travel, and meals. The goal would be to empower students with modern communication and critical thinking skills usually lacking outside big cities. For example, I would focus on activities like small debates, teamwork exercises, and maybe some public speaking practice. I remember how much I learned the first time someone taught me how to present my ideas clearly, and I think rural students deserve the same chance. Even if only a few students are inspired to start little projects or take initiative in their schools, I think that’s valuable. This experience might also help me understand more about the challenges they face and how best to support them.

text_inputs.interview_text:
I see myself as a connector — someone who brings people together for a purpose. In school, I always volunteered to lead projects, mediate class disagreements, and serve as the voice for my classmates. I am passionate about public speaking, business, and digital communication, and I try to use those skills to drive positive change, big or small. What makes me different, I think, is my drive to motivate a team: I get a lot of energy from seeing people around me succeed, and I like to celebrate others’ contributions.

I am also quite reflective. Looking back, I know I am privileged to have access to good resources and family support, so I want to channel that advantage into building something larger than myself. At inVision U, I would love to work on interdisciplinary projects, especially those dealing with educational access and youth skills development. I believe real change begins with small, organized actions, and I am not afraid to take the first step.

My greatest wish is to be part of a learning community where everyone shares and grows together — and where I can contribute both my strengths and my learning to the group’s success. Thank you for the opportunity to apply.

scenario_meta.notes: Russian speaker from Ust-Kamenogorsk, somewhat disorganized, uneven tone, good in sport but jumps topics.

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
Здравствуйте! Меня зовут Илья, я из Усть-Каменогорска. Люблю спорт, но пишу это письмо не только из-за спорта. В нашей семье всегда было непросто: когда я был в 9 классе, у мамы обнаружили серьезную болезнь, и мне пришлось рано повзрослеть. У меня как будто разделилась жизнь на "до" и "после". В школе я не был отличником, по многим предметам средне, но в футболе и баскетболе показывал хорошие результаты. Долгое время считал, что это моя единственная сильная сторона.

Когда начались сложности в семье, я перестал участвовать в соревнованиях, потому что стал помогать по дому и ещё занимался с младшим братом, чтобы он не забрасывал школу. Иногда приходилось самому готовить еду, делать уборку, покупать продукты или сопровождать брата на занятия. Было сложно совмещать это с учёбой, и часто я уставал настолько, что просто не хотелось ни с кем разговаривать. В это же время заметил, что нам в районе не хватает нормальных площадок и спортинвентаря. Часто мы с друзьями играли на асфальте, мяч был один на всех и старый, даже сетки на воротах не было.

С друзьями мы решили попросить местных предпринимателей о помощи — собрали подписи, объяснили, зачем это нужно. Было неловко обращаться к взрослым, но я понимал, что если не мы, то никто за нас не сделает. В итоге выделили деньги на ремонт одной старой спортплощадки, и мы её реально расчистили и покрасили. Помню, как отмывали ржавый турник и таскали краску в ведрах через полгорода. Через месяц там начали проходить не только наши дворовые матчи, но и тренировки для младших школьников. Я стал помогать тренировать ребят 8–10 лет, и, если честно, это дало мне почувствовать, что могу быть полезен не только для себя. Было приятно, когда кто-то из малышей после тренировки говорил "спасибо" или просто улыбался. Я не считаю себя каким-то особенным организатором, но тогда впервые увидел, что своими действиями могу менять что-то вокруг.

inVision U заинтересовал меня тем, что там ценят междисциплинарность и социальные проекты. Я еще не уверен, кем хочу быть — тренером, педагогом, может, вообще экологом, потому что участвовал в нескольких акциях по уборке берега Иртыша. Помню, как мы с одноклассниками собирали мусор в мешки, а потом обсуждали, почему люди оставляют за собой столько отходов. Мне нравится быть частью команды, когда вместе можно что-то улучшить, даже если это кажется мелочью.

Иногда мне кажется, что мои достижения слишком разрозненные, что я ничем не могу по-настоящему похвастаться. Например, у меня нет дипломов олимпиад или медалей, но есть опыт быть ответственным за других и не сдаваться, когда тяжело. Но, может быть, именно здесь я научусь связывать свои интересы и делать что-то большее. Я хочу понять, как мои увлечения спортом, заботой о природе и помощь другим могут сочетаться и перерасти во что-то полезное для общества. Надеюсь, что у вас есть место для таких, как я — кто еще ищет свой путь, кто не идеальный, но очень хочет меняться и приносить пользу. Если получится учиться в inVision U, это будет мой шанс найти себя и, может быть, однажды сделать жизнь вокруг немного лучше.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
