motivation_questions.answer: I think Kazakhstan will be more urban and developed, perhaps more digital and with better infrastructure. It seems likely that cities will grow and maybe there will be more startups or IT companies, especially since the government is investing in this area. My generation will probably play a leading role in technology adoption and managing new challenges, like adapting to automation or climate problems. I hope we will contribute to more equal opportunities in fields like science and technology, especially for people from smaller towns who don’t always get the same resources. But the actual direction will depend on many factors, like the political situation, global economics, and even how open people are to change. Sometimes progress can also bring new inequalities or unexpected issues.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: Once there was pressure in a study group to share answers before a test, but I declined and instead tried to motivate others to learn together by solving problems as a group. Some people were annoyed because I was not 'helpful' in the usual way, and a couple of friends even stopped talking to me for a while. A few others agreed with me, though, and we studied together more honestly, working through difficult math problems and explaining concepts to each other. I was driven by my views on integrity, which I think are important even if it makes things awkward. Looking back, it was uncomfortable being the only one to say no, but I still feel it was the right thing to do.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: I tutored my cousin in math ahead of her university entrance exams. She was struggling and anxious, so I spent weekends reviewing problems with her. Nobody asked me to do it, but I wanted her to have the chance I had. Sometimes she would get frustrated with certain algebra topics, and I had to find different ways to explain the same idea. We even used little drawings and real-life examples, like figuring out the price of groceries or dividing up snacks to make sense of fractions. I learned patience and also found satisfaction in explaining things at a simpler level. It made me realize how much work goes into understanding something new, and I felt more connected to her after.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer: First, honesty: I always refuse to cheat or share answers, even under pressure. Sometimes this means I get lower grades than I could, but I feel better knowing I stuck to my values. Second, dedication: I devote many hours to master topics for competitions, like last year when I spent almost every evening practicing math problems instead of relaxing. Third, reliability: whenever I agree to help someone study, I never cancel unless absolutely necessary. For example, my cousin knew she could count on me to keep every tutoring session, even if it meant rescheduling my own plans. I think these values guide me in daily life, not just in big decisions, and sometimes it’s hard but important.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: I co-organized a short mathematics circle for juniors at my school. We ran problem sessions and helped them with olympiad training. What worked was trying to make the sessions fun and interactive, like using games or group challenges, which seemed to keep the students engaged. What did not work well was advertising, so only a small group joined and some sessions felt too quiet. We mostly relied on word of mouth and a few posters in the hallway, but it wasn’t enough. Next time I would try to reach out to more students earlier and maybe use digital announcements, like the school website or a group chat, to get more attention. I also think asking teachers to recommend students could help.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: I tutored a couple of neighborhood students in algebra for a small fee, mainly during summer. I prepared worksheets and explained concepts. Sometimes, I had to find different ways to explain topics like quadratic equations or factoring, since each student learned a bit differently. The result was that both students improved grades, and I earned enough to buy study materials, like some textbooks and notebooks I needed for my own studies. It was challenging sometimes, especially if a student got frustrated, but I learned to be patient and to try breaking problems down into smaller steps. It showed me that I can apply my academic skills to help others, and also that teaching can be rewarding in more ways than just earning money.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: I would use the money to buy math and science learning kits for under-resourced rural schools near Astana, then organize weekend workshops for students and teachers. This would create value by increasing access to quality materials and support, which is currently lacking. I believe early exposure to engaging STEM resources can inspire more students. Sometimes, I’ve seen teachers in rural schools struggle to make experiments work with only very basic tools, and students get bored because it’s all theory. With kits—like basic robotics sets or chemistry experiment boxes—even a single, hands-on project could make a difference. I’d ask teachers what topics they want help with, and maybe bring some university students to help run activities. $1,000 isn’t huge, but if it gets a few students excited about science, I think it is worth it.

text_inputs.interview_text:
Hello. I chose to apply for inVision U mainly because my teachers suggested I could benefit from a more practical, team-focused learning environment. While I am confident in my academic skills, I realize I do not have a lot of experience outside classical education—most of my activities were related to exams, solving math problems, and preparation for olympiads. For example, last year I spent most of my free time preparing for the National Math Olympiad in Kazakhstan, which meant a lot of individual study and not much group work. I also participated in a few online math forums, but those interactions were usually limited to discussing specific problems rather than working together for a common outcome.

I often prefer to work alone, and find teamwork sometimes challenging, but I am willing to develop that side. For instance, in group projects at school, I sometimes struggle to communicate my ideas or take initiative when I am unsure. I do recognize that my focus has always been knowledge for its own sake rather than for an immediate impact. I am not sure yet what broader project I would want to build, but maybe I will find such ideas once I am in a community of motivated peers. I am open to learning from others, improving my soft skills, and seeing what I can do outside my usual comfort zone. I hope that by putting myself in unfamiliar situations, I can learn to collaborate better and find out how my academic strengths can contribute to something more practical.

Thank you for reading my application. I am open to interview questions and honest feedback.

scenario_meta.notes: Entrepreneurial, resourceful, demonstrates strong impact and self-awareness. Mixes English and Russian as appropriate.

text_inputs.motivation_letter_text:
Hello! Меня зовут Адиль, I’m from Kokshetau. I grew up in a mono-town on the edge of the city where almost everyone relies on one enterprise or the other. For as long as I remember, I was that person who needed to find a way around barriers — whether it was figuring out why the computer club always closed early, or why we didn’t have proper chemistry labs like schools in Astana.

When I was younger, it always frustrated me that we were told to "just accept it" when things were missing or broken. Sometimes I would go to the local library to look for solutions, but most of the books were outdated or missing pages. Still, I remember copying out diagrams of old computers by hand, just to understand how the parts worked together. At home, I would try to fix broken radios or take apart old phones, even though I rarely succeeded. I think that habit of tinkering and trying anyway stuck with me.

By age 15, I launched a small weekend workshop for neighborhood teens using leftover computer parts — we assembled basic PCs, showed how to install Linux, and even set up our own local Wi-Fi chatroom (working around the spotty internet). The idea came after seeing so many bored, talented kids spending hours at the bus stop because they thought tech was something only those in “big cities” can do. At first, only two people showed up, but after a few weeks, more started coming, even a couple of girls who said they’d never touched a computer before. It wasn’t always smooth: sometimes we’d run out of working RAM or have to stop because of a blackout. But it was never about grades — for me, it was about making real options visible.

I’m applying to inVision U because it is the first place where I see both a focus on deep learning and the tools to build genuine projects. I want to be somewhere my drive to create is valued, but also where there’s mentorship and real teamwork. Previously, I often had to act solo – even my teachers sometimes said "не высовывайся, этим тут не живут". That phrase always stuck with me, and it made me question why curiosity or initiative was seen as showing off. But I believe things can be different. I want to bring change not just to our street, but to similar towns all across Kazakhstan. Sometimes I imagine what would happen if every village had even a small tech club, or if kids didn’t have to leave their hometown to learn something new.

After a summer internship with a regional startup, I realized my true motivation is building opportunities for those who are overlooked — connecting rural youth with new skills and networks. At the internship, I worked mostly on basic database tasks, but what stayed with me was how the founders talked about their first failures and how they learned from them. It made me less afraid to admit I don’t know everything yet. I know I still have gaps: I want to be challenged, to face critique, and to learn how to scale impact beyond my circle. inVision U seems to be a place where experimenting and failing forward is not punished, but seen as part of the process — and that’s exactly what I’ve been looking for.

If admitted, I plan to collaborate across disciplines, and create something that can be exported far beyond Kokshetau. I want to meet people who have different backgrounds and learn how they tackle problems with limited resources. I’d also like to try sharing some of the DIY methods I’ve picked up, maybe by running a small workshop or helping organize an event. But most of all, I hope to return with a toolkit for others like me, so we don’t have to wait for big-city solutions. Thank you.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about the most difficult period in your life - what happened, how did you cope, and what changed in you afterwards?
motivation_questions.answer: The toughest moment was when my dad lost his job after the plant downsizing — overnight, most families on our street were struggling. Our internet was cut, heating bills piled up. I started freelancing online (making simple websites for small shops in town) to help cover basics, but it was scary. I coped by taking responsibility much earlier than planned, but it also woke me up: I became independent, learned to negotiate with adults, and stopped waiting for ideal conditions. That’s when I realized you can’t wait — you have to act, even if nobody thinks you’re ready.

motivation_questions.question: What habits or daily routines have you deliberately built for yourself, and how do they impact your results? Why did you choose specifically those?
motivation_questions.answer: Every morning I journal for 10 minutes (in Russian, sometimes in English), reflecting on yesterday: what worked, what failed, and what I want to test next. I keep a spreadsheet of experiments (from code snippets to outreach messages), and I try to update it every evening, even if it’s just a quick note. I built these habits because otherwise it’s too easy to forget small, but important lessons from each attempt—details like which email subject got a reply, or which coding approach saved me an hour. Journaling and iteration help me stay grounded and not get discouraged by setbacks—something that happens a lot when you build with little support. These routines don’t make failure less frequent, but they make it less scary, because I see progress over time.

motivation_questions.question: Was there a time when you worked toward a goal for a long time but never reached it? What did you take away from that experience?
motivation_questions.answer: Yes, I spent almost a year trying to get our local akimat to support a youth community tech hub (I wrote proposals, talked to officials, got signatures), but in the end they said "интересно, но не сейчас, нет средств". At first, I thought my approach was wrong, but I realized that sometimes, systemic inertia is not your fault. I learned to pivot — instead, we partnered with a local small business for space. Now, I know not to put all my hopes on a single route and to look for allies outside the obvious.

motivation_questions.question: Describe a project, idea, or solution you came up with entirely on your own initiative - not as an assignment. Where did the idea come from, and what was the outcome?
motivation_questions.answer: When schools closed for quarantine, many classmates lost access to teachers and internet. I set up a free offline local network with educational materials (using old routers), so neighbors could share resources even without internet. The idea came from seeing how pirated flash drives with movies already traveled house-to-house — I thought, why not do the same with lessons and textbooks? I collected scanned books, video lectures, and practice sheets, and showed families how to connect their phones or laptops to the network. Outcome: at least 20 kids used it, and now some parents want to build a more permanent "knowledge hub" for future breaks. I learned that simple solutions using what’s available can make a difference, even if it’s not perfect.

motivation_questions.question: How do you usually make important decisions - do you rely on intuition, data, or advice from others? Walk us through a specific real example from your life.
motivation_questions.answer: I mix all three. For example, when debating whether to apply for an internship outside Kokshetau, I checked transport options, housing costs, and even food prices (data), talked to my mom and mentor (advice), and then sat with the feeling — excitement mixed with fear. My intuition said, if I'm scared but interested, it's worth trying. I remember making a list of pros and cons, but in the end, the nervous curiosity won. My mom was worried about safety, but my mentor encouraged me to be brave. It worked out: that internship changed my view on what’s possible for rural youth. Now, even for smaller choices, like joining a debate club or volunteering, I notice I repeat the same process: look at facts, ask for opinions, then check how I feel.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever faced a situation where you had to choose between what was efficient and what was right? What did you do, and why?
motivation_questions.answer: Yes — during a school fundraising event, someone suggested we "adjust" our reports to look more successful. It would’ve been easier, but I argued against it. We showed the real, less impressive numbers. Some were disappointed, but I believe honesty is more important than looking good. In the long term, trust matters more for community projects. I remember how awkward it felt to stand up and tell my classmates that we shouldn't change the numbers, even though some people thought it was just a small thing. I worried that others might blame me if the project was seen as a failure. But I realized that if we started lying now, it could become a habit, and eventually nobody would trust our group again. Even if it meant facing disappointment in the short term, I wanted to make sure we were reliable and fair.

motivation_questions.question: Is there a problem in your city, country, or the world that genuinely troubles you? Why that one specifically - and what do you think is its root cause?
motivation_questions.answer: I’m troubled by the lack of access to technology and modern skills in small towns and rural Kazakhstan. This leads to youth believing their only options are migration or low-skilled work. The root? Unequal investment, lack of role models, and a stereotype that 'innovation' is for big cities. This cycle leads to wasted potential. For example, in my own small town, most students have never seen a 3D printer or attended a coding class. Some of my friends think that learning things like robotics or digital design is pointless unless you move to Almaty or Astana. I think a lot about how many talented people just give up or leave, not because they want to, but because they feel there’s no point trying. I want to break it.

motivation_questions.question: How has your worldview shifted over the last 2-3 years? What drove that change - a book, a person, an event, an experience?
motivation_questions.answer: It changed a lot after reading “Factfulness” (English) and after my own failed attempts at local government advocacy. I learned the world is more complicated than it seems. For example, “Factfulness” showed me how statistics can be misused, and how progress is rarely as fast or dramatic as news headlines make it sound. My advocacy experience—trying to push for a recycling initiative in my city, but running into endless bureaucracy and indifference—was frustrating. Still, it made me realize that change is slow, but small, consistent action beats waiting for radical shifts from above. Now, instead of feeling discouraged, I try to focus on the little things I can do, and this perspective made me more patient and persistent, less cynical about setbacks.

motivation_questions.question: What do you think Kazakhstan will look like in 20 years - and what role do you believe your generation will play in shaping it?
motivation_questions.answer: In 20 years, Kazakhstan will be far more digital but regional gaps may still exist. My generation’s job is to shrink those divides — by building networks that reach beyond Astana and Almaty. We will be the connectors, the ones moving ideas and skills outward, not just inward. I want to be part of that – making sure no bright kid feels left out due to geography. Sometimes, when I talk to friends from smaller towns, they feel like everything happens far away from them, especially in tech or science. Maybe it will be mentoring online or starting projects that include rural schools. Even now, I see young people trying to share resources through social media groups, but it’s not enough. I believe with more support and visibility, we can make sure every region has a chance to contribute to the country’s growth.

motivation_questions.question: Recall a moment when you took a stance or did something that was unpopular among the people around you. What drove you to do it?
motivation_questions.answer: Once, I challenged a common racist joke among classmates — I spoke up, and for a while, I was seen as 'тот, кто всё усложняет'. People would give me weird looks or just roll their eyes, and I felt isolated for a few days. But I couldn’t let it slide, because I’ve seen how silence leads to normalization. Even if it was just a joke, I remembered hearing similar things said to my friend in middle school, and how much it hurt her. Over time, some started reflecting, and at least in our group the jokes stopped. It was uncomfortable but necessary for our environment. I still wonder if I could have said it differently, but I don’t regret saying something at all.

motivation_questions.question: Tell us about a person or community you helped - not because you had to, but because you genuinely wanted to. What did that experience give you?
motivation_questions.answer: I mentored a younger neighbor who was always curious about computers but too shy to join clubs or ask questions in class. He would watch me working on my laptop from afar, but never really approached anyone. One day I invited him over and we started learning together at my kitchen table. We worked together on small projects, like setting up a Raspberry Pi to play retro games, and gradually he got more confident. Now he even helps other kids troubleshoot their problems at school, which makes me happy. This experience taught me the value of showing up consistently, not just giving advice or guiding from a distance. I realized how even a small effort from one person can quietly trigger a chain reaction in a community and help people open up.

motivation_questions.question: What are your three core values - and can you give one real example from your life for each, showing how it actually showed up in what you did?
motivation_questions.answer: Resourcefulness — I reused old tech and hustled to run free workshops. For example, I collected broken laptops from relatives and managed to repair a few, so more people could join. Integrity — I insisted on honest reporting even when it was "невыгодно". Once, I reported a budgeting mistake that made our team lose a small grant, but it felt right. Empathy — instead of just 'teaching', I listened to what youth in our area actually needed, adapting projects for their real context, not my assumptions. I remember one boy wanted to learn about video editing, not coding, so I helped him find resources and changed our session plans. These values shaped how I work and connect with others.

motivation_questions.question: Have you ever organized, launched, or promoted something - an event, an initiative, a small business, a club? What worked, what did not, and what would you do differently?
motivation_questions.answer: Yes, I built and promoted a zero-budget "Open Tech" club. What worked was personal outreach — talking to people face-to-face. People responded better to informal chats than to flyers or emails. What didn’t: relying on official school channels (too slow, bureaucratic). For example, waiting for the administration to approve a classroom took weeks, and by then, some students lost interest. I also underestimated how much students wanted hands-on experience, not just lectures. Next time, I’d partner with NGOs earlier for access to equipment and legitimacy. NGOs can sometimes help with things like laptops or even speakers, which would make activities more engaging. I’d also try to get feedback sooner instead of assuming what people want from the club.

motivation_questions.question: How have you earned money or created something of value before applying to university? Walk us through the idea, the process, and the result.
motivation_questions.answer: I freelanced by building websites and doing tech support for local businesses — started with social media ads, built trust, delivered results. For example, I helped a local bakery set up their first online menu, and another time fixed computers at a small grocery store. This grew into a micro-startup with classmates, now we split small contracts. Sometimes the projects are really small, like updating a Facebook page or installing antivirus, but we treat them seriously. Besides income, it gave us entrepreneurial skills and confidence to pitch to adults. I learned how to write simple proposals, talk to clients who are much older, and manage schedules when school got busy. It’s not much, but it felt meaningful.

motivation_questions.question: If you were given $1,000 and one month to do something meaningful - not necessarily to make a profit, but to create real value - what would you do and why?
motivation_questions.answer: I’d run a pop-up tech bootcamp for rural teens from northern oblasts, covering logistics and materials. Purpose: to expose those with zero access to coding/electronics to hands-on practice and local mentors. After seeing what’s possible, many would try more — it’s a seed for future networks, not just a one-off event. I remember meeting students in Kostanay who had never seen a microcontroller, even though they were curious about computers. With $1,000, I could buy simple kits—like Arduinos, breadboards, wires—and bring in a few university volunteers as mentors. The workshop would be practical, maybe building simple robots or weather stations. I want to show them technology isn’t out of reach, and even a single weekend can change someone's direction if they meet the right person or tool.

text_inputs.interview_text:
Hi and добрый день. If there’s one thing you should know about me: I’m relentless about not letting circumstances define my limits. Growing up in Kokshetau’s industrial suburb, most people expected me to do just enough, get a job at the plant, maybe leave if lucky. But from early on, I was fascinated by the idea that technology and networks could be a bridge — if not for me, then at least for my peers.

I’ve already tried (sometimes failed) to build tech initiatives locally — not for show, but because I saw how quickly even small interventions can change what young people believe about themselves. I don’t pretend to have all the answers; often my plans fall through, or I realize mid-way I’m missing key skills. But what keeps me going is the belief that if you want to shift a system, you have to act with persistence, curiosity, and humility. 

At inVision U, I hope to learn how to turn local experiments into systemic solutions — to scale what works, to work with others who challenge me, and to bring practical change where it’s most needed. I’m not interested in a comfortable path, but one that matters. Спасибо за шанс объяснить, что я могу и хочу делать.
